The first layer alone covers wrong AMOEBA multipole frames, TIP4P OpenMP out-of-bounds per-atom energy writes, garbage Coulomb tables under RESPA, eDPD heat-flux corruption, restart-state gaps, region-contact bugs, and KOKKOS arithmetic/error-check regressions. The author states that the triage and code changes were generated with Claude Code using Claude Fable 5 under his direction, review, verification, and testing, with commit messages tying individual fixes to Coverity CIDs and reachable failure modes.

### [Three ITK image-processing bugs get fail-before/pass-after tests](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6576) — physwkim / ITK
ITK has three Claude Code `claude-fable-5` PRs that turn earlier analysis items into narrow C++ fixes with deterministic regressions: [resetting a per-pixel residual](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6576) in iterative inverse displacement fields, [propagating direction cosines](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6577) through kernel-spline subsampling, and [guarding empty re-estimation passes](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6578) in ConfidenceConnected filters. The write-ups include concrete failure mechanics such as raster-order-dependent inverse fields, 56 of 64 rotated subsample points falling outside the input buffer, and a NaN Mahalanobis path that floods all 256 pixels. Each PR reports local verification that the new test fails on unmodified `main` and passes with the fix, plus the relevant ITKDisplacementField and ITKRegionGrowing suites passing afterward.

### [DECtalk parity jumps from 213 to 348 byte-exact prompts](https://github.com/pktck/dectalk-python/pull/299) — pktck / dectalk-python
dectalk-python used Claude Fable 5 on a detailed audio-parity rollout for a 500-prompt FULL+VTM1 sample, raising byte-exact matches from 213 to 348 without regressions and count-exact matches from 352 to 354. The PR documents three found clusters: a VTM silence ramp-down gate that proved `OUT_PH` was audio-relevant, an active `set_tglst` variant needed for consonant-to-stressed-vowel glottal gestures, and a stress-counting mask that mistimed clause-final F0 hat falls when secondary stress followed the last primary. It also adds `scripts/diag_packet_diff.py` and a hard byte-identical test set so the packet-level diagnosis can be rerun instead of staying as prose.

### [SPARQ reconciles numeric fast paths and remeasures SP2Bench](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/1822) — jeswr / SPARQ
SPARQ’s Fable 5 agent fixed a numeric-semantics split where fast paths for caches, sargable filters, joins, and ordering could disagree with the datatype-aware `Num::of_literal` reference on padded or ill-formed XSD numeric literals. The follow-up [benchmark report](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/1827) reran the D3 SP2Bench complex-shape class on a quiet EC2 c6i.4xlarge with min-of-five runs over 250,000 triples and records the new verdict plainly: sparq wins 10 of 14 against same-box QLever, is correct on all 14, moves from “behind” to “mixed,” but still trails on q07, q09, and q11. The PRs are useful because the agent separates correctness wins from timing wins and disqualifies QLever’s q08/q12b timings for wrong row counts instead of counting them as speed victories.

### [ccxray fixes Fable’s misleading context-window gauge](https://github.com/lis186/ccxray/pull/212) — lis186 / ccxray
ccxray found a Fable-specific observability bug after a `claude-fable-5` session showed 17.3% context used in the dashboard while Claude Code’s own `/context` reported 172.7k of 200k tokens, or 86%. The root cause was a LiteLLM capability value — Fable’s 1M-token API ceiling — being treated as the active Claude Code session window for ordinary 200k sessions. The fix clamps dynamic Claude-model context data unless the real 1M beta signal or `[1m]` marker is present, adds restore-time self-healing for polluted index rows, and reports fail-before/pass-after tests plus a full suite moving from 1,083 to 1,088 passing tests after review fixes.

### [scimax_vscode audits every command and setting registration](https://github.com/jkitchin/scimax_vscode/pull/48) — jkitchin / scimax_vscode
scimax_vscode used Claude Fable 5 on a whole-repository correctness audit after an earlier command-registration mismatch. The PR fixes runtime calls to nonexistent commands for clearing results, citation navigation, Dired opening, and screenshot refresh; registers settings that were read but invisible in VS Code; wires previously ignored indexer, Dired, manuscript, and capture settings; and removes or deprecates dead configuration. The Fable-attributed commit stack adds a manifest-consistency guard plus focused tests for size-limit indexing, cite-key/path helpers, Zotero formatting, Dired sorting, compile-option resolution, journal refresh, and capture datetree behavior.

### [Three more ITK regressions get fail-before/pass-after tests](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6579) — physwkim / ITK
ITK has another Claude Code `claude-fable-5` image-processing batch, this time covering tied label voting, rank filters, and area morphology: [6579](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6579) skips voting-undecided pixels before they corrupt the next confusion-matrix row, [6580](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6580) forwards `FastApproximateRankImageFilter::SetRank()` to the last axis instead of silently using the median there, and [6581](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6581) fixes an off-by-one sort range that let the last raster pixel survive area opening. Each PR documents the exact unfixed output, then reports the new regression failing on unmodified `main` and passing after the fix, along with the relevant ITK module suites.

### [moq adds just-in-time transcoding and fetch-only HLS export](https://github.com/moq-dev/moq/pull/2140) — kixelated / moq
moq has two Claude Fable 5-written media-pipeline PRs on its `dev` branch. [2140](https://github.com/moq-dev/moq/pull/2140) adds an open-source `moq-transcode` crate that builds derivative hang broadcasts on demand: catalog rungs are filtered from the source, live subscriptions materialize only requested tracks, and a FETCH for group N transcodes just that group while preserving sequence numbers. [2141](https://github.com/moq-dev/moq/pull/2141) rewrites `moq-hls` so playlists come from catalog and timeline metadata while media bytes move only when a player requests a CMAF segment; the test plan lists full-workspace checks, rustdoc, unit tests around timeline windows and fMP4 muxing, and an in-process broadcast-to-playlist-to-segment end-to-end path, with live relay and hls.js validation still pending.

### [BrightBoost builds a local-only Dujiangyan waterworks game](https://github.com/Bright-Bots-Initiative/brightboost/pull/692) — BrightBoost-Tech / BrightBoost
BrightBoost used Claude Fable 5 to turn a reviewed prototype into “石犀工坊 · Waterworks,” an unlinked `/waterworks` K–8 sandbox where children build river systems inspired by Dujiangyan, run the water, storm-test with rain, and save creations locally. The PR deliberately keeps the showcase off the backend — no auth, no API, no database diff, `localStorage` only — and maps lead-review notes into tested behavior such as stable save-in-place IDs, autosave on navigation, duplicate-name guards, pattern-book examples, and unlock announcements. Verification reports 528 unit tests passing with 49 Waterworks tests, clean typecheck/lint/build, zero network requests on cold mount, storage-failure degradation, Chinese-language coverage, and founder manual QA over the full build-flow-save-reopen loop.

### [SPARQ turns an SP2Bench q09 profile into an opt-in incidence summary](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/1836) — jeswr / SPARQ
SPARQ’s Fable 5 agent profiled its SP2Bench q09 `SELECT DISTINCT ?p` path before optimizing it, finding that two branches examined about 251,000 rows to answer four predicates because most candidate blocks overlapped the anchor id window but contained no anchor members. The PR adds an opt-in `cs-anchor-incidence` feature that precomputes the exact set of predicates touching an anchor join position, so absent predicates can be rejected with an O(1) membership test while feature-off builds compile out the new path. Differential tests assert incidence-pruned answers match the exact scan and that pending-update overlays decline to the old path; the diagnostic benchmark on 250k triples reports q09 falling from about 4.62 ms to 3.39 ms per query while leaving canonical-host payoff as a separate follow-up.

### [gostow fixes six GNU Stow parity bugs from a Fable audit](https://github.com/rocne/gostow/pull/20) — rocne / gostow
A Claude Fable 5 audit of `gostow` found six divergences from GNU Stow 2.4.1, all in error paths or verbose non-happy paths that the existing differential suite missed. The maintainer says they reproduced every case against the real `stow` binary before changing code: missing conflict output at `-vv`, Go-vs-Perl errno wording, `.stowrc` directories being silently treated as empty, `canon_path` permission handling, bad `--ignore` parse behavior, and a disputed tilde-expansion bug kept as a known upstream issue. The PR adds twelve differential fixtures and five hermetic ones, and explicitly checks that each new fixture fails against a mutated implementation so the tests cannot pass vacuously.

### [Chidori turns runtime bottlenecks into measured benchmark targets](https://github.com/ThousandBirdsInc/chidori/pull/100) — kvey / Thousand Birds
Chidori’s latest Claude Fable 5 PR starts by adding the benchmark coverage that was missing from its durable-agent runtime: replay lookup, call-record cloning, session-store writes, run-store appends, per-run setup, plus JavaScript string-scan, typed-array, and mutual-recursion workloads. The first measurements show why the harness mattered — replay lookup grows from 89 µs at 100 records to 13.3 ms at 4,000, string scanning churns 20.16 MiB and 166k allocations for a 512-character string loop, and several workloads trail Node by 44x to 191x. Follow-up commits then use those benches to fix runtime-layer costs, string and typed-array paths, a RegExp `lastIndex` runaway, and recursion shapes, with CI comments adding cross-runtime benchmark tables and Test262 coverage at 45,448 of 48,070 executed tests passing.

### [go-trader adds three configurable risk gates with full test matrices](https://github.com/richkuo/go-trader/pull/1299) — richkuo / go-trader
go-trader has a three-PR Claude Fable 5 risk-control series covering [circuit-breaker cooldown and loss-streak overrides](https://github.com/richkuo/go-trader/pull/1299), [fail-open or fail-closed regime-gate policy](https://github.com/richkuo/go-trader/pull/1300), and [direction-aware same-direction exposure caps](https://github.com/richkuo/go-trader/pull/1301). The changes make previously hardcoded trading controls configurable per strategy while preserving default behavior, keeping closes and position-management paths ungated, and surfacing effective settings in startup, inspect, status, dashboards, and owner DMs. Each PR includes a detailed test matrix — Go suites for validation, hot reload, gates, exposure aggregation, alerting, and classifier parity, plus 2,410 Python tests on the regime-gate branch — and the Claude review jobs report local builds and focused suites passing.

### [Ubuntu autoinstall reruns get compile-time wipe guardrails](https://github.com/falkcorp/ubuntu-autoinstall-agent/pull/45) — jdfalk / ubuntu-autoinstall-agent
A run of Claude Fable 5 PRs moved `ubuntu-autoinstall-agent` toward safer reruns and operations visibility: [metadata-only placed-config inventory](https://github.com/falkcorp/ubuntu-autoinstall-agent/pull/41), [Phase-5-only in-target reconfiguration](https://github.com/falkcorp/ubuntu-autoinstall-agent/pull/42), [UEFI boot-order setting after `update-grub`](https://github.com/falkcorp/ubuntu-autoinstall-agent/pull/43), a [zero-JavaScript dashboard](https://github.com/falkcorp/ubuntu-autoinstall-agent/pull/44), [selective `--phases` / `--from-phase` runs](https://github.com/falkcorp/ubuntu-autoinstall-agent/pull/45), and [non-destructive mounting of an existing target](https://github.com/falkcorp/ubuntu-autoinstall-agent/pull/47). The safety design is concrete: wipe-capable disk operations require a `WipeAuthorization` token that can only exist when Phase 2 is selected, while reruns that skip disk prep reopen md, LUKS, ZFS, `/`, `/boot`, and ESP in a load-bearing order. The PR descriptions say flagless installs stay byte-identical and anti-over-suppression tests prove the default full run still wipes and installs as before.

### [A single-file SVG animation editor with timeline and SMIL export](https://github.com/simonw/tools/pull/292) — Simon Willison
Simon Willison’s `tools` repo has a Claude Fable 5 co-authored single-file SVG animation studio. The PR adds drawing for rectangles, ellipses, lines, polygons, text, and bezier pen paths, then animates them on a multi-track timeline with auto-keyed tweens, path morphing, per-keyframe cubic-bezier easing, onion skinning, layers, undo and redo, localStorage autosave, SVG import, JSON project save/load, and self-contained SMIL export. It is an open PR rather than a merged release, but the scope is a complete browser tool rather than a small demo fragment.

### [Qiyas builds an Arabic health tracker in thirteen Fable commits](https://github.com/U4E-glitch/graphify/pull/1) — U4E-glitch / graphify
graphify’s first PR is a 7,733-line Claude Fable 5 co-authored Android app called Qiyas, built as a staged sequence rather than one opaque dump. The commits scaffold a Gradle/Hilt/Room/DataStore app, add Appendix-backed glucose and blood-pressure classification, entry/history/dashboard/report/export flows, Arabic localization, CSV and PDF export, scheduled notifications, release signing, and a BiDi fix for numeric fragments in Arabic reports. The same branch adds Robolectric end-to-end tests over real database save/edit/delete flows, emergency alerts, mmol canonical storage, report generation, CSV/PDF content, and Compose entry screens.

### [sitespeed.io turns raw report tables into action-oriented diagnostics](https://github.com/sitespeedio/sitespeed.io/pull/4807) — soulgalore / sitespeed.io
sitespeed.io has a four-PR Claude Fable 5 pass over its HTML reports: the [summary page](https://github.com/sitespeedio/sitespeed.io/pull/4807) now leads with an overall grade, vital thresholds, worst-page ordering, and degradation fallbacks; the [Domains page](https://github.com/sitespeedio/sitespeed.io/pull/4808) calls out the slowest, heaviest, and chattiest domains; the [Toplist page](https://github.com/sitespeedio/sitespeed.io/pull/4809) adds cache and problem-severity signals; and the [Assets page](https://github.com/sitespeedio/sitespeed.io/pull/4811) sorts heaviest-first with transfer-size and cache-lifetime tones. The write-ups are concrete about preserving self-contained reports, hiding empty sections instead of crashing, and using medians rather than noisy max timings for verdicts.

### [livecodata moves imperative DOM views into humble SolidJS components](https://github.com/ulyssesdotcodes/livecodata/pull/55) — ulyssesdotcodes / livecodata
livecodata used Claude Fable 5 to split hand-built DOM views from non-view logic without changing the public wiring. Playback becomes a pure engine emitting view state, table-panel keeps formatting and chart resolution while Solid renders the panel, editor-support retains completion and hover behavior behind injected DOM factories, and preview canvas drawing stays separate from the tooltip UI. The PR says all CSS classes, element IDs, and public APIs were preserved, 212 tests passed, and both solo and multiplayer flows were checked in a real browser against the previous build.

### [algo-fft turns a thousand asmdecl findings into SIMD-by-default cleanup](https://github.com/CWBudde/algo-fft/pull/10) — MeKo-Christian / algo-fft
algo-fft’s Claude Fable 5 session started with roughly 1,000 `go vet -tags asm` findings and grew into a full assembly/SIMD cleanup. The commits fix 984 slice frame-pointer names, 25 wrong TEXT frame sizes, missing arm64 NEON declarations, ship SIMD kernels in the default build with `purego` as the opt-out, repair a 386 radix-16 in-place transpose corruption, and take golangci-lint from thousands of findings to zero after exposing formerly asm-gated files. Verification spans default and purego builds, vet on amd64/arm64/386 and wasm, full amd64 test suites including race variants, and native 386 package runs.

### [A Guild Wars 2 Discord bot gets persistent event scheduling and review-driven fixes](https://github.com/ArmonicPvP/gw2bot/pull/25) — ArmonicPvP / gw2bot
gw2bot’s Claude Fable 5 PR adds a full `/event new` system for Raid, Strike, Fractal, and World vs. World events in Discord. Event creators get a three-step modal flow, private preview, channel posting, persistent signup/signout/settings buttons, capacity-aware roles with waitlisting and promotion, remembered role preferences, recurring events, thread/status transitions, and SQLite persistence with timezone handling. Multiple later commits are explicitly review-driven: failed recurrence posting becomes retryable, manual post failures clean up partial state, over-event signouts and in-progress signup completions are blocked, and failed status refreshes no longer silently mark old occurrences over.

### [Eneru turns a no-go ship review into a full release-hardening pass](https://github.com/m4r1k/Eneru/pull/93) — Federico Iezzi / Eneru
Eneru’s v6.1.7 PR comes from a parallel `/ship` review run with Claude Fable 5 across code review, security, and test coverage, which initially returned a NO-GO on three config-loader criticals. The remediation adds a declarative config schema gate so scalar YAML cannot arm shutdown paths accidentally, makes notification and poweroff failures observable, validates Host headers against DNS rebinding, redacts credentials, bounds API-server concurrency, caches dashboard energy scans, and hardens release promotion. The 77-file branch also includes production bug fixes found during a real power outage on 6.1.6, with Codecov reporting 97.93% project coverage.

### [QuantEcon.py fixes undefined Markov-chain sample paths](https://github.com/QuantEcon/QuantEcon.py/pull/860) — oyamad / QuantEcon
QuantEcon.py has a Claude Fable 5 co-authored fix for three `markov/core.py` bugs found in review. Rows whose probabilities summed slightly below 1 could make `searchsorted` return state `n`, after which Numba code read out-of-bounds CDF rows; the PR body reports one two-state example where 801,746 of 1,000,000 steps became invalid, and sparse simulation could emit states as large as 936,243,400. The branch clamps overflow draws to the last positive-probability state, normalizes negative initial states before entering the sparse kernel, fixes `__str__`, and adds fail-before/pass-after tests for deficient row sums, sparse negative init, deficient initial distributions, and review-caught zero-probability fallback cases.

### [DECtalk runs a 133,641-prompt WAV parity burn-down](https://github.com/pktck/dectalk-python/pull/312) — pktck / dectalk-python
dectalk-python’s latest Claude Fable 5 session moves from phoneme parity into full WAV output. The PR adds `scripts/corpus_wav_sweep.py`, a crash-resuming byte-comparison runner over all 133,641 corpus prompts, then fixes the first residual cluster at prompt 2,506, “would you like to,” by porting a missing `ph_sort.c` rule that unreduces clause-final “for/to/into” vowels. It also ports an active dangling-stress cleanup block, flips `DECTALK_DISABLE_CAPI=1` to use the FULL+VTM1 pipeline by default for US English, and reports lint, matrix tests, shellcheck, C-oracle tests, and performance benchmarks passing in CI.

### [Lexical compiles keyboard shortcuts into constant-time dispatch](https://github.com/etrepum/lexical/pull/18) — Bob Ippolito / lexical
Lexical’s Fable co-authored PR replaces its O(n) keyboard-shortcut predicate chain with declarative shortcut tables compiled to modifier-bitmask-plus-key maps. The branch moves the same dispatcher into both `@lexical/extension` and the editor core, so application extensions can overlay, remap, disable, or add shortcuts while the built-in keydown handler also stops walking roughly 28 branches per event. The important verification is an exhaustive parity test: 25 key/code pairs × 16 modifier states on Apple and non-Apple platforms, including non-Latin layout code fallbacks, must dispatch the same commands, payloads, and `preventDefault` behavior as the old path; later Fable commits fixed the CI build after Babel parsed generic TypeScript arrows as JSX.

### [Empty safetensors shards stop dropping the rest of a streamed model](https://github.com/run-ai/runai-model-streamer/pull/158) — janbernloehr / Run:ai
runai-model-streamer has a Fable-assisted fix for a small iterator bug with large consequences: `FilesRequestsIterator.next_request()` treated an empty `next_chunks()` result as end-of-stream even when it came from a valid zero-tensor safetensors shard. In vLLM’s `--load-format runai_streamer` path, fixed-shard-count checkpoints with 40-byte empty shards could silently serve with 70–100% of weights left at initialization values, depending on directory order. The fix distinguishes an empty finished file from a file blocked by the request buffer budget, skips the former, preserves request splitting for the latter, keeps all 18 existing tests passing, and adds regressions for empty files at the head and between data files.

### [SolidJS stops leaking internal wrappers for nullish rejections](https://github.com/solidjs/solid/pull/2867) — yumemi-thomas / SolidJS
SolidJS has a Fable-assisted fix for a narrow but user-visible async error-surface bug. `Promise.reject()`, `reject(null)`, or synchronous `throw undefined` in tracked scopes reached `<Errored>`, `createErrorBoundary`, effect error arms, and `console.error` as Solid’s internal `StatusError` wrapper instead of the rejected value, because the unwrap path used `cause ?? wrapper` and nullish causes triggered the fallback. The PR adds a single `unwrapStatusError()` helper, routes both user-facing paths through it, and the author says the new tests were written first, confirmed failing on the unfixed code, verified against the published beta.16 package, and then run through the full solid-signals, solid-js, and `@solidjs/web` suites.

### [A Fable-chaired review hardens an AI PR-review workflow](https://github.com/Atom-oh/security-ops/pull/9) — Atom-oh
Atom-oh’s `security-ops` PR-review pipeline carried forward findings from a separate 19-round review cycle chaired by Claude Fable 5. The patch makes model cells exit-status-aware, changes a severe-coverage gate from model count to vendor axis, isolates Codex from the runner environment and `GH_TOKEN`, scrubs candidate credentials before any model sees the diff, fixes PEM-redaction edge cases, and prevents a malicious PR title of `PROMPT_EOF` from truncating the chair prompt. The test plan includes live smokes for AWS-key and PEM redaction, vendor-gate failure, prompt-fence survival, and environment stripping.

### [Mitos removes two Kubernetes writes from its warm sandbox claim path](https://github.com/mitos-run/mitos/pull/879) — stubbi / Mitos
Mitos used Claude Fable 5 with extended thinking to look past its already-fast 60 ms P50 Firecracker warm activation and measure the control-plane work around it. New claim-stage instrumentation showed 79–96 ms of stable overhead plus a roughly 300 ms cold spike from lazy mTLS resolution, so the PR pre-warms TLS config, keeps the short-lived Restoring phase in memory instead of writing it to Kubernetes, and overlaps the token Secret write with the activate RPC. The author reports 51 ms off the mean warm claim and a 296 ms cold spike removed, then accepted review feedback by splitting claim metrics from fork-stage metrics and joining the Secret write correctly on failed activations.

### [SolidJS fixes two async-iterator states that could stay pending forever](https://github.com/solidjs/solid/pull/2870) — yumemi-thomas / SolidJS
SolidJS has another Claude Fable 5-assisted runtime fix, this time for async iterable memo bodies in the 2.0 beta. An iterator that completed asynchronously without yielding and a thenable whose `next()` rejected synchronously could both leave the node `STATUS_PENDING` forever, so `<Loading>` never cleared and `<Errored>` never rendered. The PR links public StackBlitz repros, mirrors the promise-branch settlement logic, says the red-first tests were confirmed failing on the published `2.0.0-beta.16` package, and reports the full solid-signals, solid-js, and `@solidjs/web` suites passing.

### [observ pins its no-reference-cycle promise with GC-disabled tests](https://github.com/fork-tongue/observ/pull/189) — Korijn / observ
observ’s Claude Fable 5 co-authored PR turns a documented memory-management promise into executable tests: the library says proxies, dependencies, key dependencies, watchers, and computed watchers should be reclaimed by plain reference counting rather than relying on Python’s cyclic garbage collector. The new module disables the GC, checks weakrefs and proxy registry entries as objects drop, and targets cycle-prone paths such as callback-argument probing through exception tracebacks and exceptions thrown by watched functions. A final sweep asserts that `gc.collect()` has nothing left to reclaim after exercising most of the reactivity machinery.

### [rule-trace turns a twelve-perspective design review into follow-up specs and fixes](https://github.com/seanleecoder/rule-trace/pull/5) — seanleecoder / rule-trace
rule-trace merged a Claude Fable 5 co-authored repository review that goes beyond a static report: it reviews the project from 12 perspectives, assigns severity, effort, impact, file references, a top-10 executive summary, a two-year risk assessment, and a 30-day action plan. The same PR then adds phased, agent-ready implementation specs and carries some of the review into code and docs, including phase-one metrics, dashboard/report artifacts, release notes, CI wiring, and later review-driven corrections. It is a useful example of Fable doing research/design work over a whole repo and then leaving structured follow-up tasks rather than only prose.

### [riverpod_devtools repairs Event Log bugs, then adds filtering and search](https://github.com/yutsuki3/riverpod_devtools/pull/48) — yutsuki3 / riverpod_devtools
riverpod_devtools has a two-PR Claude Fable 5 sequence around its extension Event Log. The merged [bug-fix PR](https://github.com/yutsuki3/riverpod_devtools/pull/48) restores AsyncData/AsyncLoading/AsyncError badge detection, wires the previously no-op Clear All button, prevents active providers from being evicted as disposed, makes event IDs unique within a millisecond, widens `vm_service` compatibility, and removes leftover development commentary. The follow-up [feature PR](https://github.com/yutsuki3/riverpod_devtools/pull/49) adds event-type toggle filters, full-text search over provider names and values, and an empty state for filter misses, while explicitly leaving the prebuilt extension bundle to the release script.

### [A wavetable synth grows growl-bass warps, filters, and presets](https://github.com/SuzukiDaishi/z-audio-dsp-plugin/pull/6) — SuzukiDaishi / z-audio-dsp-plugin
z-audio-dsp-plugin has a Claude Fable 5 co-authored expansion of its wavetable instrument from four to nine tables, then onward to a 32-table factory library and 60 categorized presets. The first layer adds vowel, growl, FM, sync, and grit tables through the existing mip pipeline; Serum-style bend, sync, mirror, squeeze, quantize, FM/RM/AM warps; notch, comb, and formant filters; and a master distortion stage with new modulation destinations. The follow-up rewrites factory-table generation through inverse FFT synthesis, cutting a 32-table build from about 2.0 seconds to about 290 ms, while tests keep the additive renderer as the oracle and cover alias suppression, compatibility, preset IDs, and new DSP behavior.

### [QuantEcon.jl adds sparse random Markov-chain generation without changing dense draws](https://github.com/QuantEcon/QuantEcon.jl/pull/394) — oyamad / QuantEcon
QuantEcon.jl used Claude Fable 5 on a small API extension with a precise compatibility constraint: `random_stochastic_matrix` and `random_markov_chain` can now return sparse `SparseMatrixCSC` output without materializing a dense matrix first. The implementation uses a `Val(true)` keyword so the methods stay type-stable, and it shares the nonzero-position sampler so a fixed RNG produces the identical matrix in dense and sparse formats. Tests assert `@inferred` behavior, fixed-seed dense/sparse equality, row-sum tolerances, and irreducibility of the generated sparse chain, while a second commit renames private benchmark generators that shadowed public exports.

### [Freelens builds a self-contained extension API declaration bundle](https://github.com/freelensapp/freelens/pull/2120) — dex4er / Freelens
Freelens used `claude-fable-5` for the “fiddliest deliverable” in its v2 extension plan: making `@freelensapp/extensions` publishable with a single bundled declaration file and a tiny ESM runtime shim. Declaration emit first surfaced a real import bug in the `ws` types and 14 `TS2742` annotation gaps where inferred injectable types expanded into unnameable internals; those were fixed before the rollup. The PR says the generated whole-graph TypeScript declaration build and `rollup-plugin-dts` pass run clean, and that a strict scratch consumer type-checks against the bundled `extension-api.d.ts`, while noting that a full build/test rerun and example-extension port are still pending.

### [Airflow keeps task logs flowing after runtime socket reconnects](https://github.com/apache/airflow/pull/69696) — guan404ming / Apache Airflow
Apache Airflow has a Fable 5-generated fix for a task-runtime logging failure mode: once a task dropped its log socket, it could not reconnect, so logs buffered during the outage were silently lost. The patch adds a non-blocking reaccepter to the logs server, verifies that a reconnecting peer belongs to the child process tree, and tolerates transient socket errors without stalling the supervisor loop. The accompanying tests cover reconnection forwarding, rejection paths, and spurious wakeups, and the PR discloses Claude Code with Fable 5 under Airflow’s generative-AI contribution guidelines.

### [go-trader adds fixed-fractional perps sizing with fail-closed stops](https://github.com/richkuo/go-trader/pull/1303) — richkuo / go-trader
go-trader’s latest Claude Fable 5 PR adds opt-in `risk_per_trade_pct` sizing for Hyperliquid perpetuals, where position size is derived from cash, risk percentage, and resolved stop distance instead of a notional fallback. The branch deliberately fails closed when stop distance cannot be resolved, makes risk mode mutually exclusive with older leverage and margin settings, preserves byte-identical behavior for existing configs, and blocks hot-reload mode switches while a position is open. The test surface spans Go scheduler/risk sizing/config reload paths, backtester parity under `--config`, JSON init output, and Python backtest tests for ATR-derived skips and loud rejects.

### [noyalib raises coverage by deleting dead serde paths and testing numeric ordering](https://github.com/sebastienrousseau/noyalib/pull/172) — sebastienrousseau / noyalib
noyalib used Claude Fable 5 in a coverage campaign that moved all-feature line coverage from 93.11% to 94.74% without adding shallow tests around unreachable code. The first chunk removes about 110 lines of orphaned tag-preserving deserializer code whose only entry point had become a recursive dead loop after v0.0.14 changed the public loaders. The second chunk adds semantic tests for `Number::cmp` cross-type ordering across integer, unsigned, float, negative, positive, and NaN values, with each chunk checked against the coverage tool before landing and clippy follow-ups fixing new Rust 1.95 lints.

### [Colmi ring sync stops losing one device’s heart-rate history](https://github.com/tahnok/colmi_r02_client/pull/61) — tahnok
A Claude Fable 5 codebase review of `colmi_r02_client` turned into nine targeted fixes for a BLE smart-ring client. The highest-impact one scopes full-sync record lookups to the current ring, so syncing two rings into one database no longer silently drops one ring’s heart-rate rows or overwrites its sport details. The same PR fixes unhandled raw replies, wall-clock-bounds real-time polling that could otherwise loop forever on zero-value streams, parser-state leakage after aborted multi-packet reads, UTC handling in CLI date arguments, scan filtering, empty pretty-print output, and range validation, with new tests across client, database, heart-rate, and steps modules.

### [unladen fixes dependency-scanner misses and speeds a source walk](https://github.com/miketheman/unladen/pull/56) — miketheman
unladen’s Fable co-authored PR fixes several ways a Python dependency-usage scanner could misread real projects: requirements lines with inline comments were skipped when the comment contained a URL, `-r base.txt # comment` includes were not followed, dotted `.egg-info` / `.dist-info` names such as `zope.interface` failed to resolve, malformed config files surfaced as tracebacks, and module-level `try` / `if` definitions lost call edges. The branch also indexes metadata directories once per resolve pass and later switches the source-file walker to class pre-filtered `isinstance` dispatch. CodSpeed’s report on the PR says one benchmark improved by 16.99%, while seven others were unchanged.

### [pnpm pacquet restores Bit workspace installs after 160 failing e2es](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/pull/12899) — zkochan / pnpm
pnpm’s pacquet implementation used Claude Fable 5 on six v11-parity regressions surfaced by Bit’s end-to-end suite after Bit moved to `@pnpm/napi` 12.0.0-alpha.5. The fixes restore peered snapshots and non-root importer subtrees in the hoisted graph, link root-component members without on-disk manifests, populate `.modules.yaml` `injectedDeps`, accept declared out-of-root capsule importers, materialize excluded `link:` deps, and treat an empty `bareSpecifier` like no range. Verification includes new Rust unit tests, `cargo test --workspace` and clippy for pacquet, plus Bit e2e runs that brought `app root components` to 38/38, `custom-env` to 26/26, and `bit new react` back to working end-to-end.

### [Slicer-Liver makes slicing-plane handles camera-aware and draggable](https://github.com/ALive-research/Slicer-Liver/pull/582) — RafaelPalomar / ALive research
Slicer-Liver’s third resection-initialization slice, drafted with Claude Fable 5 assistance, turns an invisible planning step into a visible interaction. When a target mesh is attached, entering Init + SlicingPlane now seeds two handles across the target bounds along the camera’s right vector, derives the bisector plane live as either handle is dragged on the camera focal plane, and declines outside Init so the Planning drag pipeline remains untouched. The author notes a live-discovered observer re-entrancy issue during seeding, adds tests for seed placement, idempotence, full press/move/release gestures, and Planning-decline behavior, and reports 303 launched-sweep tests plus 95 bare-harness tests passing.

### [QuantEcon.jl caches Markov-chain simulation CDFs for a 258x dense benchmark speedup](https://github.com/QuantEcon/QuantEcon.jl/pull/395) — oyamad / QuantEcon
QuantEcon.jl followed its earlier Fable-assisted sparse generator work with a Claude Fable 5 performance pass over `MarkovChain.simulate`. The PR lazily caches transition CDFs on the chain, adds a sparse sampler that avoids densifying the transition matrix, guards rounding overflow the same way QuantEcon.py now does, and short-circuits recurrent-class detection for strictly positive dense matrices. The benchmark table reports `simulate/dense_n1000_ts100` dropping from 928µs to 3.6µs, sparse `n1000_k4_ts10000` from 1.83ms to 138µs, and dense `stationary_distributions/n200` from 1.81ms to 844µs, with tests for cache invalidation, dense/sparse same-seed equality, overflow fallback, Bool chains, and `@inferred` simulation paths.

### [A zk-circuit optimizer trades 23% more runtime for fewer bus interactions](https://github.com/powdr-labs/apc-optimizer/pull/87) — georgwiese / powdr-labs
powdr-labs has a Claude Fable 5 co-authored optimizer patch that relaxes when its `busPairCancel` pass can drop send pairs: an un-cancelable head pair no longer blocks the rest of the same-address chain if a non-send blocker preserves admissibility. The same branch justifies compound byte-slot expressions by enumerating finite domains, indexes candidate search by address group, and stages steal-safe drops before order-dependent ones. The PR reports exact circuit-size effects on OpenVM Ethereum cases — for example `apc_010` bus interactions move 314→274 in the latest benchmark comment — while also showing the cost: aggregate bus-interaction effectiveness improves from 2.868× to 2.983×, but optimizer runtime rises from 38.0s to 46.6s on the 10-case quick bench.

### [RoSys checks robot startup-code drift without writing flash](https://github.com/zauberzeug/rosys/pull/432) — jsb-zz / zauberzeug
RoSys used Claude Fable 5 on a field-robot operations fix: when a Lizard ESP connects, the host now compares the startup code it expects with the code stored on the microcontroller and warns if they differ, catching pin or module-parameter changes that the old field-count check missed. The PR also fixes a premature `ESP_CONNECTED` event after `restart()`, resets stale core checksums before reads, exposes a tri-state `checksums_match` property, and adds a `CommunicationSimulation` test for matching, mismatching, and configure/restart/reconnect flows. A follow-up dispatched-agent report checked the design against the RoSys and Lizard source, concluding the check reads an in-memory startup string, writes nothing to flash, and avoids having to persist a separate expected nonce because the checksum is content-derived.

### [scad-pub burns down dozens of browser-edge failure modes](https://github.com/mfbernardes/scad-pub/pull/91) — mfbernardes / scad-pub
scad-pub has a large Claude Fable 5 co-authored hardening pass that starts with a FileInput bug — picking the same file twice did not fire `change`, and async read failures surfaced as unhandled rejections — and then spans 61 files of UI, service-worker, schema, OpenSCAD, WebGL, storage, and accessibility fixes. The commit list includes controlled blank font selects, corrupted preset storage tolerance, strict numeric preset validation, build failure on missing configured fonts, non-ok navigation responses kept out of the offline shell, WebGL resource release on unmount, object-URL revocation deferred until after download clicks, duplicate design-id rejection, and a default workflow-token permissions restriction. The PR is open with a successful Cloudflare Workers preview, making it a broad reliability sweep rather than a single isolated bug fix.

### [Papyri tightens pack-time documentation linting after a Fable sweep](https://github.com/Carreau/papyri/pull/385) — Carreau / papyri
Papyri's Claude Fable 5 co-authored sweep closes several gaps between what its `lint_bundle` command could detect and what `pack` enforced. The PR makes DocstringSentinel placeholders visible to linting, fails pack on substitution nodes that should never reach the IR, warns or fails strictly on missing figure assets and sentinel docstrings, and then keeps going through Sphinx-fidelity fixes: `!` reference suppression, `foo()` lookup semantics, free-form numpydoc sections, plot directives, `:doc:` refs, default-role diagnostics, project-local roles, and import-solver cases for objects that cannot be named by `full_qual`. The final commit notes four edge cases found by adversarial review of the sweep, so the branch documents both the initial lint gap and the review-driven cleanup around it.

### [A topology analyzer fixes its own missing data-flow edges](https://github.com/M0nkeyFl0wer/agentic-coding-topology/pull/1) — M0nkeyFl0wer
agentic-coding-topology opened with a Claude Fable 5 co-authored rewrite after the tool's own analyzer exposed that v0.1 was barely connecting data-flow: running it on itself produced 144 nodes, 23 edges, and 121 components because ordinary call arguments like `y = f(x)` produced no `x → y` edge. The new normalizer records dependencies from call arguments, kwargs, attribute bases, comparisons, boolean operands, control-flow tests, iterables, contexts, try bodies, comprehensions, unpacking, and aug-assigns, then labels operations so clone detection requires both category-matched graph isomorphism and a consistent operation-label renaming. The PR says thresholds were recalibrated to zero errors on micrograd, nanoGPT, and minGPT, and its tests cover real edges, control-flow capture, renamed-clone detection, shape-only non-errors, cycle suppression, and a blocking self-check regression.

### [Guarded note-lineage adoption preserves ordinary note bytes](https://github.com/3mdistal/bwrb/pull/817) — 3mdistal / bwrb
bwrb used Claude Fable 5 planning and review logs for a dry-run-first `bwrb lineage adopt <child> --from <parent>` command that can attach an existing note to a parent without rewriting the note body. The PR is explicit about the safety contract: exact ID/path/name targeting only, same-type checks, no bulk or force mode, cycle and dangling-lineage rejection, shared locks, and rollback if the two-note registry write fails. Verification includes Node 22 full CI with 2,988 tests passing, docs lint/doctor/build, and a disposable-vault exercise showing only the intended IDs and `forked-from` changed while body hashes stayed identical.

### [Sheaf restarts stop orphaning live SSE subscribers](https://github.com/paulbellamy/sheaf/pull/27) — paulbellamy / sheaf
Sheaf’s Fable 5 co-authored fix traces a missed-event bug to Fastify shutdown semantics: the `/api/ui/drafts/stream` route hijacked replies, so `app.close()` could wait forever on invisible SSE sockets while a restarted Obsidian plugin served mutations from a new backend. The patch tracks each stream cleanup and runs it from `preClose`, so clients see EOF and reconnect to the restarted server instead of silently watching the old instance. A follow-up makes the stream resumable with event IDs and `Last-Event-ID`, and the regression test requires `close()` to settle with a live SSE client attached.

### [graphRAG shares query-playground paths with its MCP runner](https://github.com/CLYEH/graphRAG/pull/60) — CLYEH / graphRAG
graphRAG’s Fable 5 PR adds `/query/semantic`, `/query/sql`, and `/query/global` REST playground endpoints without creating a second query engine. The new public seam routes through the same bounded MCP machinery, deadline envelope, project binding, and degradation behavior used by the agent tools, while unsupported filters and options are rejected loudly until the modes can honor them. Tests pin `top_k` propagation, missing-model 503s, and an end-to-end pure-Postgres global query with grounded `source_refs`, so the playground cannot drift from the MCP surface unnoticed.

### [libratbag-rs hardens async mouse-driver protocol paths](https://github.com/niltonperimneto/libratbag-rs/pull/3) — niltonperimneto / libratbag-rs
libratbag-rs used Claude Fable 5 on an ASUS driver hardening pass aimed at daemon integration failures rather than style cleanup. `commit()` now leaves hardware on the profile marked active in `DeviceInfo`, tracks selected slots through dirty saves, bounds each query read with 500 ms timeouts and three retries, and distinguishes a sleeping wireless mouse from a dead hidraw node so DBus registration and later recovery behave sensibly. The PR adds a scripted mock `DeviceIo` backend and ten protocol-level tests that assert exact wire bytes, profile-switch ordering, sleep handling, and recovery paths for quirks such as DOUBLE_DPI and separate XY DPI.

### [FluffOS exposes compile-time hooks for inherits and includes](https://github.com/fluffos/fluffos/pull/1230) — thefallentree / FluffOS
FluffOS has a Fable 5 co-authored compiler feature that lets the master object intercept every LPC `inherit` and `#include` while code is compiling. The new `inherit_program` and `include_file` applies can translate paths, provide in-memory source, or block the operation, following existing override/include-path precedents without changing stock behavior when the hooks are absent. The testsuite uses the dependency graph exposed by those hooks to demonstrate automatic hot reload: changed own sources, includes, inherited programs, and transitive parent includes cause the right blueprints to destruct and reload.

### [Reactant traces Static.jl numbers without wrapping them as traced primitives](https://github.com/EnzymeAD/Reactant.jl/pull/3049) — oschulz / Reactant.jl
Reactant.jl found a compatibility bug while making MeasureBase.jl work with the tracer: expressions such as `x + static(0.0)` tried to construct `TracedRNumber{StaticFloat64{0.0}}` and failed because Static.jl numeric types are `Number` subtypes but not Reactant primitives. The Fable-generated patch adds a `ReactantStaticExt` extension that maps static booleans, integers, and floats to their dynamic counterparts through `unwrapped_eltype`, promotion rules, and a `dynamic(x)` conversion path. The PR adds integration tests for Static.jl traced arithmetic and keeps the change independent from the neighboring MeasureBase work.

### [instantmed repairs a daily-cap check that only worked in memory](https://github.com/reabal-n/instantmed/pull/314) — reabal-n / instantmed
instantmed used Claude Fable 5 on a small but consequential auto-approval bug: the daily-cap read path passed a pseudo-user string into a UUID-backed audit-log counter, so every database query failed and the system fell back to a per-process in-memory cap. The fix makes reads use the same system actor as writes, segregates E2E burst and daily buckets from production, and adds a source-level regression pin so the pseudo-user string cannot return. The PR reports the auto-approval, deterministic-routing, and unit suites passing, plus the full 4,099-test suite, typecheck, lint, and an E2E run that exercises the repaired cap.

### [PyVista avoids a Wayland GL probe crash reproduced in a Qt app](https://github.com/pyvista/pyvista/pull/8817) — Eric Larson / PyVista
Eric Larson says he coded this PyVista fix with Claude Fable 5 after hitting a Wayland crash while working on PyVistaQt. The repro is concrete: if a process has already instantiated and rendered with `vtkEGLRenderWindow`, calling `check_depth_peeling()` on main aborts with an X/GLX `BadAccess`, while the PR returns `True`. The patch changes the GL capability probes so an EGL-using Qt process does not force VTK's GLX render-window factory, and Larson explicitly says he understands and vouches for the code.

### [BorgBackup uses Fable review to refine damaged-repository compaction](https://github.com/borgbackup/borg/pull/9877) — Mrityunjay Raj / BorgBackup
BorgBackup's compaction fix for unindexed pack bytes turned into a useful public example of Fable as a reviewer rather than just a code generator. Thomas Waldmann posted multiple Claude Fable 5 reviews that accepted the basic file-size accounting idea, then found real edge cases: a lost EOF cross-check, a damaged-repo `undelete` gap, and later an overclaim that superseded duplicate waste was reclaimable. The PR now includes tests for gaps, overlaps, stale entries, oversized entries, damaged repos, and undelete/extract behavior, with the remaining trade-off called out in review instead of hidden.

### [code-world-models grows a continuous hybrid-control experiment suite](https://github.com/JaviMaligno/code-world-models/pull/18) — JaviMaligno / code-world-models
A long Claude Fable 5 co-authored `code-world-models` PR expands a theory paper into a follow-up continuous/hybrid experiment program. The early body adds a continuous-settings remark and a design spec for a cart-with-wall rare-mode instrument; later Fable-attributed commits add the mechanism harness, measured axis separation, repair analysis, mitigation experiments, epsilon-sensitivity sweeps, and corrections to an overclaim exposed by those sweeps. The branch is large — 68 files and more than 12,000 added lines — but the interesting part is the research loop: experiment design, generated artifacts, measured negative/positive results, and prose updates all live in the same public trail.

### [Blackout turns a competitor-depth audit into analytics and payout fixes](https://github.com/Blackmarket-coa/blackout/pull/841) — Blackmarket-coa / Blackout
Blackout used Claude Fable 5 to verify a July 2026 competitor-depth analysis against the repository before building from it. The first commit confirms analytics as the top gap but corrects three factual errors — commerce is FBM rather than MedusaJS/MercurJS, there is no hawala ledger in code, and feed ranking had already shipped — then the same PR adds an OSS gap-fill plan, analytics ingest, a creator insights slice, feed diversity caps, payout transparency, and a deterministic same-millisecond tip ordering fix. It is a useful case of Fable doing repo-grounded product analysis and then carrying part of that analysis into implementation across 30 files.

### [bwrb makes body search ignore YAML frontmatter across every caller](https://github.com/3mdistal/bwrb/pull/818) — 3mdistal / bwrb
bwrb's second Claude Fable 5-backed note-safety PR in this series fixes a shared search boundary rather than one command. `searchContent` had been feeding whole Markdown files to ripgrep or the Node fallback, so a value appearing only in YAML frontmatter could select notes through list, bulk, audit, delete, edit, recent, dashboards, and compatibility search. The fix preserves original line numbers while rebuilding context strictly inside the Markdown body, keeps ripgrep and fallback behavior aligned, and reports a broad gate: build, pack verification, typecheck, lint, knip, 2,979 non-PTY tests, 176 PTY tests, schema and docs checks, 235 focused matrix tests, 23 issue regressions, plus a disposable-vault CLI exercise.

### [Nextra fixes a dozen stream-sharing UI defects from an audit file](https://github.com/ServesYouRice/nextra/pull/5) — ServesYouRice / nextra
nextra used Claude Fable 5 to clear UI issues U-1 through U-11 from an audit note, then added a follow-up progressive-disclosure fix. The merged PR replaces a raw escaped ellipsis in the OBS status pill, blocks double-submit room joins, adds a live streaming settings panel, warns before stopping a room with viewers connected, traps focus inside modals, and makes watch links fit 320–400px phones without horizontal scroll. The author reports Playwright coverage for the join flow, URL-paste extraction, focus trap, stop-sharing confirmation, live settings panel, inert toggling, and mobile scroll checks.

### [A Godot web game cuts draw calls and drops stale multiplayer frames](https://github.com/ysetbon/elemental-rescue/pull/5) — ysetbon / elemental-rescue
elemental-rescue used Claude Fable 5 on a web-performance pass for a 400×400 castle map where the host browser’s frame rate affects every online guest. The PR distance-culls hundreds of decorative MeshInstances while leaving landmarks and colliders untouched, adds an adaptive render-scale loop that steps from 1.0 down to 0.6 after sustained low-FPS windows, and gives each relay guest backpressure so stale position snapshots are dropped instead of accumulating seconds of lag. The write-up says the changes are deterministic for host/guest state, passed a local create/join/binary-forward smoke test, and left touched GDScript clean under gdparse.

### [A Thai design system fixes component rules that were teaching the wrong patterns](https://github.com/vititth-eng/ccd-design-system/pull/5) — vititth-eng / ccd-design-system
ccd-design-system used Fable to graduate two components and correct two design-system rules that downstream apps were likely to copy. The PR adds a segmented filter and a neutral progress meter, restores small buttons to body-size type instead of caption type, removes the same shrunken button from shell page actions, and makes long modals keep their head and foot pinned while the body scrolls. The interesting detail is that each rule is backed by gallery examples — tabs versus segmented filters, two signals side by side, button sizing, long-form modals, and modal versus inline treatment — so the fix changes the teaching surface as well as the CSS.

### [A climate-model aggregator review turns into invariant checks and new tests](https://github.com/ai2cm/ace/pull/1347) — jpdunc23 / ACE
ACE’s PR for normalized correction metrics includes a Fable 5 pre-review that did more than say “looks good.” The review found that explicitly enabled correction metrics could be silently skipped without a normalizer, that time-mean accumulation could become biased if sample counts or variable sets changed, that the multi-window arithmetic had no direct test, and that a private series dataclass was being imported across modules. The author accepted the points: the follow-up commit raises on config/normalizer mismatch for non-default configs, guards sample-count and variable-set invariants, adds a two-window hand-computed test plus error tests, and makes `SeriesData` public.

### [SPIR-V translator patches remove reader-side dead work](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/SPIRV-LLVM-Translator/pull/3850) — MrSidims / KhronosGroup
KhronosGroup’s SPIRV-LLVM-Translator has a two-PR Fable-assisted reader optimization: [one patch](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/SPIRV-LLVM-Translator/pull/3850) stops `validateWordCount()` from seeking to the end of the input stream and back for every module-scope instruction, caching total stream size once instead, while [the follow-up](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/SPIRV-LLVM-Translator/pull/3852) skips maintaining `NamedId` data that only the writer consumes when decoding an existing module. The author says global-variable- and decoration-heavy toy modules translate about 20% faster after the first patch. Both PRs are small, but they show Fable being applied to a mature compiler-adjacent codebase where the useful work is identifying reader/write-path separation and unnecessary per-instruction I/O.

### [IceStorm transient topics get destroyed-state guards from an automated audit](https://github.com/zeroc-ice/ice/pull/5901) — pepone / ZeroC Ice
ZeroC Ice followed an earlier Fable audit patch with another small correctness fix in IceStorm transient mode. The PR reports that destroyed transient topics still accepted `subscribeAndGetPublisher` and `link` calls until manager reaping removed the servant, leaking per-subscriber servants and blocking re-subscription of the same identity; it now throws `ObjectNotExistException`, matching the persistent implementation. The same audit also found that `IceStorm/Finder` was not registered when `IceStorm.Transient=1`, despite the Slice interface documenting it as always available, and the transient `single` and `createOrRetrieve` suites remained green.

### [Wildbook bulk imports stop indexing encounters before image derivatives exist](https://github.com/WildMeOrg/Wildbook/pull/1673) — JasonWildMe / Wildbook
Wildbook’s Fable/Codex sequence started with a bulk-import race: [PR #1673](https://github.com/WildMeOrg/Wildbook/pull/1673) moves encounter OpenSearch indexing after `_master` image derivatives are durably committed, because one observed import left about 34% of encounters permanently without thumbnail URLs. Fable’s QA review verified the ordering fix, called out residual races and missing observability, and identified two adjacent bugs that became Fable-led follow-ups: [a single-id derivative helper that was a silent no-op because `new ArrayList<Integer>(id)` created an empty list](https://github.com/WildMeOrg/Wildbook/pull/1674), and [an executor leak where every bulk index created a fixed thread pool for one task and never shut it down](https://github.com/WildMeOrg/Wildbook/pull/1675). The follow-up PRs both report `mvn compile` passing and include Codex review comments confirming the targeted fixes.

### [chidori-js kernelizes captured loop cells and posts full runtime benchmarks](https://github.com/ThousandBirdsInc/chidori/pull/101) — kvey / ThousandBirdsInc
chidori’s Claude Fable 5 PR makes captured loop bounds and accumulators eligible for kernelization by mapping closure cells into kernel registers, then writing them back on exit, bail, or interrupt. The body spells out the soundness constraint — regions that both write a cell and call a pinned closure stay generic because the callee could snapshot the same upvalue — and adds a corpus for captured bounds, accumulators, late entry, string taint, TDZ reads, bail interleaving, and `-0`. CI comments publish cross-runtime benchmarks against Node and Bun, exact heap-utilization tables, and Test262 coverage at 45,448 of 48,070 executed tests passing.

### [vLLM fixes a CUDA graph capture crash on H200 MIG slices](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/48261) — njhill / vLLM
vLLM has a Claude Fable 5 co-authored bugfix for ModelRunner V2 speculative decoding. The PR traces an illegal-memory-access crash during prefill CUDA graph capture to reused FlashAttention metadata buffers whose contents had already been overwritten by the target model's capture pass; replay was unaffected, making it a capture-only failure. The fix builds fresh dummy inputs and attention metadata for every speculator prefill warmup and capture pass, removes the old `AttentionStatePair` plumbing, and says the previously failing 16 GiB H200 MIG slice plus a full H200 both passed after the change.

### [Texera accepts pandas numpy scalars at Python-operator output boundaries](https://github.com/apache/texera/pull/6278) — eugenegujing / Apache Texera
Apache Texera's Python worker had a real pandas boundary bug: `df.sum()` and `df.any()` can produce `numpy.int64` or `numpy.bool_`, but tuple finalization later checked them with plain Python `isinstance`, so semantically valid INTEGER and BOOL outputs failed schema validation. A Claude Fable 5 co-authored PR normalizes numpy integer scalars through the existing range checks, converts `numpy.bool_` to `bool`, and deliberately leaves out-of-range values loud instead of overflowing silently. The public Codecov comment reports every modified coverable line covered, and follow-up commits tighten LONG bounds after review.

### [A tiny SSH server drops another 1.8 KB with generated crypto constants](https://github.com/eisbaw/nano_ssh_server/pull/30) — eisbaw / nano_ssh_server
nano_ssh_server has a Claude Fable 5 co-authored size-golfing pass that makes a new `v26-genk` build the smallest version yet. The branch generates SHA-512 and SHA-256 constants at startup from the FIPS prime-root definitions, constructs Ed25519 base points at startup, packs KEXINIT name lists, and uses a tiny linker script plus section-header stripping to cut a same-toolchain build from 13,928 bytes to 12,074 bytes. The PR includes a step-by-step optimization log and says real OpenSSH clients printed `Hello World` over three sequential connections, wrong passwords were rejected, `tests/run_tests.sh v26-genk` passed 3/3, and the build produced zero compiler warnings.

### [bandocapt tunes retrieval and then documents a flaky legal-answer regression](https://github.com/vi-phuong-158/bandocapt/pull/20) — vi-phuong-158 / bandocapt
bandocapt's Vietnamese legal-assistance chatbot has a stacked Claude Fable 5 upgrade: [the retrieval PR](https://github.com/vi-phuong-158/bandocapt/pull/20) boosts exact form and decision-number tokens such as `NA17` and `5568/QĐ-BCA`, rewrites short follow-up questions before embedding, moves utility calls to `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, and keeps risky embedding task-type changes gated off by default. The follow-up [UX PR](https://github.com/vi-phuong-158/bandocapt/pull/21) adds streaming status events, exact-title guide deep links for 57 catalogue entries, opt-in Telegram alerts for source-number mismatches or bad feedback, and diagnostic logs for blocked content. The test plan is unusually concrete for an app PR: 151/151 and then 154/154 tests, clean builds, a TR03 smoke, three 30-question regression runs with no confirmed legal hallucinations over 90 turns, and a documented GV02 flaky case traced to Gemini sampling rather than the retrieval changes.

### [supablock gives agent sandboxes a no-mount read-only toolbox](https://github.com/filipecabaco/supablock/pull/15) — filipecabaco / supablock
supablock already exposed `ls` and `cat` without mounting a filesystem; a Claude Fable 5 co-authored PR extends that no-mount mode to `find`, `grep`, `head`, and `tail` for containers, CI, and agent sandboxes. The commands walk the API-backed tree through the same Router guarantees as the mount path — GET-only access, redaction, deterministic output — while binary bodies are flagged rather than dumped and directory walks continue past per-node errors like Unix tools. Follow-up commits make output pipe-safe, share a warm cache across CLI reads, seed a more complex Docker end-to-end database, and publish e2e failure diagnostics to the job summary.

### [RealityEngine_CI finds 1,320 machines after a corpus-layout drift fix](https://github.com/jateeter/RealityEngine_CI/pull/59) — jateeter / RealityEngine_CI
RealityEngine_CI had a generator drift bug: `cesgen` still assumed a flat `examples/machines` layout even after the corpus moved into domain subdirectories. The Claude Fable 5 co-authored fix defaults to the sibling `RealityEngine_Machines/machines` corpus, supports `MACHINES_DIR`, walks subdirectories recursively by basename, and resolves the compiled replay engine through `ENGINE_DIST` with a fallback to the sibling AI build. Regenerating the fixtures now covers 1,320 machines, producing 4,952 oracles and 4,927 contracts, and the C++ parity binaries replay both fixture sets green over 8,763 steps.

### [Bounded replanning for a coding-agent harness](https://github.com/pattei/ralph/pull/5) — Ian Patterson / ralph
Ralph is a shell harness that keeps coding agents moving through a task queue; its Fable 5 co-authored replanning feature gives a stalled run a deliberately constrained escape hatch. When work blocks or deadlocks, the outer loop can launch a planner session that may only add, tighten, unblock within a per-task budget, or retire tasks with a replacement and dependent-task rewrite. Every change is CLI-validated, lock-gated, journaled in append-only `REPLANS.md`, and committed only after the outer loop verifies that the planner touched nothing else; the PR adds 508 tests for the task-plan verbs and 210 runner tests.

### [A CNC analysis tool becomes a shared pipeline and web app](https://github.com/theveloped/analyzer/pull/5) — theveloped
A Claude Fable 5 session turned this manufacturing-analysis CLI into a shared `pipeline.py` core, a typed process registry and FastAPI job layer, then a React/Three.js client that can upload parts, stream cached fields, generate parameter forms, and run CNC or injection-molding analyses through the same cache-writing code. The work includes an algorithmic correction rather than just an interface rewrite: a visibility-based accessibility calculation replaces meshlib’s unstable undercut verdict near tangent walls. On the included `Aligator.STEP` fixture, isolated-face speckles for ±Z fall from about 13,800 to 180 while vertical walls classify deterministically, with analytic regression coverage and the existing command tests still passing.

### [A property finder fills the gap left by email alerts](https://github.com/Akibalogh/listings-analyzer/pull/5) — Aki Balogh
This Fable 5 co-authored change makes a property-listing analyzer query a saved Redfin search itself on a configurable weekly cadence, since email alerts were missing listings. It fetches result pages through Jina Reader to work around Redfin’s cloud-IP bot blocking, extracts and deduplicates listings by address, then hands new finds to the existing enrichment and scoring pipeline; first-time “Worth Touring” results trigger the same Slack notification rule as manual additions. An authenticated on-demand sync endpoint and 12 tests cover parsing, pagination, deduplication, failure handling, scheduler gating, authorization, and notifications.

### [A zero-shot Pygame comparison finds Fable closer to Opus than a leap ahead](https://www.xda-developers.com/i-used-claude-fable-5-for-zero-shot-coding-and-understood-why-anthropic-locked-it-down/) — Abhinav Raj
XDA’s Abhinav Raj gave Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 the same single High-effort prompt: make an original Pygame game in which every failure teaches a mechanic. Both outputs ran without manual debugging and independently converged on a dungeon-crawler premise, but Fable built multiple levels with sentries, zap tiles, crumbling floors, and a failure-driven in-game codex; Opus made a more streamlined lantern game. Raj’s mixed result is useful: Fable consumed more than 22% of his available credits against roughly 15% for Opus, and he judged the returned model closer to “Opus 5.0” than the dramatic capability jump he expected.

### [An e-reader game suite gets an on-device store and a full review pass](https://github.com/jop3/ebook-games/pull/12) — jop3 / ebook-games
A seven-commit Claude Fable 5 session on `ebook-games` did more than tidy Go formatting: it added Spelbutiken, an on-device installer/updater that fetches GitHub releases over the reader’s Wi-Fi, writes installs atomically, and is driven end-to-end against a fake release server. The same 92-file branch fixes review findings across a group of e-ink games, including non-reproducible map iteration, alternate valid puzzle solutions that could never win, corrupt-but-decodable saves that bricked launches, and AI moves that used cards not actually in hand. It adds real tap/swipe/back-key play tests, verifies deterministic generation against an unpruned solver on 40 seeds, and reports a cross-compile to the device’s 32-bit ARM SDK image.

### [A school-admin system moves to central records and role-based access](https://github.com/consolacionburriana-tic/tools/pull/5) — mcmespana / tools
This Claude Fable 5 co-authored production migration turns a school administration tool into a central-records system: it imports student and teacher exports, previews field-level sync conflicts, applies matching and relationship updates transactionally, and records each sync. The author reports a live import of 642 students and 97 teachers, a code regeneration that left 638 students with unique identifiers, and a licenses-data backfill with no unlinked orders. The 83-file branch also replaces a fixed-password administration path with domain-restricted Google login and role/module guards; its test plan includes clean builds, Neon-backed sync idempotence checks, and real JWT-session authorization tests for an administrator versus an ordinary teacher.

### [Seven verified defects from an adversarial Rust database audit](https://github.com/yangop8/gstore-rust/pull/5) — yangop8 / gstore-rust
A Claude Fable 5-assisted, multi-agent audit of the Rust RDF database gstore-rust surfaced seven findings that the author says were each hand-verified before patching. Two were security-critical SPARQL parsing failures: a leading comment could hide an update from the privilege gate, and trailing commands after a query were silently ignored; the same pass caught three crash paths, poisoned-lock handling, and an `ORDER BY`-after-projection bug that returned the wrong row under `LIMIT`. The PR keeps the deferred, riskier Raft and parser findings public instead of folding them into the batch, and adds regressions while taking the workspace from 860 to 864 passing tests with clippy clean.

### [A Discord matchup command stops loading 463,000 rows into Python](https://github.com/imperasus/sylqon/pull/5) — imperasus / sylqon
Sylqon’s Discord `/matchup` command had been loading full ORM records — including a large JSONB statistics column — for every relevant game, then pairing results in Python across a crawled dataset of 463,000 participants. This Claude Fable 5 co-authored patch replaces that path with a single typed SQL self-join aggregate while preserving same-lane/opposite-team pairing, mirror-match handling, null-safe positions, case-insensitive names, loss semantics, and the existing minimum-games threshold. The author reports 133 passing tests, and closed the PR after cherry-picking it onto the linear main branch.

### [Airflow fixes SQL checks around negative expected values](https://github.com/apache/airflow/pull/69736) — Apache Airflow contributor
Apache Airflow’s `SQLColumnCheckOperator` and `SQLTableCheckOperator` had a sign error in tolerance handling: multiplying a negative expected value by `(1 ± tolerance)` could tighten a bound instead of widening it, so an exactly matching `-1000` could fail a `geq_to=-1000` check. This Claude Code/Fable 5 co-authored patch computes the margin as `abs(expected) * tolerance`, leaving positive cases unchanged and making negative ranges expand outward as intended. Parameterized regressions cover all five comparators, positive values, and exact no-tolerance behavior.

### [PostHog notebooks gain dependency-aware input widgets](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/70179) — Daniel Visca / PostHog
A Claude Code/Fable 5 session added typed text, number, date, and dropdown input cells to PostHog’s revamped notebooks. Applying a value generates a constrained Python assignment through the existing run path; the widget is a source node in the dependency graph, so downstream cells become stale and can rerun automatically. The PR explicitly tests the security seam — identifier validation and quoting so widget input cannot escape an assignment — along with dependency wiring and auto-run behavior; the four touched suites report 45 passing tests.

### [Texera stops silently dropping malformed CSV and JSONL rows](https://github.com/apache/texera/pull/6323) — Eugene Gujing / Apache Texera
Texera inferred scan-column types from its first 100 rows, then silently converted later parse failures to null and filtered whole rows out of query results. A Claude Code/Fable 5 co-authored patch changes that failure mode to a fail-fast error naming the offending row, value, and column, while retaining the original exception as its cause. The author verified the changed scan behavior manually in the UI with a bad value on row 150, and reports 28 focused tests plus a 1,721-test backend gate passing.

### [Mitos designs a pre-claimed checkout path for warm sandboxes](https://github.com/mitos-run/mitos/pull/896) — stubbi / Mitos
Mitos uses Firecracker microVM snapshots but still spent control-plane time creating and claiming a sandbox for each hosted request. This Claude Fable 5 co-authored PR adds an opt-in buffer of already activated, org-less sandboxes; an eligible request claims one through a single resource-version-guarded label patch, while empty buffers and ineligible requests retain the old path. The branch records race, controller envtest, unit, lint, Helm-render, and build checks, and deliberately makes no speed claim before rollout: its 20-request production baseline is committed for a before/after measurement after deployment.

## 2026-07-11

### [An 8-million-token research preprint, audited from primary sources](https://www.futureofbeinghuman.com/p/a-quick-update-on-using-claude-fable-5) — Andrew Maynard
Andrew Maynard gave Fable 5 in Claude Code an education-and-AI research task, the original draft, critical reviews, his own feedback, and a folder of cited documents, then left it to work. Its own audit counted more than 80 subagents, 2,000 tool calls, 120-plus primary sources checked, nearly 15 task-clock hours, and over 8 million tokens — nearly half on verification — before producing a 53-page preprint. Maynard found the result academically rigorous and unusually transparent about its process, but also dense, stylistically homogenized, and still dependent too often on secondary sources.

### [A newsletter draft rebuilt against 110 previous editions](https://charliehills.substack.com/p/how-to-actually-use-fable-5) — Charlie Hills
Charlie Hills used Fable 5 to analyze 110 past MarTech AI editions, derive a scoring rubric, score the draft of the article readers now see at 51/100, and rewrite it until it cleared the bar. His report turns that experience into a repeatable four-stage loop: make a draft, check it against an explicit rubric, revise, and stop only when the check passes. He also documents the guardrails he found necessary in long sessions, including asking the model to verify claimed progress against real results and to stop rather than make unrequested changes.

### [Eighty review findings become a 57-commit scientific-computing repair pass](https://github.com/jkitchin/discopt-doe/pull/1) — John Kitchin
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored review of the experimental-design package `discopt-doe` produced 80 findings across four priority levels, each with a proposed confirmation, fix, and verification path. The resulting PR was merged after 57 commits touching 49 files: among the fixes are honoring Excel formula cells instead of dropping runs, avoiding double-counting prior Fisher information across sequential rounds, returning constraint-feasible designs, and detecting non-orthogonal ANOVA inputs. The public branch is a useful example of a model-led repository audit being turned into an inspectable, incremental repair series rather than a single undifferentiated rewrite.

### [A privacy review keeps agent memory tools scoped by default](https://github.com/ribbons-digital/memory-lane/pull/186) — ribbons-digital
The maintainer asked Fable 5 to pre-review a deliberately narrow change to Memory Lane’s Pi adapter: project memory review and deletion should stay project-scoped unless a caller writes `--all`. The PR changes flag parsing so `--all` cannot be mistaken for a memory ID, adds coverage for default, global-only, null-project, and explicit cross-project cases, and documents the behavior. After resolving an isolated-worktree setup problem, the author reports focused and full adapter tests plus an end-user-style transcript showing that inaccessible deleted-memory text does not leak.

### [pnpm fixes a v12 self-update failure without weakening its symlink guard](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/pull/12930) — pnpm contributor
A Claude Code session using Fable 5 traced a pnpm v12 update failure to the unscoped package wrapper’s literal `@` path segment: the native-binary trust-root reader walked the global virtual-store layout incorrectly and rejected a legitimate sibling-slot symlink as outside the store. The fix derives the root through `format_global_virtual_store_path`, the same formatter that writes that layout, instead of maintaining a separate upward walk. Regression tests cover the previously broken unscoped wrapper, a scoped wrapper, and the security boundary that must still reject a symlink genuinely escaping `links`.

### [PostHog makes long-running notebook cells safely cancellable](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/70168) — Daniel Visca / PostHog
A Claude Code/Fable 5 session added an end-to-end cancel path for PostHog’s revamped SQL and Python notebook cells. The important detail is that cancellation is tied to a specific run: queued work can be cancelled before execution, stale requests cannot `SIGINT` whichever cell happens to be active, and an interrupted run returns its already captured stdout, stderr, and figures rather than presenting a generic failure. The author reports 107 backend endpoint tests, 22 kernel-bootstrap tests, and 25 frontend logic tests, while documenting that cancelling the server-side ClickHouse query itself remains out of scope.

### [A dock workspace learns to move tabs safely between windows](https://github.com/neomjs/neo/pull/15017) — Mnemosyne / Neo
Neo merged a Fable 5-authored cross-window drag-and-drop layer for docked workspaces. Foreign drops now go through the project’s atomic two-document `transferItem` executor, update the receiving workspace’s durable placement hints in the same commit, and fail closed for unstamped payloads, unresolvable sources, or item-ID collisions rather than silently applying a local drop. The PR’s focused suite exercises the real coordinator and executor over two simulated windows, including the collision and one-shot suppression cases; the dashboard suite reports 257 passing tests.

### [A GUI harness catches Fable verifying the wrong thing](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/76553) — Claude Code user
One Claude Code user documented a failure pattern during a Fable 5 repair session for a live GUI verification harness. While generalizing a routine run, the model first introduced an `__file__` `NameError` in code executed without that variable, then fixed it but added data to the application model without refreshing the visible worksheet; the analysis produced a report while the on-screen input lacked the response column it had analyzed. Its delivery receipt still reported success because it checked that a report rendered, not the stronger invariant that the visible input and analyzed data matched — a specific example of self-verification checking a proxy rather than the user-facing state.

### [Six sites rebranded by routing Fable only to design decisions](https://codemeetai.substack.com/p/my-honest-weekend-building-with-fable) — Malik Chohra
Malik Chohra used Fable 5 to set design tokens and consistency rules for a shared Next.js boilerplate, then let cheaper subagents apply routine edits across six live sites during a weekend rebrand. He says Fable's initial use as the session model stalled parallel subagent fan-outs; pinning those workers to Opus 4.8 while retaining Fable for architecture and token choices removed the failure. The deployment produced a reusable AI Web Launcher workflow, and he frames repo indexing plus model routing—not Fable alone—as what prevented the agent from inventing structure.

### [A book-authoring app builds cloud collections around two live desktop sessions](https://github.com/BloomBooks/BloomDesktop/pull/8048) — John Thomson / BloomBooks
Bloom Desktop’s team has used a Claude Fable 5 agent for a large, still-draft Cloud Team Collections implementation: 217 commits, 240 files, and roughly 41,000 added lines. It stores book contents in S3 and locks, versions, and membership in Supabase with row-level security, then adds sign-in, sharing, remote-update handling, and a Playwright-over-CDP harness that drives two real application instances against a local stack. The PR reports all individual end-to-end cases and the full C# suite passing except one known same-machine lock-takeover assertion; subsequent review caught and pinned an S3-prefix credential-scoping flaw, a reaper counter bug, and error-code handling with targeted fixes and tests.
