### [Five trading strategies get reproducible edge verdicts instead of a roster default](https://github.com/richkuo/go-trader/pull/1325) — richkuo
A Fable 5 Claude Code session analyzed the five registered go-trader strategies that had no published edge verdict, using fee-aware M5 screens, noise gates, held-out 2023–2025H1 windows, and a separate funding-aware run where the regular harness could not represent hedged carry. The resulting study recommends retaining only short, ranging-gated legs for two anchored-VWAP strategies, deprecating the ATR-band spot-long leg after a −23.4% gross result, and keeping a bear-pullback short leg that reported +13.49% net per leg with a trade-level noise p-value of 0.007. It explicitly withholds a verdict on the funding strategy rather than treating naked shorts as a proxy, and includes commands and committed harness outputs to reproduce each conclusion.

### [A repair session repeatedly verifies proxies instead of the visible result](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/76557) — Mig-Sornrakrit
A Claude Code user documented a follow-on Fable 5 failure case in a GUI automation harness: after the model correctly diagnosed that its receipt only checked whether a report rendered, it added a new check against a pre-staged sheet rather than the sheet actually displayed to the user. In both cases the report could be present while the visible worksheet was not the input that had been analyzed, so the automated receipt returned success on a proxy rather than the required equality. The author’s workaround is unusually specific: replace model-written updates with the app’s real handler, assert surface-level equalities, enforce the rule in project files, and retain human visual review as the final gate.

### [A quantization audit fixes silent MoE corruption paths before Blackwell support](https://github.com/varjoranta/turboquant-vllm/pull/57) — gaby / turboquant-vllm
A Claude Fable 5 session audited TurboQuant-vLLM’s weights-compression path and found failure modes that could silently discard tensor-parallel MoE loads, compress uninitialized data, or replay a CUDA graph with a previous layer’s codebook. The resulting 11-file patch changes load accounting and replay behavior, makes Triton-less hosts use documented fallbacks, and gates GPU targets by the CUDA release that supports them so B200/B300 systems do not break JIT builds. The author reports 191 CPU tests passing with 21 hardware-gated skips and four adversarial review rounds, while explicitly leaving CUDA compilation and live Blackwell/TP smoke tests for a machine with `nvcc` and appropriate GPUs.

### [A flaky-test repair separates timer races from overloaded-host failures](https://github.com/dork-labs/dorkos/pull/226) — doriancollier / Dorkos
Dorkos used Fable 5 to investigate a batch of flaky relay, watcher, and command-palette tests rather than merely adding retries. The repair exposes a deterministic TTL-sweep hook to replace a periodic-timer race; it also reproduces file-watcher timeouts under real cross-process contention and distinguishes those from a wider host-starvation ceiling, with a follow-up issue for the latter. The author reports each changed test passing 10–15 isolated runs and repeated full package gates without `VITEST_RETRY`, and notes an early verification mistake—leaving a fast background sweep enabled—that Fable’s iteration caught and removed.

### [A zsh proxy hook stops deleting a live configuration](https://github.com/aieo-product/AIkeychain/pull/143) — syn-otani / AIkeychain
A macOS-facing AIkeychain bug came from using Bash’s `/dev/tcp` probe in `.zshrc`: even a running proxy was judged dead, so its configuration file was deleted before `BASE_URL` and the token could be sourced. The implementation was written with Codex, while Fable 5 handled design, adversarial review, and validation; it retained `/usr/bin/nc` over the more complex `ztcp` alternative and identified that existing managed blocks needed an in-place migration instead of an early return. The PR includes a red reproduction against a live listener, five real-`/bin/zsh` regression cases, and reports all 144 Swift tests passing, while preserving separate follow-ups for proxy identity and startup-race concerns.

### [A clinical benchmark drops a leakage-prone feature and its score falls](https://github.com/harshsingh8005/rosenbound-benchmarks/pull/5) — harshsingh8005
A Claude Fable 5 session audited a public mortality benchmark and found that `n_diagnoses` was an untimestamped, discharge-coded count — information unavailable when the prediction is meant to be made. It replaced that feature with a strictly prior-admission Charlson history, then relocked the demo result: AUROC fell from 0.6642 to 0.6197, consistent with removing leakage rather than improving a model. The same 31-file branch adds a deterministic 3,700-report FAERS adverse-event reproducer with a train-before-test vocabulary, reports 24 passing tests and four expected data-backed skips, and leaves the untested internal-repository check explicitly as follow-up work.

### [A Codex plugin uses Fable as a read-only plan advisor](https://github.com/Cjbuilds/Codex-Orchestration/pull/1) — Cjbuilds
Codex-Orchestration added an opt-in MCP bridge that calls Fable 5 through an authenticated Claude Code installation while keeping a separately selected model responsible for execution. The workflow pins the model and effort, scrubs provider overrides, validates plan decisions, checks returned model metadata, and treats failures as non-approval rather than routing around them. Its author reports 139 unit tests, marketplace install/upgrade lifecycle smokes, and a live end-to-end run in which Fable returned `PLAN_APPROVED` before a GPT-5.6 executor returned `END_TO_END_OK` without altering files.

### [A TypeScript agent-definition parser follows a Python oracle](https://github.com/aurokin/custom_skills/pull/5) — aurokin
A Claude Fable 5 session ported a shared Python agent-definition schema into TypeScript for a multi-harness custom-skills tool, keeping the Python implementation as the parity oracle. The new parser covers enums, regular expressions, XOR rules, reserved-key checks, Copilot target gating, and skill-name normalization; 63 parity tests include three real definitions. The author also used a workflow evidence pass, adversarial review, and Diffwarden, fixing two findings while explicitly declining the rest for oracle-compatibility reasons, and reports 224 tests green.

### [Romania’s grid dashboard adds regional price context and an interconnection map](https://github.com/aoprisan/Megawat/pull/5) — aoprisan
After cataloguing feasible features for Megawat, a Claude Fable 5 session shipped two of its recommendations: regional day-ahead price context and a live Romanian interconnection map. The backend now stores ENTSO-E A44 prices by zone, compares Romania with Hungary, Bulgaria, and Serbia over shared hours, and rejects non-EUR documents; the frontend adds an accessible drill-down map whose arrows reflect import or export direction. The author reports 74 backend tests, `clippy -D warnings`, TypeScript checks, the full end-to-end verification script, and Chromium screenshots in light, dark, and 390px layouts.

### [A Fable conductor replaces redundant planning with plan reviews](https://github.com/filippo-fonseca/better-gsd/pull/5) — Filippo Fonseca
Better GSD, a Claude Code orchestration plugin, is adding a Fable-as-Advisor mode for sessions whose conductor is already running Fable 5. Instead of launching a second Fable pre-planner for each work unit, the conductor reviews sealed plans, steers agents before execution, reads commit logs and verification reports, and writes the next wave’s seed plans directly; raw source and diffs remain with a separate Opus reviewer. The change is off by default outside three explicit activation paths and adds 15 gate cases plus a new run-live seed-skip test; its author marks the real end-to-end Fable session as the remaining unchecked test.

### [A Maltese Parliament podcast feed keeps episodes after sources forget them](https://github.com/mdebono/parlament.podcast.mt/pull/5) — mdebono
A Claude Fable 5 session reworked `parlament.podcast.mt`, which had rebuilt its RSS feed from only the 20 newest plenary sittings, making episodes disappear when the source rolled forward and omitting committee meetings. The new path merges the existing archive with the Parliament homepage’s Latest Media Files API, stores every mirrored recording in an R2-backed catalogue, freezes published metadata and GUIDs on first ingest, and rebuilds the feed from that catalogue. Its added tests cover a source outage after initial ingest, duplicate discovery across the two sources, failed audio mirroring, catalogue-write failure before publication, multipart recordings, and unexpected API response shapes.

### [A Fable review finds a Bitcoin sale path that asks for more bitcoin than exists](https://github.com/genesisdotre/network-state-genesis/issues/3) — marsrobertson
A Fable 5 review of an on-chain NFT contract began by recompiling the verified source, matching it byte-for-byte to deployed code, and simulating suspect calls against Ethereum mainnet. It found that `purchaseWithWBTC()` requests `10 ** 18` base units from an eight-decimal token — 10 billion BTC rather than one — so the advertised purchase path must always revert. The same investigation isolated a Solidity 0.8.0–0.8.3 compiler behavior in which an earlier `emit` let a later bare event invocation compile; the reviewer reproduced it with a minimal contract and found that the deployed bytecode behaves identically to the corrected `emit` form.

### [A Fable review modernizes ROOT’s standalone Minuit2 build](https://github.com/root-project/root/pull/22788) — Henry Schreiner
While updating ROOT’s standalone Minuit2 build, Henry Schreiner fixed several Windows and CMake compatibility problems, then asked Claude Fable to review the work; the review found additional faults in include paths, package-registry assumptions, nested-build configuration, install directories, and a cache-value typo. The resulting nine-file patch forwards generator configuration into the nested CMake build, replaces an inert package-registry export with an explicit `Minuit2_DIR`, uses `GNUInstallDirs`, and removes obsolete imported-target fallbacks. Schreiner reports testing locally, and the pull request identifies Fable as the reviewing assistant rather than claiming a fully autonomous change.

### [An ATmega emulator finds three firmware bugs before hardware testing](https://github.com/laurb9/tiny_scope/pull/32) — laurb9
A Claude Fable 5 session added a simavr-based CI smoke test for the Tiny Scope firmware, running an emulated ATmega328P against a committed serial-trace oracle while compiling all trace instrumentation out of the production build. The emulator immediately exposed three bugs: an unchecked SSD1306 framebuffer allocation that could corrupt low memory and reboot real hardware, an assignment mistaken for a comparison that indexed ADC settings out of bounds on fresh EEPROM, and a double-shift that disabled the wrong pin’s input buffer. The author reports all five PlatformIO environments green and an unchanged production Uno footprint of 21,756 bytes of flash and 490 bytes of RAM.

### [A recursive graph query exposes stale closure-table rows](https://github.com/fiedl/wingolfsplattform/pull/132) — fiedl
A Claude Fable 5 session added a Postgres recursive-CTE query path alongside a Rails application's existing materialized graph closure, without switching production reads over yet. Its parity suite covers cycles, diamonds, cross-type paths, expired memberships, and membership gaps; a rake task then compared the two implementations against a restored production database with 53,504 direct links and 159,805 closure rows. It found 13 mismatches in 6,600 comparisons, and manual chain checks attributed the sampled differences to missing or stale closure rows rather than the CTE traversal; the author reports all five local test-suite slices green.

### [A document-filing agent catches its own zero-byte PDF bug](https://github.com/beep-effect/beep-effect/pull/370) — kriegcloud / beep-effect
A large Claude Fable 5-authored legal-document-intake branch adds text extraction for PDF and DOCX files, a schema-validated filing decision with a visible rationale, and an inbox route for uncertainty, provider failures, or low-confidence classifications. A real-browser smoke using deliberately uninformative filenames correctly filed an MSA PDF and a motion-to-compel DOCX from their extracted text; an invalid API key routed both to the inbox instead of silently guessing. The same smoke revealed that pdfjs detached the uploaded byte buffer, leaving a filed PDF at zero bytes despite green unit tests; the fix copies input before extraction and checks that the resulting 937-byte file matches its digest.

### [A sabotage test proves three Python mocks had never reached production code](https://github.com/mrveiss/AutoBot-AI/pull/11620) — mrveiss
AutoBot-AI used Fable 5 to audit its Python test-loader machinery after discovering that `mock.patch()` could attach to a MagicMock standing in for a `services.*` module, leaving the real function untouched. The audit examined 261 patch calls, confirmed three inert tests in pricing-staleness coverage, and changed the fixture loader to bind real-loaded service modules onto the parent stub. The maintainer verified the repair by forcing the checked function to return a stale date: two tests then failed as they should, and returned to 63 passing after the change was reverted; the merged one-file patch also records 131 unchanged pre-existing collection errors.

### [A multipart upload path proves out across direct and relay storage](https://github.com/cagojeiger/filegate/pull/10) — cagojeiger
A Claude Fable 5 session added multipart uploads to Filegate while keeping a single two-phase contract across direct object storage, streamed relay-to-S3, and relay-to-filesystem modes. It treats reissuing a part URL as both lease renewal and resume, serializes simultaneous writes to the same part, and leaves the accounting model based on the declared full-file size rather than individual chunks. The author reports a 12MiB three-part end-to-end scenario passing 52 assertions twice in each mode, including resume, out-of-order parts, incomplete commits, expiry reclamation, and zero incomplete vendor sessions; the regression suites report 49 upload, 50 relay, 72 registry, and 25 unit tests.

### [A Zstandard backend keeps a 100-case round-trip matrix green](https://github.com/jasontitus/lrzip/pull/1) — jasontitus
A Claude Fable 5 session added a Zstandard backend to lrzip, mapping its levels 1–9 onto Zstandard’s 1–22 range and using long-distance matching over windows scaled from 24 to 27 bits. The same change stops rescaling LZMA levels, makes the dictionary explicit up to 128MB, and preserves the classic zpaq configuration after a text test measured 0.204 in 49 seconds versus 0.207 in 55 seconds for a libzpaq alternative. The author reports that the updated backend matrix round-tripped all 100 cases with `-Z` enabled, while clearly noting that archives using the new block type require the updated program version.

### [A production audit finds former trip members could still read and write data](https://github.com/Chravel-Inc/chravel-web/pull/800) — MeechYourGoals / Chravel
A Claude Fable 5 production-readiness audit found that a trip-planning app’s live database still gave former members read and write access to chat messages, locations, artifacts, payment attachments, media, reactions, and more because several RLS policies used status-agnostic membership checks. The proposed migration changes those policies to require active membership, while the same review routes notifications through a canonical service-role function rather than trusting client-provided targets. It also fixes an offline-sync race with an atomic compare-and-swap claim and a concurrency regression test, and makes rate limiting and payment-webhook idempotency fail closed.

### [A batch pipeline generates 120 guided prompts and source-checked shrine records](https://github.com/ishioka771977-sketch/uranai-monster/pull/5) — ishioka771977-sketch
The Japanese spiritual-guidance app Uranai Monster used Fable 5 Batches to generate all 120 combinations in a structured “seed” content set, passing its forbidden-word and length checks after individually retrying 17 initially rejected cells. In the same branch, its source-preserving collection path switched from search to direct retrieval and extraction, then found 15 usable shrine records across 13 prefectures; each accepted record includes a verbatim deity citation, and the pilot reports zero rejected matches. An end-to-end check covers the path from a birth date through the calculated guardian, month branch, generated seed, and location-specific recommendation.

### [An agent team moves its operating rules to one source of truth](https://github.com/CityBear3/dotfiles/pull/5) — CityBear3
A Fable 5-led Claude Code setup refactored its own global instructions after finding seven mismatches between the top-level policy and the skills and agent definitions that actually govern behavior. The new split leaves global instructions for phase gates, escalation, and autonomy boundaries, while agent lists, triage criteria, and loop mechanics now have a single owning definition; the maintainer kept Fable 5 as lead and Opus 4.8 as teammates. Structural checks confirmed six replacement phrases appeared exactly once, eight stale phrases disappeared, and the installed `CLAUDE.md`, design-discussion skill, and code-reviewer copies matched the source after deployment.

### [A one-line CSS bug report turns into a real-browser investigation](https://simonw.substack.com/p/claude-fable-is-relentlessly-proactive) — Simon Willison
Simon Willison gave Fable 5 a screenshot of an unwanted scrollbar in Datasette Agent and the instruction to look in dependencies. The Claude Code session ran the local application, tried Chrome, Firefox, and WebKit, then discovered Safari was the relevant browser and wrote temporary template JavaScript to open the otherwise keyboard-only dialog. To measure the shadow-DOM textarea, it also built a small local CORS receiver and injected a browser-side probe; after Fable fell back to Opus, the continuation tested and verified the two-line CSS fix. Willison published the complete terminal transcript and estimates the session would have cost about $12.11 at full API prices.

### [Prepared geometries cut a 20,000-row spatial predicate probe from 1,246 to 17 microseconds](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/1942) — jeswr / SPARQ
A Fable 5 agent reworked SPARQ’s GeoSPARQL relation registry so a repeated polygon gets an indexed `PreparedGeometry` on its first warm cache hit, while one-off streaming operands retain the old path. The branch differentially checks all 24 supported relations across nine geometry classes, including cold and warm cases, malformed patterns, CRS mismatches, and deliberately sabotaged cache/table variants. In the author’s explicitly non-canonical EC2 probe — a 151KB, 3,900-vertex polygon against 20,000 points — the old path took about 1,246 microseconds per row and the new one about 17, with identical results; default, no-default, and all-feature Rust tests, Clippy, docs, and a 91.62% coverage gate are reported green.

### [Redis makes four-worker takeover approvals atomic](https://github.com/mrveiss/AutoBot-AI/pull/11660) — mrveiss / AutoBot-AI
AutoBot-AI’s four Uvicorn workers had kept takeover and desktop-streaming session state in per-process dictionaries, so an approval or WebSocket broadcast could land on the wrong worker. A Fable 5-led change moves metadata to Redis, uses `GETDEL` so only one worker can approve a request, and relays cross-worker desktop events through pub/sub while leaving actual WebSocket clients local. A two-manager fake-Redis test covers visibility, expiry, double-approval prevention, relay delivery, and self-echo suppression; the author reports 14 focused tests and lint passing, and notes that the main session found and repaired 12 un-awaited call sites plus a relay that the delegated implementation had failed to start.

### [Scheduler timers gain an explicit process-exit opt-in](https://github.com/kibertoad/toad-scheduler/pull/275) — kibertoad
A Fable 5 co-authored Toad Scheduler change adds an opt-in `unref` setting for cron, interval, and long-interval jobs, allowing a Node.js process to exit naturally when the scheduler is its only remaining handle. The implementation propagates the setting through Croner and the long-interval job’s internal timer chain, while a scheduler-wide default yields to an explicit per-job choice and browsers remain a no-op. The proposed tests inspect timer reference state for the default and opted-in cases, including the long-delay path and scheduler-level overrides; documentation cautions against enabling it for a standalone worker that relies on scheduled timers to stay alive.

### [An initialization audit catches two double-start bugs](https://github.com/neomjs/neo/pull/15039) — Grace / Neo
A Claude Fable 5 agent audited 40 external `initAsync()` calls across 22 production files and moved them to the idempotent `ready()` surface. In the process it found StorageRouter was initialized twice on every boot because its guard checked an unset `_initPromise`, while AgentOrchestrator recreated every MCP client after construction; it also removed a DreamService guard that could never fire. The branch adds a 120-line invariant test for non-super initialization calls and nonlocal `_initPromise` reads, demonstrated red-to-green with exact file-and-line failures; its author leaves the focused suite and hosted CI as the remaining regression gates.

### [A desktop policy tool proves fail-closed data access](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/1946) — jeswr / SPARQ
A Fable 5 autonomous agent added an opt-in ODRL policy workbench to SPARQ’s desktop application, letting a user author Turtle policies, evaluate a party/action/target request, and compare the same SPARQL query with and without named-graph access controls. The native path deliberately uses the one-shot bridge rather than a conditional variant with a known public-widening hazard, while the web build says plainly that it cannot run the native feature. Its tests show a prohibition hiding a previously visible secret memo for one requester, malformed policy input returning zero rows with a visible parser reason, and a lean build failing loudly rather than silently bypassing the feature; the PR reports 17–19 Rust tests by feature state, 53 UI unit tests, and 68 Playwright tests passing.

### [A histogram port matches the original renderer again](https://github.com/jwildfire/safety.viz/pull/23) — jwildfire
A Claude Code session with Fable 5 compared safety.viz against the original RhoInc renderer and found that grouped charts were recomputing their own bin domains: the ADBDS demo showed 8, 10, and 10 bins where the original shared 15 bins from the full result set. It also replaced a placeholder Shimazaki–Shinomoto calculation and restored the original five-bin floors, unique-value clamps, and edge-case behavior after executing the original d3 v3 implementation. The fix was checked against the live demo’s shared edges and totals, with 85 unit tests, 19 Playwright tests, and 104 evidence records reported green; the author notes that Linux screenshot baselines still need their intentional update.

### [An external audit restores annotations lost during share-code conversion](https://github.com/damdor/poe-buildwright/pull/5) — damdor
An external Fable 5 audit of Poe Buildwright found that `/agent/build` silently stripped every annotation when turning a plan into a share link: build notes, capture names and notes, target notes, and gear notes all arrived blank. The merged five-file repair maps those fields through the conversion, adds machine-readable validation diagnostics and an example manifest, and also corrects a pre-existing route-cost bug that labelled previously covered targets unreachable. The author checked a plan with one build, two captures, four targets, and a gear note against local Wrangler Pages, decoding the returned share code to confirm all annotations survived and that over-budget output carries the suggested remedy.

### [List filters become opt-in SQL queries with a real application test](https://github.com/jacobgawel/flexapi/pull/5) — jacobgawel
FlexApi merged a Fable 5 co-authored query layer that lets its list endpoints accept ANDed `field:operator:value` filters and comma-separated sorts, but only for scalar entity properties explicitly marked `QueryField`. It builds expression trees for EF Core to translate to SQL, fails invalid metadata at startup, and returns bad client queries through a central 400 handler rather than treating them as server errors. The 37-file change adds unit cases for filtering, sorting, metadata, and validation plus Testcontainers integration tests against Postgres and Redis; its GitHub Actions “Build & Test” check completed successfully.

### [Thirty-three CI jobs become nine without dropping a gate](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/1972) — jeswr / SPARQ
A Fable 5 agent consolidated five SPARQ GitHub Actions workflows from 33 jobs to nine, preserving the old commands as named steps while eliminating roughly 24 runner claims per event. The hard part was branch protection: the PR programmatically checks that every formerly gating job still fails the summary gate while the three advisory checks remain non-blocking, and retains the feature-selector job ID and output order needed by downstream Kani verification. `actionlint`, YAML loading, gate and advisory-registry tests, feature-matrix goldens, and the selector self-tests are all reported clean; the author asks reviewers to verify the resulting check inventory on pull request, merge-queue, and main-push runs before merging.

### [An opt-in WASM policy evaluator adds 135KB, with a real runtime smoke](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/1973) — jeswr / SPARQ
Another Fable 5 agent session added a non-default `policy` feature to SPARQ’s WebAssembly crate, exposing small ODRL policy-evaluation and conflict-detection functions without shipping it in the standard JavaScript bundle. The author measured the disabled build as byte-identical to `origin/main`; enabling the evaluator raised the pre-optimization WASM from 1,580,701 to 1,715,729 bytes. Besides unit tests for permit, deny, malformed input, and conflict handling, the branch built an actual Node-target WASM package and exercised the exports in a `POLICY-WASM-SMOKE-OK` run; a mutation that hard-coded allow changed two tests from green to red.

### [One GPU decode now feeds multiple video-transcoding rungs](https://github.com/moq-dev/moq/pull/2158) — kixelated / moq-dev
A Claude Fable 5 session refactored moq-transcode so multiple output sizes share one source subscription and decoder instead of decoding the same input once per rung. CUDA frames now resize on the GPU with a box-filter kernel and stay in device memory through encoding, while a CPU path remains as fallback; the accompanying CLI adds a feature-gated `moq transcode` command. The author ran GPU kernel-versus-CPU checks on an RTX 3070 Ti, a deterministic two-rung software test, and a live relay pipeline from ffmpeg through two exported H.264 outputs, checking dimensions and 4:2:0 format with ffprobe; driverless builds are also reported to fall back cleanly.

### [Four audits find a duplicate-receipt race in an offline warehouse app](https://github.com/PaulDelamare/Nutrichain-Mobile/pull/5) — PaulDelamare
A Fable 5 audit pass over Nutrichain Mobile found that `expo-camera` can call its frame processor again before React re-renders, allowing one pallet barcode to create two stacked receipt screens and two receipts. The same PR fixes an older blocked operation disappearing after 50 newer rows, a hard-coded online indicator, unhandled SQLite failures, and SQL tests that could stay green even if parameter order stopped every scan from being marked synchronized. It replaces string-fragment assertions with real SQLite execution on Node 22, catches a malformed interpolated `IN` clause that typechecking missed, and reports 106 passing tests, clean typechecking, lint, and a successful GitHub Actions run.

### [A synthetic camera test makes mobile barcode failures diagnosable](https://github.com/nmcginn/bluray-collection-tracker/pull/5) — nmcginn
After barcode scanning failed on real discs, a Fable 5 session traced the problems to low-resolution camera delivery, missing rotated-frame scanning, and phone camera selection that could choose a fixed-focus or ultra-wide lens. The fix requests 1080p, enables ZXing’s `TRY_HARDER` mode with UPC/EAN-only decoding, requests continuous focus where available, and adds a camera switcher plus a copied diagnostic report of scanner and lookup events. The author verified decoding, lookup logging, the developer panel, clipboard export, and the no-camera path end to end in Chromium using a synthetic UPC-A video.

### [A computer-use agent clears 10 of 13 basic benchmark tasks](https://github.com/trycua/cua/pull/2162) — ddupont808 / Cua
A Fable 5 evaluation branch added a function-tool fallback for the Bedrock model, whose API does not provide Anthropic’s hosted computer tools, and made the agent loop tolerate Bedrock’s strict tool-result pairing. The author reports Fable completing 10 of 13 CUA-Bench basic tasks and a 0.267 mean on the full KiCad set; retries were raised to six so provider 503s do not immediately abandon a long run. The public pull request has green agent, computer, MCP, lint, and build checks, while the accompanying Muse Spark comparison scored 10/13 basics and 0.246 across 23 KiCad tasks.

### [Four adversarial reviews repair quantizer and loader correctness bugs](https://github.com/varjoranta/turboquant-vllm/pull/58) — gaby / TurboQuant
A Claude Fable 5 audit of TurboQuant’s vLLM integration found 15 issues in learned-rotation reconstruction, variable-bit MoE decoding, AWQ packing, pruning saliency, and MLX loading; four adversarial review passes reduced the finding count from 15 to 8, 4, 1, and then zero. The fixes make CPU-runnable tests out of a suite that had been hidden behind a CUDA skip, and add regression coverage for learned-rotation parity, AWQ order, slot-major pruning, and per-projection MoE bits. The author reports 221 CPU tests and pre-commit passing, and the PR’s Python 3.11, 3.12, and 3.13 CI jobs are green; GPU-only dispatch changes remain explicitly unrun on hardware.

### [A 13-million-execution fuzz sweep finds the remaining primitive-layer nits](https://github.com/nxm-rs/nectar/pull/227) — mfw78 / nectar
Claude Fable review lenses inspected a Rust storage refactor with a differential wire harness over roughly 4,000 node images at both reference widths, 13 million fuzz executions, and a per-crate semver audit. The sweep reported no blockers or major defects, but did surface incorrect primitive naming, duplicated width constants, manual mode implementations, stale documentation, and a fuzz lockfile that dirtied the tree. The targeted follow-up derives codec widths from their types and removes the duplicated implementations; the visible CI has green unit, fuzz-build, smoke-fuzz, Clippy, documentation, audit, and WebAssembly checks.

### [A one-million-token model is no longer compacted as if it had 200,000](https://github.com/affaan-m/ECC/pull/2497) — nankingjing / ECC
An ECC integration discovered that its transcript-context resolver treated unmarked `claude-fable-5` sessions as having the conventional 200,000-token window until observed usage passed that threshold, even though Fable 5 is configured with a native one-million-token window. That inflated the calculated context percentage and could recommend compaction prematurely in the early part of a long session. The two-case regression test reproduces the bad 200,000 result at 50,000 observed tokens for both bare and vendor-prefixed model IDs; the revised test file reports 32 passing cases.

### [A five-agent audit finds three high-severity legal-AI gaps](https://github.com/LegalQuants/lq-ai/issues/288) — Saif Al Younan
A Claude Fable 5 multi-agent audit swept LQ.AI’s gateway, API, web, desktop, bridges, skills, CI, and Docker surfaces, then adversarially rechecked each candidate against the project’s actual loopback-only Compose deployment. The resulting 22 findings include three rated High: unauthenticated inference and embeddings routes on the flat Compose network, a chat-creation IDOR that can retrieve another user’s knowledge-base chunks, and stored XSS in a skill viewer that renders Markdown without DOMPurify. The report also documents limits on reachability rather than treating every finding as internet-exposed, and excludes two apparent credential findings after tracing them to the auditor’s uncommitted local files instead of `origin/main`.

### [A music-player audit maps crashes, races, and unbounded downloads](https://github.com/WpfMusicPlayer-Dev/WpfMusicPlayer/issues/51) — WpfMusicPlayer-Dev
A Claude Fable 5 scan of WpfMusicPlayer traces several native/managed boundary failures with file and line references, including a `Dispose()` race around FFT data, lyric tags without milliseconds throwing across the C++/CLI boundary, and audio buffers freed immediately after asynchronous FAudio flushing. It also identifies an NCM album-art path that fetches metadata-supplied URLs without a host allowlist, timeout, or response-size cap, plus shutdown joins that can wait behind those HTTP calls. The public issue separates code-path confirmations from items needing reproduction and supplies concrete test or stress-test recipes rather than presenting the findings as finished fixes.

### [One malformed embedding setting can restart-loop a memory server](https://github.com/CarlDog/openchronicle-mcp/issues/14) — CarlDog
A Fable 5 fleet review of OpenChronicle’s `main` branch found that an invalid embedding timeout or provider value can escape configuration parsing, terminate `oc serve`, and loop forever under the project’s `restart: unless-stopped` Compose policy. The reviewer recommends reusing the existing safe float parser and degrading an invalid provider to `none`, matching the system’s stated FTS5-only fallback behavior for adapter initialization failures. The same pass counted 373 test functions across 37 files, checked the documentation against the current repository, and found no SQL-injection concern in the parameterized SQLite paths.

### [An agent context map cuts instruction footprint in half](https://github.com/levibroeksma/dart-analytics/pull/5) — Levi Broeksma
A Claude Fable 5 session reorganized the instruction and architecture material for dart-analytics around a context map that tells each agent which small “context pack” it needs for a task. The root `CLAUDE.md` dropped to about 3.3KB, the combined nested-instruction footprint fell from 23.6KB to 11.3KB, and an 8.8KB database-spec index now points to six layer-specific chapters instead of requiring a 42KB read. Before removing roughly 1.3MB of raw conversation history, the session built a decision ledger and a consistency checker that caught two more stale references on its first run.

### [A paper-trading bot finds six live-market blockers before trading](https://github.com/acantrell0523/polymarket-bot/pull/1) — Austin Cantrell
A long Claude Fable 5 hardening pass on a Polymarket sports bot moved from synthetic tests to real ESPN and Polymarket probing, where it found six ways the bot could silently make wrong decisions. Among them, one-page market discovery missed WNBA games around offset 2,500 and MLB around 3,500; a flat 2% fee model misranked trades; and recreating position state on every scan meant time-based exits and trailing stops never survived a restart. The repaired pipeline found four paper trades across 70 real markets using live prices and DraftKings consensus, while the author kept paper trading enabled and grew the regression suite to 225 tests.

### [Recursive graph reads replace a stale closure table](https://github.com/fiedl/wingolfsplattform/pull/133) — Sebastian Fiedlschuster
In a follow-up to a production graph-data investigation, a Claude Fable 5 session moved the application’s transitive ancestor and descendant readers from a materialized closure table to recursive CTEs over direct links, without changing writes. The work also preserved special-group push semantics, replaced association-shaped call sites with explicit walks, and avoided Rails method/association name collisions uncovered during the migration. Five CI slices were green, and a 6,600-comparison run on a production copy found 28 mismatches (0.4%), all in previously diagnosed stale-closure-row cases rather than the new traversal path.

### [A transit matrix run fixes eleven wrong cells while speeding up the rest](https://github.com/cafein-py/cafein/pull/107) — HTenkanen
This Claude Fable 5-, Opus 4.8-, and GPT-5.5-assisted change speeds Cafein’s door-to-door public-transit matrices through four separately measured changes: expanding-ring street snapping, dense reusable search state, stop-side pointset linking, and a stop-major layout. An independent minute-mark oracle found that the old window sampler disagreed on 11 cells; the repaired sampler matched all 3.3 million cells, while the other changes preserved 2,305,593 reachable cells exactly. The author also added a linking-equivalence test across graph and contraction-hierarchy engines, and reports the full Rust workspace tests, formatting, and `clippy -D warnings` passing.

### [A language-learning podcast survives the phone screen turning off](https://github.com/Darkein/learn-japan/pull/80) — Darkein
Claude Fable 5 co-authored a merged repair for Learn Japan’s PWA podcast player, where recreating `Audio` for every segment and relying on `setTimeout` gaps let Android background throttling silently stop a lesson after the screen locked. The replacement keeps one audio element alive, plays generated silent WAVs for quiz gaps and handoffs, preloads the next TTS segment, and preserves in-place pause/resume and MediaSession controls with lock-screen artwork and seeking. The author reports 441 unit tests, a PWA build, and Playwright coverage for uninterrupted segment chaining, media artwork, pause/resume, and quiz-gap behavior; Android Auto remains explicitly out of scope because a PWA cannot provide its native media service.

### [A gateway routes Claude Code subagents by task and model tier](https://github.com/usejota/agentic-model-gateway/pull/3) — brunotorres-jota
An implementation credited to Claude Fable 5, Opus 4.8, and GPT-5.5 turns the `claudim` Claude Code wrapper into a model-routed delegation layer: sessions receive named delegate agents, premium models can require per-spawn approval, and an opt-in strict mode rejects unapproved agent types without changing ordinary inline work. The branch also adds a Fable override to the gateway’s admin UI and a renameable side-by-side local install, after a live fan-out exposed that workflow calls missing `agentType` silently ran on the expensive session model. Its reported validation includes 1,724 tests, 100 hook cases, isolated renamed-installer checks, a three-agent live local-gateway smoke, and a live admin-API persistence check; the pull request remains a draft.

## 2026-07-12

### [A scene-driven onboarding tour adds six browser smokes](https://github.com/yuubae215/easy-extrude/pull/318) — yuubae215
Claude Fable 5 co-authored Easy Extrude’s desktop onboarding tour, where a pure state machine turns committed scene facts into the next add, select, grab, edit, or extrude prompt instead of tracking presentation history. The implementation preserves state on malformed data, yields no hint for corrupt data, persists only completed or dismissed display state, and leaves the mobile gesture overlay alone. The author reports 598 unit tests, 23 contract checks, six E2E smokes, a live completion walkthrough, and a 1.83KB gzip increase; the public gate and E2E checks are passing while the PR is open.

### [Group-ingestion writes stop retrying in lockstep](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/69946) — José Sequeira
José Sequeira used Claude Code with Fable 5 first to compare PostHog’s group and person batch stores, then to implement fixes for single-row cache misses, reference-equality false writes, and fixed-interval CAS retry storms in ingestion. The 28-file branch adds deep comparison, jittered retries, batched prefetch, and an opt-in `UPDATE … FROM UNNEST` path with an individual-write fallback, while keeping the two larger changes disabled by default for rollout. The author reports 175 targeted tests and a real-Postgres integration case for the JSONB merge semantics; the public pull request remains open.

### [A 1,500-tool-call comparison separates procedure from discovery](https://github.com/fivetaku/fablize) — fivetaku
The author of the Fablize Claude Code plugin compared Fable 5 with Opus 4.8 across 19 controlled A/B runs and 26 working sessions, totaling about 1,500 tool calls. Closed work with an answer—builds, code, and logic—was effectively tied, but Fable more often followed open-ended implications far enough to find extra defects; an injection experiment did not make Opus find those defects on its own. The plugin therefore ships only the measured procedural habits, such as running artifacts and requiring completion evidence, and labels the small single-family study as directional rather than a precise performance estimate.

### [Five frozen runs find no silent omissions in the Fable sample](https://github.com/shortgigsfounder/Research-LLM/pull/5) — shortgigsfounder
A frozen T1 research harness reran the same request five times with Claude Fable 5 at its authorized 16,384-token limit, scoring both the primary instruction and recall of planted violations. Fable returned 1.0 on both measures in all five runs; the author explicitly calls the result “Unknown,” not a clearance, because the sample is small. In the same harness, Grok 4.5 held primary-instruction performance at 1.0 but recalled only 0.5 and 0.3 of the planted violations on two of five otherwise identical runs, which the report classifies as reproduced silent omission.

### [A five-pass extension audit closes a default-deny bypass and crash paths](https://github.com/jtrachtenberg/decant/pull/49) — jtrachtenberg
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored review of Decant’s PDF-conversion browser extension examined its classifier, interception shell, converters, configuration, and transport in five passes. It found that an explicitly empty activation allowlist silently restored the default enabled hosts, a hostile chart point index could request a multi-gigabyte allocation, and document conversion used both quadratic prefix slicing and spread calls that could overflow on very large inputs. The repair keeps an empty allowlist empty, bounds the chart cache, makes the punctuation scan linear, fixes the XLSX width calculation, and adds tests for those and two prompt/interception failure paths; the author reports the full suite green.

### [A Raft trace study separates 97-of-97 replay from full formal correctness](https://github.com/jdubray/SysMoBench-1/pull/5) — jdubray
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored SysMoBench study built a deterministic three-node etcd/Raft trace harness and a 97-window corpus spanning elections, proposals, heartbeats, stale votes, and higher-term re-elections. The corpus regenerated byte-for-byte and a hand-written reference specification replayed all 97 windows, but one-shot Fable derivations were not fully correct: four of five reached 97/97 trace conformance while missing a heartbeat commit clamp caught by bounded open-world exploration. After one round of evaluator feedback and a full re-score, Fable reached four all-pass specifications out of five, compared with two for Opus and none for Sonnet or Haiku; the study also shows why checking only a model’s own repair feedback would have missed Sonnet regressions.

### [A streaming audio rewrite measures both a keylock gain and crossover losses](https://github.com/robmorgan/timestretch-rs/pull/5) — robmorgan
A merged Claude Fable 5 co-authored rewrite of timestretch-rs adds an opt-in three-band streaming engine, a varispeed-first keylock path, and a manifest-driven BPM-accuracy harness. The keylock path reduces the Live-profile pitch-wobble p95 from about 100 cents to 12 and improves its steady quality row from 0.9982 to 0.9997, with a 16-sample tempo-control lookahead; the new multi-resolution path is allocation-free after warmup but introduces 278.6ms or 557.3ms gates in its supported profiles. The author also documents mixed results rather than claiming a blanket win: the multi-resolution engine ties single-vocoder sub-bass similarity at 0.9997, but its 200Hz and 4kHz crossover seams lower broadband scores, and one pre-existing modulation benchmark still fails identically on main.

### [A 63-call App Store review baseline finds wording drift, not missed cases](https://github.com/berkayturk/appstore-precheck/pull/24) — berkayturk
A three-model baseline for an App Store preflight reviewer ran Claude Fable 5 three times over each of 21 labeled cases, with `max_tokens=8192`, always-on thinking, and the dataset hash recorded in the committed manifest. Fable found all 11 planted findings and cleared all 10 clean cases, yielding 1.00 precision, recall, and F1 in this small fixture; 20 of 21 cases were unanimous across repeats. Its one real weakness was interpretation rather than detection: it called a clean release-note example “not applicable” instead of “pass” on all three runs, and split on another clean review-prompt case; the author says the wording clarification belongs in the prompt rather than changing the baseline.

### [Mutation tests make access-control compilers prove they fail closed](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2028) — jeswr / SPARQ
A Claude Fable 5 agent added 370 lines of behavioral tests to SPARQ’s access-control benchmark, covering WAC, ACP, and ODRL compilation and oracle decisions. The five tests make no-match requests deny, check that deny intents never become permissions, and verify that unsatisfied count, temporal, and compound conditions block access; they also include matching allow cases so a trivial “deny everything” implementation fails. The author manually flipped four implementation behaviors—including an ACP oracle that always allows and an ODRL deny-to-permission compiler change—and reports each mutation turned tests red before reversion; the crate test suite reports 132 green tests and clean Clippy.

### [Quiet ADC sampling preserves timer and PWM clocks](https://github.com/laurb9/tiny_scope/pull/38) — laurb9
A Claude Fable 5 session reworked Tiny Scope’s AVR voltmeter and slowest capture mode to take one conversion at a time while the CPU sleeps in idle mode, using the ADC noise canceler without stopping `micros()` accounting or PWM test signals. It leaves the high-speed free-running path unchanged and lets non-AVR targets fall back to their existing implementation; along the way it removed a shadowed ADC mode field and a double-sampling bug in the rolling Vpp calculation. The branch adds 69 lines across six files, keeps Uno globals at 490 bytes, and its public checks show clean builds for Uno, SAMD, ESP32, and three Teensy targets plus a passing 8/8 simulator trace.

### [A deep code review finds UTF-8 panics, stale reads, and a shell bypass](https://github.com/jyjeanne/crustly/pull/37) — jyjeanne
A graph-guided Claude Fable 5 review of the Crustly agent runtime found five byte-index truncations that can panic on multibyte text, a tool-result cache that could return pre-write file data, and loop detection that collapsed distinct `read_file` calls into the same signature. The same pass found context compaction counting summaries twice, retries surfacing the wrong final error, and a shell allowlist bypass through operators such as `&&`, pipes, command substitution, and redirection. The resulting 10-file patch centralizes character-boundary truncation, invalidates cached reads after mutation, and rejects compound shell syntax; Linux and macOS test jobs, Clippy, and formatting are green, while Windows and coverage jobs remain in progress.

### [A fail-closed table distinguishes unavailable ACLs from forbidden requests](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2055) — jeswr / SPARQ
A Claude Fable 5 agent added a public-API integration test that maps a Solid server’s ACL state to HTTP behavior: an unloaded or transient ACL must deny with a retryable 503-class response, while definitive anonymous and authenticated denials become 401 and 403 respectively. The table also asserts that an ACL-discovery link remains present even for an unloaded policy and that no retryable state ever allows access. Three deliberate regressions—including dropping the unloaded state from `is_retryable` and making its decision fail open—made the new tests fail before being reverted; the open PR’s crate and feature-matrix checks are green.

### [Encoded and Unicode `.acl` names enter a fail-closed escalation corpus](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2056) — jeswr / SPARQ
A Claude Fable 5 session built a corpus for the less obvious child names that can shadow a Web Access Control document: percent-encoded dots, full-width and compatibility Unicode dots, trailing dots, and trailing spaces. It verifies that anonymous and non-control principals cannot create these variants against either an existing resource or its container, while an owner control case proves that the denials are not caused by missing resources. The PR reports 18 unit tests and a doctest green, plus mutation checks showing that both a no-op canonicalizer and a naive ACL guard are caught; its public CI and coverage gates are green.

### [Sixty-four randomized datasets probe every named SPARQL leakage channel](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2059) — jeswr / SPARQ
A Claude Fable 5 agent added a deterministic, seed-recorded fuzz battery for SPARQ’s pattern-scoped datasets. For each of 64 generated scope masks, it compares results with a separate oracle store in which hidden N-Quads are physically deleted, covering ordinary selects as well as OPTIONAL, EXISTS, NOT EXISTS, MINUS, aggregates, graph enumeration, and property paths. The test rejects no-op masks and requires at least half the generated cases to hide data; temporary removal of the masking filter exposed a select-all leak, while the pattern-scope feature check and coverage gates are green on the still-open PR.

### [A Solid read path falls back to Turtle instead of failing on an exotic `Accept`](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2060) — jeswr / SPARQ
Claude Fable 5 reworked SPARQ’s content-negotiation seam so JSON-LD profile preferences are parsed deterministically, but a request for an unsupported media type now gets a Turtle representation rather than a 406. An explicit `q=0` refusal is still a 406, and the patch keeps the existing local-only JSON-LD posture by testing that remote contexts are rejected rather than fetched. Fifteen new tests cover profile tie-breaking, wildcard and fallback cases, refusals, and local round trips; the author reports 577 unit tests plus integration suites green in both default and all-feature modes, with the PR’s remaining coverage shards still running.

### [A cold review separates four real receipts from nine unsupported claims](https://github.com/jmill823/deltascanner-ai-pm/pull/14) — jmill823
A frozen Claude Fable 5 cold review of Deltascanner’s “Structural Mistrust” framework found 13 issues, led by a claimed pre-registered evaluation bar with no timestamped receipt, a narrative whose highlighted catches do not join to its ledger rows, and several demonstrations that pass by construction. It also independently checked four cited commits and found their hashes, dates, pull-request numbers, and described changes matched the private repository’s history. The merged exhibit publishes the full findings table with statuses, freezes the source review alongside a SHA-256 file, and deliberately leaves two private-repository hashes unlinked rather than presenting them as publicly inspectable evidence.

### [A transit benchmark measures both routing engines](https://github.com/cafein-py/cafein/pull/113) — Henrikki Tenkanen
Henrikki Tenkanen used Claude Fable 5 alongside GPT-5.5 to add a router switch to Cafein’s grid benchmark, so the existing RAPTOR backend can be compared with TBTR without conflating TBTR’s transfer-set precomputation with matrix time. The new result records carry the selected router and a separate `tbtr_set_seconds` field, while `matrix_seconds` remains matrix-only. Both benchmark commands ran end to end on a Helsinki-region grid with each router and produced identical reachable-cell counts; Black and Flake8 also passed.

### [A TypeScript Playwright launcher is ported to Rust](https://github.com/JustasMonkev/jm-playwright-args/pull/6) — JustasMonkev
Claude Fable 5 ported the `pw-args` library and CLI from TypeScript to Rust, mirroring source and specification files and adding an end-to-end case that drives a real Playwright run through the compiled binary. Review iterations restored the npm-facing launcher, CommonJS and declaration support, signal cleanup, and a pure-JavaScript fallback so a fresh `npx pw-args` install can work without Cargo. The latest revision also adds Windows console-control forwarding and records a successful `cargo check --target x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`.

### [A deletion benchmark catches an incremental-reasoning seam](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2068) — Jesse Wright
A Claude Fable 5 agent added SPARQ’s deterministic deletion-heavy reasoning benchmark, generating LUBM-class synthetic RDF data and checking after randomized insert/delete sequences that incremental closure exactly matches a full rematerialization with no ABox-triggered rebuilds. The work found a real seam in the existing driver: deleting one axiom-typing triple can legitimately rebuild and fail the invariant at a 3,000-unit tier, so the benchmark pins draw-safe sizes and tracks a driver repair separately. Corrupting a pinned closure count and deliberately selecting the known-bad tier each made the run fail before the changes were reverted; small, medium, and large tiers are reported green.

### [A presentation agent gains static and visual deck checks](https://github.com/noamh98/workshop-design-system/pull/5) — noamh98
Claude Fable 5 built a Hebrew-language presentation-agent MVP with an outline-to-build-to-render-check workflow, a static deck validator, and a Playwright visual gate that screenshots slides and flags overflow or overlap. The static validator’s ten checks were calibrated to zero errors across all 17 templates and existing decks. The render check then found two slide-two cards sharing one CSS grid area; moving the second card to the empty third column left both QA gates clean.

### [A Saturn emulator turns BIOS bring-up into deterministic replay tests](https://github.com/codedevmk/Mnemos/pull/296) — codedevmk
Claude Fable 5 co-authored four parity-repair commits in Mnemos’ Sega Saturn bring-up after the implementation exposed timing and state-boundary problems in SH-2 reservations, SCU behavior, VDP state loading, and debugger dispatch. The 46-file branch adds a local-only BIOS checkpoint runner, deterministic power-on and save/load replay coverage, and new SCU, SMPC, VDP1, and VDP2 implementation paths. The author reports 293 passing MSVC Debug tests, three of three final Saturn tests, seven of seven changed-component release checks, and successful repeated checkpoint runs with a pinned US 1.00a BIOS; the visible Windows, Linux, formatting, and governance checks are green.

### [A cron token ceiling ends unattended runs through the normal budget path](https://github.com/alexgunsberg/hermes-agent/pull/5) — alexgunsberg
Claude Fable 5 co-authored a merged Hermes Agent monitor that records API latency, token and context size, and tool runtime in a separate local SQLite database, while storing no request content. The same change adds `cron.max_run_tokens`, with a per-job override, and drains the existing iteration budget when an unattended run crosses its non-cache-read token allowance instead of introducing a separate abort path. Its 14 focused tests cover event recording, malformed-payload failure tolerance, reporting, idempotent installation, and ceiling resolution; the author also reports 824 agent, 659 cron, 1,039 CLI, and 1,741 plugin tests passing apart from pre-existing environment/order flakes.

### [A 20-lane harness measures trust-graph closure without changing the default build](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2093) — Jesse Wright
A Claude Fable 5 agent added a standalone benchmark project for SPARQ’s opt-in certification-edge closure, timing anchor-and-certification combinations while checking admitted and rejected rule counts before each measurement. Its strict-additivity lanes require byte-for-byte identical output when there are no certifications, no permitted depth, or only forged signatures, so an opt-in feature cannot change direct rules merely by being present. The author reports a 20-of-20 smoke run and mutation checks in which both an extra output rule and a one-field tamper made the driver fail; the visible project CI includes passing test, coverage, CodeQL, and `cert-graph` feature jobs.

### [Derived memberships preserve gaps that an old table flattened](https://github.com/fiedl/wingolfsplattform/pull/134) — Sebastian Fiedlschuster
In the semantic core of a migration away from stored indirect memberships, a Claude Fable 5 session made ancestor-group membership a read-time derivation from direct rows. The new layer returns separate validity ranges when someone leaves and rejoins instead of treating the old minimum-to-maximum envelope as continuous membership. On a production copy, 268 of 300 sampled materialized rows agreed exactly; the 32 differences were attributed to zero-length rows, stale envelopes, or one inverted range, and the author says the full suite was re-run after review rounds.

### [A recommendation gate stops trusting one perfect demo fixture](https://github.com/ChelseaKR/queer-the-stacks/pull/32) — ChelseaKR
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored evaluation change replaced a single hand-built recommender fixture whose content and hybrid modes scored 1.0 everywhere with a seeded synthetic-library battery. The new default run compares content, hybrid, and popularity across ten seeds, requires a median MAP@5 uplift of at least 0.5 and no losing seed, and also checks that removing curated lists or shuffling 20% of candidate tags does not improve the content result. A test deliberately zeroes the author-loyalty bonus and confirms that the reduced uplift flips the gate to failure; the public CodeQL, verification, and standards checks are green while the PR remains open.

### [Snap feedback uses a static ring when motion is reduced](https://github.com/yuubae215/easy-extrude/pull/320) — yuubae215
Claude Fable 5 co-authored a merged Easy Extrude interaction change that renders a short snap-engagement ripple only on engage or retarget transitions, leaving hold and release quiet. The implementation derives ring size from the snapped entity’s bounds, caps the flash at 300ms, and substitutes a static ring for reduced-motion users. The author reports 616 unit tests, 23 contract checks, seven E2E tests, and a visual run covering overshoot, settling, disposal, and the reduced-motion state; the visible E2E and project gate jobs passed.

### [Tokio traces gain latency attribution and regression gates](https://github.com/aoprisan/wyrd/pull/5) — aoprisan
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored Wyrd change adds `why-slow`, which splits a Tokio task’s lifetime into its own poll time, waits on resources, timer waits, scheduler lag, and idle time; resource waits name the holder and whether that holder was itself blocked elsewhere. Its companion `diff` compares a baseline recording with a current one using stable task and resource identities, normalizes per instance, and exits 2 for a new deadlock or 1 for poll/wait regressions and newly saturated channels. The merged 16-file patch exposes both queries through the CLI and MCP server, with synthetic trace tests that assert the latency buckets partition the entire task window and JSON-RPC tests for the new tools.

### [Target-frontier pruning keeps Pareto routes bit-identical](https://github.com/cafein-py/cafein/pull/116) — Henrikki Tenkanen
Henrikki Tenkanen used Claude Fable 5 alongside GPT-5.5 on Cafein’s multicriteria transit router, adding target pruning that drops a new label when the destination’s existing frontier already dominates it. The important exception is pinned in a regression test: a later, two-leg route with the same effective time and fare bucket but lower exact grams must survive as the cleaner representative. The author reports exhaustive-oracle tests, fixed relaxed and diverse route sets, and a McTBTR-versus-McRAPTOR comparison all retaining identical journeys; the public lint job is green while the cross-platform test matrix is still running.

### [A WASM feature guard catches a directory-dependent build gap](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2106) — Jesse Wright / SPARQ
A Claude Fable 5 agent found that SPARQ’s `+simd128` Rust flag lived under a crate-local Cargo configuration, so root-invoked builds and sibling WASM crates could silently miss it. The merged repair moves the target-scoped setting to the workspace root and adds a standard-library parser that reads a generated WASM binary’s `target_features` section, with a deliberately absent `relaxed-simd` check proving the guard can fail. The author makes no speed claim—there are no handwritten vector kernels yet—and expects only a small one-time bundle-size ratchet; public CodeQL, fuzz, benchmark, exact-equality, and gate checks passed.

### [Fail-closed time encoders reject ambiguous XSD values](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2104) — Jesse Wright / SPARQ
Claude Fable 5 added opt-in host encoders for SPARQ’s `xsd:dateTime` and `xsd:date` value lanes, mapping accepted values to signed millisecond epochs while keeping distinct per-datatype constants. The new parser rejects untimezoned values, even `+00:00` offsets, leap seconds, `24:00:00`, non-canonical years, surplus or trailing-zero fractional precision, and epoch values that would overflow rather than rounding or wrapping them. Eleven direct tests cover negative epochs, calendar validity, and cross-precision ordering, and a deliberately broken scaling conversion made the relevant tests fail before it was reverted. The author explicitly limits the result to the host half of a future circuit design rather than claiming production soundness; the merged branch’s test, feature-matrix, conformance, CodeQL, and gate jobs passed.

### [A DOCX numbering field stops being a silent no-op](https://github.com/wrzonance/SpecR/pull/469) — thewrz / SpecR
A Claude Fable 5-orchestrated SpecR change fixes a quiet contract problem in DOCX numbering profiles: clients could write a `tier` that disagreed with the actual `ilvl`, but the apply path ignored it. The new API derives the tier from `ilvl` and `articleIlvl`, rejects a contradictory write with a 422 that names the bad entry, and applies the same rule through both REST and MCP schemas. The author’s full-corpus A/B found zero changes across 705 fixtures—as intended, because runtime classification remains level-driven—and reports 1,832 unit tests plus 1,337 of 1,338 integration tests passing, with the remaining failure reproduced on clean main; the public test, typecheck, lint, and CodeRabbit jobs are green.

### [A cultural-heritage pipeline stops swallowing API and write failures](https://github.com/fmadore/iwac-ai-pipelines/pull/4) — fmadore
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored 56-file repair of IWAC’s Omeka-based AI processing pipelines replaces several silent or misleading failure paths with explicit handling. It now raises when model configuration construction would drop a system prompt or temperature, preserves an intentional `0.0` temperature, distinguishes transient Gemini 429s from exhausted quota, puts connection/read timeouts on every Omeka request, and re-raises failed CSV writes. The branch also protects JSON-LD resources without `@value`, adds thread-safe batch rate limiting, and introduces coverage for the shared provider, client, retry, reconciliation, and limiter modules; its public lint-and-test check is green.

### [A dry run rehearses an agent installer without launching an agent](https://github.com/volter-ai/open-autonomy/pull/181) — otto-runhuman
A Fable 5 skeptic review found that exercising Open Autonomy’s real installer had started Termfleet processes and came close to dispatching a coding agent, stopped only by an unrelated failure. The resulting merged `--dry-run` traces the whole install chain but refuses live GitHub probing, skips dependency installs, commits, provider startup, provisioning, and board dispatch, while still making the actual profile and direction decisions. In a disposable repository, the author compared process, tmux, listening-port, and Git status snapshots before and after; all were unchanged, and a later real compile produced exactly the 37 files the rehearsal had predicted. The change adds 168 passing tests across the risky paths, including poison functions for spawn and kill calls, plus end-to-end checks that the repository stays untouched.

### [A SHACL gate stops a broadening certificate from escalating authority](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2125) — Jesse Wright / SPARQ
A Fable 5 adversarial audit uncovered a privilege-escalation path in SPARQ’s certification graph: the authority gate assumed an added structural constraint always narrowed a SHACL shape, even inside non-monotone components such as `sh:not` or `sh:or`. The reproduction gives a `sh:not` case where a certificate accepts an integer-aged node the original anchor rejects, yet the old gate derived authority from it. The merged repair only attempts containment for an allowlisted conjunctive subset and rejects non-monotone, unknown, or custom predicates as potential broadening. Its tests retain semantic validation of the exploit, ensure ordinary conjunctive narrowing still works, and include mutations that make either the exploit guard or the positive case fail.

### [A price-risk radar tests its own forecasts against climatology](https://github.com/aoprisan/Megawat/pull/13) — aoprisan
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored Megawat feature estimates the probability of an expensive or negative electricity-price hour over the next 72 hours, using 120 days of residual-load and price analogues weighted by day type, hour, recency, and a Gaussian kernel. It labels official price curves as facts rather than forecasts, defines “expensive” from the trailing 30-day P90, and exposes effective sample counts instead of presenting a single opaque score. The dashboard also runs a leak-free 14-day walk-forward backtest with Brier scores against climatology and a five-bin reliability table; the PR adds a 902-line Rust risk module, 119 pages of method notes, frontend tests, and a route-level verification check.

### [A Fable advisor fails after any prior tool call](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/76189) — perelin
A Claude Code user reports that selecting Fable 5 as the server-side advisor works in a fresh session but returns `advisor_tool_result_error` with `unavailable` after any earlier tool call, including one `Bash(ls)` or `Read`. Their 113-call field sample recorded 104 failures in sessions with prior tools and nine successes with none; a headless, same-machine repro then isolated the difference, while replacing only the advisor with Opus made the tool-call sequence work. The report covers Claude Code 2.1.201–2.1.206 on macOS and says the behavior reproduced with Opus, Sonnet, and Fable as the main model, making Fable specifically unusable for the post-orientation advisory role it is intended to fill.

### [A Pascal GPU quality fix with no measured throughput cost](https://github.com/spiritbuun/buun-llama-cpp/pull/80) — apollo-mg
Working with Claude Fable 5 on bug discovery, root-cause analysis, benchmark design, and the write-up, apollo-mg found that `llama.cpp`'s FP16 fast path hurts output quality on Pascal GP100 (SM 6.0) without a useful speed gain. The three-line fix routes SM 6.0 through FP32 arithmetic, reducing median KLD on a Qwen3.6-27B Q6_K WikiText-2 panel from 0.002298 to 0.000001 and lifting same-top-token agreement from 96.53% to 99.89%. Across three model classes, prompt processing stayed within noise, while token generation improved 1.4% on the hybrid model; the author says they supervised the work and merged the patch after reproducing the panel on the exact fork.

### [A Hue CLI keeps every failure inside its JSON contract](https://github.com/vyakimov/luxctl/pull/1) — vyakimov
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored hardening pass for the `luxctl` Philips Hue command-line client fixes paths that had leaked raw Python tracebacks: missing resources, malformed JSON input, unreadable input files, flag parsing, unexpected internal exceptions, and non-JSON 401/403 responses. It also adds opt-in certificate pinning with a distinct `tls_verification_failed` result, lets bridge credentials come from environment variables, and makes the CLI report one JSON envelope even for unexpected errors. The merged six-file PR adds a 37-case `unittest` suite plus JSON-only smoke tests, and its CI matrix passed on Python 3.10 through 3.13.

### [A forum client fixes a search parser using live 57,000-page threads](https://github.com/ForumHFR/redface2/issues/894) — XaaT / XaTriX
A Fable 5 investigation of Redface2's intra-topic search replayed the real HFR endpoint anonymously and while logged in, including a 57,000-page thread, and found that search-result forms legitimately omit `firstnum` while carrying a `currentnum` continuation cursor. Treating that field as required meant non-filtered searches could receive a valid matching page but render “no results”; the same live tests showed that the existing `p` pagination parameter does nothing and that server replies truncate at roughly 200 matches. The linked [merged repair](https://github.com/ForumHFR/redface2/pull/896) makes the anchor nullable, replaces a synthetic fixture with sanitized captured responses, adds parser/ViewModel regressions, and passed build, lint, Detekt, and test checks.

### [A language-growth model tests whether short and long checklists can share a scale](https://github.com/dseinternational/vocabulary-growth/pull/159) — frankbuckley
A Claude Fable 5-assisted statistical review of a child vocabulary study revisited its largest modeling caveat: pooling 416-item and 810-item MacArthur-CDI-derived checklists on the 810-item scale. The new Bayesian crosswalk fits the 34 children assessed on both forms, finding the short-form/full-form ratio is near one at the 22–37-month ages where the shorter form is used, while an alternative per-form denominator is ruled out with `P(R < 1.95) = 1.00`. The author reports both outcomes at R-hat 1.00, with model-fit tests passing on Ubuntu and macOS; the conclusion remains deliberately bounded to a small level-dependent residual rather than declaring the forms interchangeable at every age.

### [A workflow dispatcher stops reporting success when no runner started](https://github.com/Automattic/agents-api/pull/420) — chubes4
On a WP Cloud runtime with no reachable local loopback and `DISABLE_WP_CRON=true`, an Action Scheduler workflow executor reported every intended worker as dispatched even when all of its `127.0.0.1:80/443` requests had been refused, leaving jobs pending forever. The author used Claude Fable 5 through an OpenCode subagent fleet for the runtime investigation, fix, and smoke coverage; the dispatcher now returns only the number of accepted loopback requests, allowing its caller to fall back instead of receiving fabricated success. The new pure-PHP smoke test forces two connection failures and expects zero accepted workers, then verifies a three-request mixed run reports exactly two.

### [A transit router caches its multicriteria transfer set without changing journeys](https://github.com/cafein-py/cafein/pull/119) — Henrikki Tenkanen
Henrikki Tenkanen used Claude Fable 5 at xhigh reasoning alongside GPT-5.5 to add a persistent cache for Cafein's dominance-aware multicriteria TBTR transfer set. The cache is keyed by service date and resolved emission factors, survives saved network artifacts, and is invalidated when new trip distances change the geometry-derived emissions used in its reductions. Cached and fresh routers must return exactly equal journey-frontier columns and emissions; the PR also tests date and factor mismatches plus save/load round trips, and reports the full Python and Rust suites, Black, Flake8, Cargo fmt, and Clippy clean.

### [A Claude Code router learns that the Fable advisor is not the main model](https://github.com/KarpelesLab/teamclaude/pull/99) — MagicalTux
TeamClaude's maintainer captured a subtle Claude Code integration failure: a `/advisor fable` request leaves the executor model at the top level and places `claude-fable-5` inside the root `tools` array. Routing only on the top-level field could send an advisor call to an account without Fable headroom, after which upstream disables the advisor for the remainder of the session; it could also silently spend a different account's Fable allocation. The fix scans only direct fields of root `tools` elements, rejects decoys embedded in schemas or conversation text, requires an account to be eligible for both executor and advisor, and falls back to executor-only routing when no account satisfies both. The author reports 257 tests, including chunked parser input, route-pin precedence, quota skipping, and the degraded path.

### [pnpm gets a two-stack release-planning engine](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/pull/12953) — zkochan
A Claude Code session using Fable 5 built the first two phases of pnpm’s native monorepo-versioning work in both the TypeScript CLI and its Rust counterpart. The 63-file, 6,644-line branch adds changeset-compatible change intents, semver-aware release plans, dependency propagation, per-package prerelease lanes, a consumed-intent ledger, and format-preserving workspace updates; its integration tests exercise the real CLIs from recording an intent through lane graduation. The author flags an intentional gap—traditional `pnpm version <bump>` forms remain unported in Rust—rather than presenting the two implementations as identical.

### [A 100-document alignment corpus holds its composition quotas exactly](https://github.com/Mycelium-tools/alignment-data-pipeline/pull/73) — runs-on-vibes
An alignment-data pipeline replaced LLM-invented document categories with a weighted, deck-sampled matrix of about 778,000 combinations, then used Fable 5 for its document-rewrite pass. In the committed 100-document run, the matrix held a 16-language split and 40/40/20 tone allocation exactly; all documents cleared the gate, all 20 deliberately skeptical documents remained accepted, and the audit found no lexical or semantic near-duplicates above its thresholds. The branch also records six recovered tag retries, three judge-JSON retries, and two multilingual evaluation bugs exposed by the run, with raw plans, drafts, rewrites, scores, and audit files committed for inspection.

### [Parallel OWL EL reasoning keeps the sequential output bit-for-bit identical](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2167) — Jesse Wright / SPARQ
A Fable 5 agent added an opt-in bounded-thread mode to SPARQ’s OWL EL classifier without changing the default build. Workers derive rule firings from a fixed round snapshot, then a sequential apply phase preserves the existing link and role-closure machinery; differential tests compare every result and ordering against the single-threaded engine at one, two, and eight threads. The report makes no speed claim: it instead documents ten-run determinism stress tests, a W3C fixture lane, and mutation experiments that exposed a missing rule arm and an original test fixture that had falsely passed.

### [Incremental Datalog maintenance is checked against fresh recomputation](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2183) — Jesse Wright / SPARQ
A Claude Fable 5 agent added an opt-in live materialization API for SPARQ’s stratified Datalog engine, maintaining base facts and per-stratum derivations across inserts, deletes, and batched updates. Its differential battery compares the maintained closure with a fresh evaluation after every step in five program shapes, six random seeds, and 12 update steps each; hand fixtures cover recursive diamonds, negation flips, aggregate thresholds, and ownership transfers. The author deliberately limits the claim to fewer rule-firing candidates on two controlled cases rather than a wall-clock speedup, and says three mutation removals made the corresponding tests fail before the code was restored.

### [A geometry filter is skipped only for index rows certified exact](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2184) — Jesse Wright / SPARQ
A Claude Fable 5 agent added an opt-in GeoSPARQL path that lets a spatial provider certify its candidate set as exact, allowing SPARQ to omit a residual DE-9IM filter only when every surviving binding has that certificate. Unindexed bindings always continue through the geometry filter, so a mixed corpus keeps the original semantics; the new recognition also covers the previously missed constant-region-first `sfContains` form. The PR’s differential tests compare the feature with post-hoc evaluation, and a manually applied mutant that treated partially certified results as complete admitted a known false positive and made two tests fail.

### [Root-mounted static apps stop 404ing every page except their homepage](https://github.com/HarperFast/harper/pull/1769) — heskew
A Claude Fable 5-generated Harper PR traces a production regression in which `static.urlPath: '/'` served the homepage but returned 404 for every subpath after upgrades from 5.0.x to 5.1.18. It normalizes both `/` and an empty mount path into the default middleware chain, avoiding an impossible `//` segment-boundary match and a prefix-stripping edge case. The new incident-shaped integration fixture fails five of seven route checks before the repair and passes all seven afterward, while the existing subpath suite remains seven of seven; the public CI matrix is still running.

### [A deferred Fable advisor seat becomes a typed request instead of a quiet shortfall](https://github.com/ViperJuice/agent-harness/pull/185) — ViperJuice
Agent-harness had treated a Claude/Fable advisor leg as unavailable whenever a non-Claude caller had no controlling terminal, even though its Claude TUI adapter creates its own PTY and can authenticate with Claude Max.
