### [A Fable harness removes half its injected release-note blocks](https://github.com/baekenough/oh-my-customcode/issues/1473) — baekenough
An owner auditing a Fable 5 orchestration harness measured about 49,500 static tokens injected into every session or spawn: 46,483 visible rule tokens plus 3,020 from `CLAUDE.md`. The review found 74 old Claude Code release-note blocks consuming about 8,701 tokens and a corpus growing without any retirement rule. The owner subsequently archived platform-bug history in six rules and hid roster, command-catalog, plugin, and MCP metadata in `CLAUDE.md`, cutting injected release-note blocks from 83 to 41 while retaining the last three releases and passing the project's Sauron and documentation validation.

### [A partial adversarial audit finds 21 reproducible errors](https://github.com/jordanelias/ttrpg/pull/135) — jordanelias
A six-agent Claude Fable 5 adversarial audit examined a 13-commit, roughly 5,400-line observatory and registry branch, finding 21 reproducible problems before the Fable session cap stopped two auditors and prevented independent verification and synthesis. The Opus orchestrator then self-verified the reported findings against the code; this first reconciliation fixes a test whose fixture never exercised its comparison-operator guard and sorts Tarjan-derived cycles so the emitted JSON no longer varies with `PYTHONHASHSEED`. The author reports 65 observatory tests and local gates passing, and the PR's visible checks are green.

### [Human judges expose a prompt rule that made jokes converge](https://github.com/gyu-don/ogiri-ai/pull/16) — gyu-don
The maintainer of a Japanese comedy-answering skill published three human-evaluation rounds comparing Claude Fable 5 High, GPT-5.6-sol High, and Fable with the skill enabled or disabled. A rule distilled from the first round — add concrete traces, numbers, or locations — made the skill-on Fable sets collapse into the same understated shape and lose two of three prompts to bare Fable; demoting it to a conditional repair rule produced two selected answers on the next prompt versus one for the bare model. The new workflow keeps generation and judging separate, uses blind pairwise rankings rather than absolute scores, and treats human judgment as the final decision.

### [A data description stops turning into a one-bullet list](https://github.com/owid/owid-grapher/pull/6753) — Marigold / Our World in Data
With Claude Fable 5 writing and an OWID engineer steering, this Grapher change makes a one-entry `descriptionKey` render as ordinary Markdown prose in indicator metadata and download READMEs, instead of a lone bullet that can mangle multi-line text. It keeps multi-entry descriptions as lists, normalizes the value at the type boundary, and adds tests for single, empty, multiline, and multi-item cases. The public typecheck, test, build, CodeQL, formatting, bundle, and security checks are green.

### [A heartbeat repair keeps a wallbox connected for hours](https://github.com/NIBEGroup/openeebus/pull/47) — kseebauermm
An OpenEEBUS contributor used Claude Code with Fable 5 to restore a disabled announcement for the mandatory Scenario 3 heartbeat in an energy guard. On an Elli Charger Connect 2, the missing announcement had kept the wallbox from subscribing to DeviceDiagnosis and made its SHIP connection drop about five seconds after connecting; with the two entries restored, heartbeats continued at the configured interval and the connection stayed stable for hours. The author reports all 672 `ctest` unit tests passing, alongside a human hardware review and end-to-end limit-write confirmation.

### [A 14-scope refactor keeps its old edge cases](https://github.com/navels/neal/pull/4) — navels
Neal's maintainer used Fable 5 as planner and coder for a structural refactor of validation, provider adapters, orchestration, and an 11,000-line test file, with GPT-5.6 Sol reviewing every one of 14 fresh-context implementation scopes. Review caught behavior the existing suite missed: stateful getter reads changed during a Zod migration, literal types widened, inherited properties slipped through, and a test-file split needed a SHA-256 body-accounting oracle. The suite grew from 1,288 to 1,521 tests, and the public Node 22, 24, and 26 verification jobs pass; a separate generic-agentic/OpenRouter compatibility smoke job currently fails with exit code 1.

### [Studio's sidebar becomes a flat list of sites](https://github.com/Automattic/studio/pull/4191) — bcotrim / Automattic
Automattic's Studio team used Fable 5 under close direction to extract and adapt a 17-file site-centric sidebar from a reference branch, then human-reviewed the result. The new sidebar opens a site's latest chat or a new-chat view, condenses session activity into one priority-ordered indicator, and keeps row actions, drag reordering, and light/dark status colors in the redesigned surface. The contributor reports typechecking, unit tests, and light/dark browser checks completed before review; the visible CodeQL analyses are green.

### [A fixed popover layout targets a repeat Mac crash](https://github.com/repoprompt/repoprompt-ce/pull/548) — baron
RepoPrompt CE used a Claude Fable 5 review to narrow a macOS `EXC_BAD_ACCESS` seen twice in Sentry, across versions 1.0.27 and 1.0.28, to a likely AppKit popover-resize path. Fable’s pre-implementation review treated that attribution as circumstantial but backed a small mitigation: keep the execution-location picker at a fixed, clipped height while its loading, populated, empty, and error content changes. The branch adds a layout-invariant test and a repeated open/close smoke; focused layout tests, build, lint, and preflight passed locally, while the full hosted test matrix was still queued.

### [An overnight Fable workflow loses every scheduler](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77462) — pavel-y-ivanov
A Claude Code desktop user ran a long Fable 5 coordinator session with three independent wake mechanisms: a roughly 25-minute `ScheduleWakeup` loop, an hourly in-session cron job, and persistent monitor tasks. All stopped producing turns for about six hours while the app remained open and detached child processes from the same session still completed, leaving review and merge work stalled until the user manually messaged the session. The report asks for a watchdog or durable wake path, since an agent cannot observe a scheduler outage from inside the inactive session.

### [Dropped songs become playable rhythm-game charts](https://github.com/dgomezpelaez/Rhythmit/pull/7) — dgomezpelaez
Rhythmit merged a Claude Fable 5 co-authored auto-charting feature that decodes a dropped MP3 or OGG, then analyzes it in a Web Worker with STFT spectral flux, adaptive thresholds, peak picking, and autocorrelation-based BPM and beat-phase estimation. It maps bass, mid, and treble onsets to play directions and produces easy, normal, and hard charts subject to spacing, chord, jack, and existing `parseChart` rules; audio and results are cached by SHA-256 in IndexedDB. A synthesized 128 BPM track exercised the whole pipeline headlessly at 128.2 BPM with onset error centered at 0 ms and generated charts that could be fully cleared.

### [A preregistered anomaly layer is killed by its own confirmation run](https://github.com/passiveintent/Palimpsest/pull/4) — purushpsm147
Palimpsest used Claude Fable 5 to preregister an anomaly-detection experiment’s criteria and adversarial predictions before changing implementation, then committed the harness, raw outputs, seeds, and replay commands. Calibration locked the parameters, and the three-seed confirmation run found no cases where the CS layer detected something the substrate method missed or beat by more than 60 seconds: it missed 90 of 90 S4 cases that a 15-second classical cadence caught. Its false alarms did drop from 5,424 per day to 0.33, but the project concluded that the layer was precise about nothing useful and recorded a `KILL` verdict; both visible checks passed.

### [A native Fable lane for read-only code review](https://github.com/Lykhoyda/ask-llm/pull/228) — Lykhoyda
The ask-llm plugin adds `/fable-review`, an isolated read-only review command pinned to `claude-fable-5` instead of whatever model the provider currently defaults to. Its owner reports a live smoke that confirmed the reviewer used Fable, alongside 487 plugin tests, build, lint, documentation, and plugin-validation runs; all seven visible checks are green. The live Fable pass also surfaced a follow-up: the generic read-only test does not require a `tools` array to exist, even though the shipped Fable agent supplies an explicit allowlist.

### [A browser-only investigation of a stray scrollbar](https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jun/11/fable-is-relentlessly-proactive/) — Simon Willison
Simon Willison gave Fable a screenshot and one sentence about a horizontal scrollbar in Datasette Agent, then watched it construct a browser-debugging loop around the live application. The session made scratch HTML cases, found Safari windows through Quartz, captured them with `screencapture`, injected a keyboard event to open the modal, and ran a tiny CORS-enabled server to collect Shadow DOM measurements. Fable then hit a guardrail and handed the transcript to Opus, which tested and verified the eventual two-line CSS repair. AgentsView recorded 68,606 output tokens, a 113,178-token peak context, and an estimated $12.11 for the combined session.

### [Fifty-seven browser demos from roughly 275 subagent runs](https://github.com/elder-plinius/FABLE-SHOWCASE) — elder-plinius
elder-plinius used a single Claude Code prompt to fan out roughly 275 Fable 5 subagent runs over a day, spending the account's weekly allowance on a design, build, enhancement, and QA pipeline. The resulting repository contains 57 creative web demos, from particle-life simulations and physics sandboxes to music tools and procedural worlds. Each demo is a standalone `index.html` with no build step, CDN, framework, or network request, and the gallery uses headless-browser thumbnails embedded as data URIs.

### [An in-place broadcast repair for Reactant StructArrays](https://github.com/EnzymeAD/Reactant.jl/pull/3065) — oschulz
While making PropertyFunctions.jl work with Reactant, oschulz says Fable 5 isolated a failure in struct-returning broadcasts over plain traced arrays: an internal helper allocated a destination type the plain broadcast style could not create. The three-file proposal splits out an in-place `elem_apply_via_while_loop!` helper, lets the StructArrays extension write to its caller-provided destination, and removes an allocation-then-copy pass. Its regression test JIT-compiles a two-column named-tuple broadcast and checks both sum and product fields; the broad CI matrix was still running when the PR was inspected.

### [A token-count fallback stops 502ing for Fable on Bedrock](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/70768) — Chris Volzer / PostHog
PostHog found that its Bedrock account's default data-retention mode rejected `claude-fable-5` even for a count-token request. If Anthropic's first-party count endpoint had a transient error, the fallback then failed too and clients got a 502; AWS's runtime counting endpoint also made a guaranteed-failing round trip for Fable's cross-region model ID. The proposed fix counts Fable payloads under the identically tokenized Opus 4.8 ID, which is allowed in the account's mode, and bypasses the runtime call entirely; the author reports 1,168 gateway tests passing, with the named Semgrep, Python-quality, and LLM-service checks green.

### [Caching CSS selectors yields 2.8x faster matches in axe-core](https://github.com/JustasMonkev/axe-core/pull/1) — Justas Monkev
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored axe-core branch attacks repeated work in its accessibility-engine hot paths: parsed selector expressions are cached for a run, selector cache lookups use `Set`, and several array scans that grew quadratically are replaced with set-based accumulation. It also computes stacking context once per grid node instead of twice, avoiding repeated reads of roughly 15 computed-style properties. The author reports about 2.8x faster `matches()` calls in a microbenchmark; the 19-file change is still open and has no public check rollup yet.

### [IPython defers Jedi startup and bounds debugger caches](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/pull/15310) — Matthias Bussonnier / Carreau
A 24-file Claude Fable 5 co-authored IPython performance pass moves the `jedi` and `parso` imports out of `import IPython` and into first completion, which the author says saves about 50 ms on a warm import. The same branch hoists repeatedly compiled completion and LaTeX regexes, and swaps class-level 1,024-entry debugger caches — which could retain whole frame back-chains for the life of the process — for bounded per-instance caches cleared after each interaction. The open PR includes completion and debugger regression tests; its public matrix is mixed, with several Linux, Windows, and macOS jobs passing while Windows 3.14, macOS 3.13, and a 3.15-dev job currently fail.

### [A Fable diagnosis removes an invalid cross-process JIT optimization](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/62377) — topolarity / JuliaLang
Julia contributor topolarity credits Claude Fable 5 with diagnosing a subtle JIT problem that a Buildkite run had exposed: a static-base-address optimization is no longer valid when generated code is shared across processes. Rather than add another special case, the one-file proposal removes the small optimization for JIT code, matching the project's existing decision to omit it for ahead-of-time code. It is a compact example of Fable being used to trace a runtime-specific regression back to an assumption invalidated by a broader compiler change.

### [A BYOK Fable client switches to adaptive thinking](https://github.com/screenpipe/screenpipe/pull/5187) — EzraEllette
Screenpipe found that its generated BYOK provider entry sent Fable 5 the older `thinking.type=enabled` plus token-budget request shape, which the model rejected with a 400 before chat could begin. The repair updates the paired Pi packages, flags Fable 5 and newer Claude models for adaptive thinking, and maps the selected level to `output_config.effort`; the author exercised the emitted Fable payload and got an accepted request. A focused desktop Rust test could not run because that worktree lacked bundled Tauri resources, but the patch adds configuration regression tests and its Clippy/format and dependency checks are green.

### [A tool-use block disables Fable as an advisor](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/76199) — alexpriest
A Claude Code user built a small matrix around the advisor tool and found that a Fable advisor works on a clean transcript but returns `unavailable` after any earlier `Bash` or `Read` call, even with no MCP servers and only a few hundred tokens of context. The same executor and prompt still work with an Opus advisor; a clean second turn without a prior tool call also succeeds, separating the failure from ordinary continuation. Follow-up runs in the Agent SDK and separate terminal clients reproduced the boundary, while a canary test showed Opus receives the complete tool history — narrowing the issue to Fable’s advisor handling of transcripts containing tool-use blocks rather than a missing transcript or a client-side rollout gate.

### [A microscope configuration audit keeps the safety claims narrow](https://github.com/thomdehoog/ZMART-microscopy/pull/2) — thomdehoog
A Claude Fable 5 adversarial review checked a Leica microscope configuration-loading change against a fixed commit and concluded that malformed limits files could not partially apply and no ungated state was reachable. It still found three major problems — an orientation-load crash on connect, a defaults fallback that could widen an operator-narrowed envelope without warning, and a stale origin docstring — alongside seven minor findings and eight nits. The review and repairs are preserved in the 194-file branch; the regular Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS test jobs are green, while its separate offline jobs remain failing.

### [A student portal prototype begins with a research ledger](https://github.com/gasyoun/Systema-Sanscriticum/pull/514) — gasyoun
For a student-cabinet redesign, gasyoun used two Claude Fable 5 sessions: one for research and interview work, then a resumed verification session after the first hit a limit. The merged deliverable preserves a 178-line evidence ledger, a comparison of six education products, 20 recorded design rulings, and four candidate directions rather than silently selecting a winner. Its first direction is a four-page Russian course-workspace mockup; headless Chrome found no console output or horizontal overflow at 390 px, and seven desktop/mobile light-and-dark screenshots are committed alongside it.

### [A CAN-library audit finds an unfinished transport stack](https://github.com/dborgards/CanKit.Pro/pull/1) — dborgards
A full-repository Claude Fable 5 review of the .NET CAN-bus library examined roughly 245 C# files and 33,600 lines across its core, seven hardware adapters, ISO-TP transport, tests, and CI. The merged report says the core and adapters are production-ready but documents an ISO-TP prototype with a public `NotImplementedException`, inverted CAN/CAN-FD frame builders, malformed flow-control frames, a lost data byte in multi-frame sends, and a scheduler busy loop that is never started. The author turned those findings into a 1,175-line architecture document and a 399-line requirements specification that distinguish existing layers from the proposed protocol-stack work.

### [Three reproduced static-server bugs become a new repair register](https://github.com/italopaesano/koa-classic-server/pull/76) — italopaesano
A Claude Fable 5 full-code review of the 2,993-line `koa-classic-server` implementation reproduced three defects against real requests while 963 tests across 54 suites and ESLint were clean. A non-Latin-1 filename can turn a valid request into a 500, directory sort and pagination links drop `urlPrefix`, and a nonmatching ETag can incorrectly fall through to `If-Modified-Since` and return 304. The new 369-line register also separates a plausible LFU-cache accounting race from nine smaller hardening findings, records exact locations and proposed fixes, and keeps the older 20-item register as closed history.

### [A model-specific gate removes the shared task list](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77500) — Lumos221
A Claude Code user mapped a Fable 5 agent-teams failure across four CLI versions, two accounts, interactive and headless modes, and several competing model families. In fresh interactive Fable 5 sessions, `ToolSearch` could not find `TaskCreate`, `TaskUpdate`, `TaskList`, or `TaskGet`; the same machine exposed and executed them under Haiku, while headless Fable sessions also retained them. Debug-log comparisons showed two different dynamic-tool registries from the same binary, and a named Haiku teammate spawned by a gated Fable lead could still write to the shared task list.

### [A Fable advisor crosses OpenRouter without copying credentials](https://github.com/ZiJinZiMing/Codex-Orchestration/pull/1) — ZiJinZiMing
ZiJinZiMing added a Claude Fable 5 advisor route to a Codex orchestration plugin using Claude Code, CC Switch, and OpenRouter, keeping the OpenRouter credential out of Codex configuration and plugin state. The workflow requires JSON-schema-validated, fail-closed advisor decisions before a Sol or Terra root delegates bounded implementation work to Luna. The author reports 161 unit tests, lint, compilation, lifecycle, packaging, and isolated install checks passing, plus a live Fable route on macOS and a Windows root → Fable → Luna → root smoke whose independent results matched.

### [Seven green tests report zero coverage](https://github.com/coverlet-coverage/coverlet/issues/1984) — tobiwae
Using Claude Fable 5 alongside Opus 4.8, tobiwae traced a .NET Framework 4.8 coverage regression that leaves `coverlet.console` runs green but produces empty reports. A self-contained NUnit repro gets 7/7 tests passing yet visits 0 of 13 sequence points under Coverlet 10.0.1, versus 13/13 under 6.0.4; the report also reproduces the result through the collector path. The investigation force-JITs the injected exit handler to show the missing `DefaultInterpolatedStringHandler` type, explaining why the CLR swallows the unload-time failure and coverage silently disappears.

### [An unprefixed Fable request takes the wrong OAuth route](https://github.com/maximhq/bifrost/issues/5189) — eyeveil
Testing Bifrost's current development build, eyeveil found that a Claude Code OAuth request for `claude-fable-5` can be resolved to another configured provider when the model name is not prefixed with `anthropic/`. The reproduction pairs a keyless Anthropic OAuth provider with a second catalog advertising the same name: the resolver chooses the latter while the Anthropic authorization context remains attached. The issue narrows the cause to the keyless provider being absent from the resolver's candidate catalog and includes a four-step reproduction plus a proposed fix path.

### [A Fable-authored hook keeps a review agent working during fan-out](https://github.com/JPHutchins/code-review/pull/75) — J. P. Hutchins
J. P. Hutchins observed a ten-way Claude Code subagent fan-out freeze its main reviewer at 32% of the wall-clock budget, leaving it without a draft and posting the seed text unchanged. The Claude Fable 5-authored repair rewrites permitted subagent calls to run in the background and refuses a fan-out until the main agent has written its own draft. The author reports 613 CI tests, built-CLI coverage of the draft gate, and live tests on Claude Code 2.1.197 and 2.1.209: launch stubs returned in under a second and the main agent made a tool call 8.9 seconds into a 25-second subagent run.

### [A second student-portal mockup turns design rulings into eight pages](https://github.com/gasyoun/Systema-Sanscriticum/pull/516) — gasyoun
For the second iteration of a Russian student-portal redesign, gasyoun used Claude Fable 5 to carry five explicit stakeholder rulings into an eight-page course-workspace prototype rather than open another design direction. The 24-file branch adds course, library, calendar, progress, billing, and help surfaces, along with hash-addressable tabs, a persistent theme toggle, and retained fold-state without external requests. Headless Chrome found no console messages or 390-pixel horizontal overflow across all eight pages after one `min-width:0` correction; eleven desktop/mobile light-and-dark screenshots and the decision record are committed, while the PR remains deliberately unmerged pending review.

### [A reading-study mediation decision becomes a repeatable graph check](https://github.com/dseinternational/language-reading-predictors/pull/331) — Frank Buckley
A Claude Code/Fable 5 run settled a disputed adjustment-set question across 13 mediation models in a language-reading study by adding a time-unrolled DAG derivation and a NetworkX script. It found that keeping baseline `E` and `R` variables did not alter backdoor blocking, while each natural-effect model still has a treatment-induced mediator–outcome confounder that prevents identification; no model was refit and the headline results stay unchanged. The branch adds 40 parameterized guards over the live specifications, reports all 479 Python tests passing, and has green macOS, Linux, lint, and spellcheck checks.

### [A second reviewer catches three gesture-state mismatches before merge](https://github.com/ForumHFR/redface2/pull/941) — XaaT
XaaT asked Claude Fable 5 to implement a multi-touch cancellation path for an Android forum app’s page swipe, after first using GPT-5.6 Sol to specify the state machine. Sol’s first review rejected the change for three semantic mismatches with the existing gesture handler, including captured vertical deltas and a misclassified consumed-up event; Fable’s follow-up applied those corrections and the second gate approved it. The merged two-file repair adds six Compose JVM tests that deliberately omit the magnifier tree to avoid a false pass from event starvation, and its build, lint, static analysis, assembly, test, and repository-guard checks are green.

### [A Fable-led subagent loses its wake-up after starting a remote job](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77578) — AnMakc
A Claude Code 2.1.209 user ran Fable 5 as a background coordinator and found that a subagent could start a tracked background SSH waiter, then be marked completed while that waiter was still alive. The author reproduced the lost wake-up twice with child lifetimes of 91 seconds and about five minutes, plus two earlier 9- and 14-minute cases: each child wrote its expected output, but never re-invoked its subagent. Identical waiters attached directly to the Fable main session woke reliably, narrowing the failure to background children of subagents; the current workaround duplicates every watcher at the coordinator and manually resumes the dormant worker.

### [Fable’s desktop reasoning panel stays empty despite the documented setting](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77460) — r99-creator
A macOS Claude Desktop user inspected local Claude Code transcripts after finding that Fable’s expandable “Thought process” panels never showed any text. In a same-session A/B run, all 23 Fable 5 thinking blocks were empty while 17 Haiku subagent blocks contained plaintext; the author’s broader scan found 331 empty Fable blocks and zero populated ones. Setting `showThinkingSummaries: true` before the tests did not change the result, and the report traces the difference to Fable’s `display: "omitted"` default rather than a merely cosmetic UI failure.

### [A Fable challenge becomes a preflight check for multi-model advice](https://github.com/9uiLe/plugins/pull/58) — 9uiLe
While dogfooding a “Decision Council” workflow, 9uiLe checked the requested Fable 5 terminal configuration before dispatch and incorporated Fable’s objections to treating configuration as proof, leaving token ceilings unset, and accepting weak authorization records. The resulting guard requires separate requested and runtime-effective model and effort fields, finite round/retry/fallback and token bounds, and explicit approval before budget excess can proceed. Its tests deliberately block silent fallbacks and model or effort mismatches, while allowing a low-stakes run to proceed only as explicitly degraded when runtime metadata is unavailable; the visible verification check passed.

## 2026-07-15

### [A model switch leaves commit credits on Fable](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77612) — rec3141
A Claude Code 2.1.207 user began a session on Fable 5, switched it to Opus 4.8, and confirmed through `/model` that the active model had changed. The model's injected self-identity string nevertheless remained Fable for the rest of the session, so it rejected the correct status reading as inconsistent and stamped subsequent Git commits with `Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5`. The report separates the stale model-visible context from `/model`, which continued to show the right active model.

### [LiteLLM consumes Fable's fallback request before Anthropic sees it](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/issues/33294) — wwwillchen
A LiteLLM 1.89.2 user tested Anthropic's server-side fallback on Fable 5 with an Opus 4.8 fallback and a documented beta header. When the fallback used Anthropic's bare model name, LiteLLM returned a 400 model-authorization error; when it used LiteLLM's configured alias, the request streamed Fable's cyber refusal unchanged and never called Opus. The issue includes the proxy configuration, curl reproduction, and streamed events, tracing the conflict to LiteLLM treating Anthropic's `fallbacks` field as its own router configuration.

### [A context-window tag silently swallows Fable's requested effort](https://github.com/VikashLoomba/agentprism-workflows/issues/147) — VikashLoomba
A live AgentPrism run requested `claude-fable-5[high]`, but the recorded model was `claude-fable-5[1m]` at default effort. The model matcher reasonably selected the 1-million-token variant, then incorrectly treated any bracket as proof that the requested effort had already been supplied, so it neither set `reasoning_effort: high` nor emitted its normal fallback record. The author proposes recognizing only brackets that actually contain the requested effort token, preserving both the intended call and audit signal.

### [Microsoft Graph connections fixed after an end-to-end pass](https://github.com/bagofwords1/bagofwords/pull/665) — yochze
Driving OneDrive, SharePoint, and Outlook through bagofwords’ UI exposed two broken connection paths: the credential test stripped arguments from clients that accept `**kwargs`, and Outlook’s per-user OAuth route was simply missing its connection type and `Mail.Read` scope. A Claude Fable 5 co-authored repair adds the variadic-client guard, the missing OAuth wiring, a regression test, and a feedback-loop document with UI screenshots plus a real-completion run against a stubbed Graph boundary. The PR was merged with the data-source integration, LLM integration, and Playwright checks green; its two database E2E jobs were still running when inspected.

### [A two-connection test closes a financial reconciliation race](https://github.com/Mazikorn/Coreone-Procurement-Sales-and-Inventory-PSI-Management-System/pull/189) — Mazikorn
Claude Code running Fable 5 fixed a reconciliation race in which another connection could close a hospital month after a recalculation had passed its first check, allowing the recalculation to overwrite finalized differences and pending collections. The repair rechecks closure status after SQLite’s `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` lock, and uses a real second `DatabaseSync` connection to pin both the old silent overwrite and a competing-row `UNIQUE` failure. The author reports four deterministic race tests, including a deliberately broken variant that fails, plus 84 reconciliation tests, 1,414 backend tests, and TypeScript checking; the public vitest, E2E, gate, secret-scan, and AI-review checks are green, while the PR remains open.

### [An agent tool makes unsupported mutations and remote exports fail closed](https://github.com/samibel/graphi/pull/51) — samibel
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored hardening pass for the Graphi code agent closes three paths outside its stable scope: extract/move refactors now reject before reading the code graph, nine Labs HTTP routes return 403 unless an operator opts in, and remote callers cannot make the server write exported memory to a path they choose. The tests assert zero planned work and byte-identical source/graph state for rejected refactors, preserve the registered route surface while blocking handlers, and move file writes to the local CLI with mode 0600. The 108-file PR reports its Go test gate, formatting, vet, layer guard, coverage check, and full-flow smoke green; the public CI rollup also shows green egress-tripwire, release-binary, benchmark, and correctness jobs, with several broader jobs still in progress.

### [Mid-turn Fable narration disappears from Claude Code resumes](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77651) — kk1987
A Claude Code 2.1.210 user traced a Fable 5 transcript-loss bug to responses with interleaved thinking: narration emitted between tool batches was neither rendered nor saved to the local `.jsonl`, so Ctrl+O and `--resume` could not recover it. In an approximately one-hour session, the user saw about 30 mid-turn text blocks but found only 11 persisted records; a roughly 1,800-character analysis immediately before `AskUserQuestion` vanished entirely. Their structural dump shows dropped blocks following two near-adjacent thinking records, while comparable turns with one thinking record survived, and the issue includes shell and `jq` reproduction steps.

### [Four bad imports no longer force a 15,000-page rescan](https://github.com/MyNameIsTaeYeong/project-context-loop-system/pull/97) — MyNameIsTaeYeong
A Fable 5 co-authored repair changes a Confluence incremental-sync failure mode in which even one import error froze the watermark, causing every later cycle to refetch all 15,000 pages; the author had four permanently failing pages. Failed page IDs now go into a retry table and are force-fetched on the next run regardless of the changed-page query, allowing the watermark to advance without discarding missed updates. The patch keeps conservative fallbacks — retain the watermark if retry storage fails, revert to a full fetch if retry lookup fails, and do not erase deferred pages from a truncated enumeration — with regression coverage for the changed behavior.

### [Claude subscription OAuth kept outside an agent sandbox](https://github.com/NVIDIA/OpenShell/pull/2285) — cjfit
NVIDIA OpenShell contributor cjfit says Claude Fable 5 helped prepare this human-prompted and human-reviewed provider for running Claude Code in a sandbox through an Anthropic Pro or Max subscription instead of an API key. The gateway collects and refreshes login material on the host, keeps the real OAuth token out of sandbox environment variables, filesystems, and logs, and injects it only at the `inference.local` egress boundary; sandboxed clients receive a placeholder token and the real base URL. The author reports unit suites for provider setup, static-config resolution, non-injection, and refresh behavior, plus a manual golden path in which bare `claude` authenticated in a newly created sandbox without prompts; the PR's visible DCO and required-status gates are green.

### [Four founder tasks, a Fable sweep, and a useful challenger](https://superframeworks.com/articles/fable-5-vs-gpt-56-sol-founder-benchmark) — Ayush Chaturvedi
Superframeworks ran Claude Fable 5 through Claude Code against GPT-5.6 Sol on product scoping, customer-interview synthesis, support-and-churn analysis, and pricing. The team preserved raw candidates, generated scorecards, and re-judged cleaned outputs; Fable won all four, averaging 53.8/60 against Sol’s 47.2, with eight final model calls taking 10 minutes 38 seconds. Their task notes credit Fable with a tighter pricing model and more executable operating decisions, while Sol produced useful technical details and objections; the authors explicitly limit the result to this four-task founder benchmark.

### [A transcript meter misses most delegated Claude usage](https://github.com/jewei/claude-meter/pull/19) — jewei
While checking Claude Code transcripts on their own machine, jewei found that Claude Meter scanned only parent-session JSONL files, missing the subagent transcripts that hold delegated work; context-fork transcripts would have caused the opposite error by replaying parent history. The repaired scanner counted 11,101 messages instead of 4,655, and the author’s seven-day estimate rose from $153.85 to $696.92 after including subagents, one-hour cache writes, and Fable 5’s correct offline fallback rate rather than Sonnet’s. The patch adds seven focused tests, and the author reports 270 core tests passing.

### [A React cache subscriber stops updating a sibling during render](https://github.com/HarperFast/studio/pull/1510) — Kyle Bernhardy
During a runtime-verification pass over Studio’s analytics UI, Kyle Bernhardy found that a React Query cache callback could call `setState` while a sibling component was registering a fresh query, producing React’s “Cannot update a component while rendering a different component” warning. The Fable 5-attributed repair replaces the listener with `useSyncExternalStore`, defers notifications through React Query’s `notifyManager`, and adds a regression test designed to fail both with the old state setter and with a bare external-store implementation. The author reports the same browser run returning zero console errors, and the visible PR verification checks are green.

### [A Sanskrit grammar gets a 64-claim evidence register](https://github.com/gasyoun/SanskritGrammar/pull/186) — gasyoun
A Claude Fable 5 session ran six parallel readers through all 48 lessons of Bühler’s 1923 Sanskrit grammar, harvesting 404 falsifiable assertions and promoting 64 into a checked register: 58 true, four overstated, one false, and one untestable. The work pairs the claims with corpus-derived frequency checks, including a 56.8% seṭ-future figure and a finding that the perfect is more frequent than the imperfect in the cited corpus, contrary to the book’s stated direction. A concurrent five-claim Sonnet pass was reconciled explicitly rather than discarded, and the merged branch’s Python tests and Docusaurus build are green.

### [A Fable debugging pass widens a SciPy solver test skip](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/25632) — Ralf Gommers
SciPy maintainer Ralf Gommers used Claude Fable 5 while reproducing and monitoring a failure in the single-precision TFQMR solver test. On an ill-conditioned matrix with a condition number around 4,000, the residual stalled near `2e-2`; platform rounding put it just under the tolerance on x86-64 but over it on i686, Linux AArch64, ASan, and WASM. The proposed repair widens the skip from 32-bit platforms to all platforms; initial build-only, package-build, and non-default-interpreter checks are green while the larger matrix is still running.

### [A Pali translation bake-off uses Fable as its referee](https://github.com/chercherai/buddhomics/issues/2) — chercherai
To choose a machine translator for 13.1 million untranslated Pali characters, chercherai ran 12 difficult passages — 330 segments spanning verse, dialectic, logic, and conditional formulas — through six models, scoring them against Claude Fable 5 translations made directly from the Pali before it saw the candidates. Opus 4.8 led the comparison with two slips, while Fable caught both; the later GPT round also caused the author to revise two of Fable’s own referee calls, including an attested reading of *sattussada*. The report recommends Fable 5 through the Batch API for a consistent full-corpus register, with Opus 4.8 as a fallback for classifier refusals, and estimates the discounted job at about $190. Scripts for passage selection, model runs, and the annotated comparison report are committed, although the raw outputs remain local and gitignored.

### [A GPU simulation meets its budgets — and records the renderer hang](https://github.com/qwasg/Rurix/pull/135) — qwasg
A Fable-assisted Rurix milestone ran a 131,072-particle GPU simulation through three measured local budgets on an RTX 4070 Ti: 11.461ms per SPH step, 0.1157 seconds per 1280×720 offline frame, and 14.8334ms per frame across a 600-frame real D3D12 window. The branch includes the individual trials, aggregate JSON, clock settings, and an executable final-frame sampler that checked sky and water pixels after the live-window run. It also records a negative result rather than hiding it: the intended 256-samples-per-pixel, four-bounce offline renderer hangs on suspected PTX divergence/reconvergence paths, so the checked production slice is temporarily locked to 32 samples and two bounces pending a tracked toolchain fix.

### [A Sanskrit grammar register finishes 68 pending checks](https://github.com/gasyoun/SanskritGrammar/pull/201) — gasyoun
Claude Fable 5 completed the 68-item backlog for a verification register built around Zalizniak’s 1978 Sanskrit grammar, taking it from six seed claims to 74 adjudicated entries. The resulting fact-axis tally is 57 true, zero overstated, zero false, and 17 untestable claims; the last group names missing instruments such as period-tagged corpus slices, a treebank, compound typing, and a morphoclass crosswalk instead of being silently discarded. The merged branch adds reproducible DCS corpus metrics, including a 3.4-million-token final-consonant census, preserves two failed proxy experiments with their numbers, and has green Python-test and documentation-build checks.

### [An eight-surface admin audit finds eighteen high-severity problems](https://github.com/netdust/stridelms/pull/9) — netdust
A Claude Fable 5 session traced eight Stride LMS admin-workspace surfaces, their REST endpoints, and exports, producing a production-readiness review with 18 high-severity, 30 medium-severity, and roughly 22 low-severity findings. The report organizes those findings under six root causes and a four-phase remediation plan; five parallel end-to-end traces supplied the source evidence, and the highest-severity singleton findings were independently rechecked. It also distinguishes a stale roadmap item already fixed in the tree from an unresolved anonymous-lead search problem, rather than presenting the audit as a flat bug list.

### [Fable adds a population-peak status to UN projections](https://github.com/owid/etl/pull/6442) — edomt
Our World in Data contributor edomt says Claude Fable 5 wrote a new UN World Population Prospects table that labels every country-year from 1950 to 2100 as before, at, or after its population peak. The implementation combines observed estimates through 2023 with medium projections thereafter, marks cases still growing in 2100 as not yet peaked, and includes assertions for one row per country-year, full coverage, and nonmissing populations. Its spot checks put China’s peak in 2021, Japan’s in 2009, Bulgaria’s in 1988, and India’s projected peak in 2061; the branch’s build-and-deploy and security checks passed, while the chart-diff gate is awaiting review of the new chart.

### [A scheduled Fable loop idles after saying it will continue](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77727) — Shawna Cason
In a local design-and-build session, Shawna Cason ran Claude Code’s dynamic `/loop` with Fable 5 for 1,250 assistant messages and no other model in the transcript. After a capitalized instruction not to stop, Fable replied that the loop was armed and continuing, but the session was idle seven minutes later; Cason says the same instruction had to be repeated three times in about an hour. The report identifies the model’s own explanation — an overly long wake-up interval — and notes a similar incident on Opus 4.8, so it points to the loop/wakeup harness as a possible contributor rather than treating the result as a model-only failure.

### [Completed background agents lose their resumable transcripts](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77730) — Shawna Cason
In a separate Fable 5-led HTML/SVG design workflow, Cason reports that at least five background agents became unresumable minutes after completing, returning “No transcript found for agent ID.” Each replacement had to reread state handoffs, 15-plus reference images, and prior render proofs; Cason estimates 30,000–60,000 tokens of reinitialization per respawn, after one long-lived agent had accumulated more than 450,000 tokens. A queued message to a nearly finished agent was also never delivered, so one round contradicted three user design decisions and required another correction pass; the issue supplies the failure sequence and a reproduction shape involving heavy tool use.

### [Observational memory reaches Mastra’s Convex adapter](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/pull/19474) — ediskandarov
A Claude Code run using Fable 5 added Observational Memory to Mastra’s Convex storage adapter: a new indexed table, 18 client operations, atomic server-side read-modify-write flows, JSON serialization for reserved keys, and a migration path for existing deployments. The author says the model wrote the design, implementation, and tests while a human drove requirements and reviewed the approach; a live self-hosted Convex deployment passed the 76-test shared memory kit, and the package’s offline suite reported 258 passing tests. The 19-file feature was merged after four commits.

### [Source-qualified schema references keep a wiki’s local format intact](https://github.com/ProfessionalWiki/NeoWiki/pull/1044) — malberts
For NeoWiki’s planned multi-source schema work, a Fable 5 design-and-review pass chose a distinct reference value object and JSON objects for foreign references, avoiding an ambiguous `source:Name` string format while preserving byte-identical bare strings for local data. The resulting 49-file branch routes unresolvable sources through existing null handling across validators, actions, RDF projection, and Neo4j updates; its tests probe accessor ordering, seeded-data byte identity, and every current ingress for a deliberately deferred canonicalization case. Fable handled design and review while an Opus 4.8 subagent implemented the code; the author says human diff review remains pending, but all 12 visible PHP, lint, build, and test checks are green.

### [A Factory dashboard measures throughput, cycle time, and stalled work](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/pull/19483) — abhiaiyer91 / Mastra
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored Mastra branch adds a Factory metrics page from the work-item and stage-history data already in the system: gap-filled throughput, median and p90 cycle time, per-stage dwell, work in progress, aging items, source mix, and human-versus-agent transitions. It also fixes a regression that discarded worktree scope when opening a run session, leaving work without a thread and redirecting the UI to `/new`, and replaces one hardcoded board action with stage-aware choices such as Investigate, Build, and Review. The author reports 454 unit, 89 node, and 415 MSW tests green, plus clean type and formatting checks, across the 21-file change.

### [A social-content pipeline replaces unattended posting with a reviewable outbox](https://github.com/AI7891/IRIS_Headless_Engine/pull/7) — AI7891
This Fable 5 co-authored IRIS change retires its automated Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, and YouTube posting path in favor of a human-confirmed outbox, explicitly citing platform-account and long-lived OAuth risk. It creates per-platform packages with size, caption, title, hashtag, and UTM-link rules; records their Pending, Exported, Posted, or Skipped status in SQLite; can export them to Google Drive; and leaves the old provider path disabled by default behind a feature flag. The 20-file branch adds package-builder, service, formatter, and SQLite regression tests, while its visible build-and-test check passed.

### [Tuple broadcasts work again over traced Julia arrays](https://github.com/EnzymeAD/Reactant.jl/pull/3067) — oschulz
Reactant contributor oschulz credits a Fable 5 Max diagnosis with fixing a `MethodError` that appeared when a function returning a tuple or struct was broadcast over a traced array. The three-file patch adds the missing StructArrays allocation hook, keeps unrelated `similar` failures intact, gives users a hint when StructArrays is not loaded, and adds tuple and named-tuple integration tests; the resulting broadcast produces a `StructVector` and lowers `sin.(a), cos.(a)` to whole-tensor StableHLO operations. The public matrix already has successful Linux, Windows, ARM, and TPU core/integration jobs, but several macOS jobs remain queued and a few broader jobs are failing, so this is an open repair rather than a completed merge.

### [A Fable reviewer finds two gaps in a five-file provenance fix](https://github.com/kuhlman-labs/fishhawk/pull/1946) — fishhawk-dev
Fishhawk asked Claude Fable 5 to review a change that explains why a declared review scope can differ from the staged diff without being a real drift. Fable approved with two low-severity verification findings: the claimed byte-identical legacy render was checked only through marker strings, and several path assertions could silently pass if a future refactor omitted the paths entirely. The run otherwise reports a green verification gate and a five-file declared/staged match, making the review useful as a concrete example of an agent flagging test-strength rather than product-behavior problems.

### [A 30-agent workflow gives Fable its own frontier tier](https://github.com/hatch3r/hatch3r/pull/131) — Denis Masatovic
Denis Masatovic’s Hatch3r release splits a multi-agent setup into four model classes and makes reasoning effort an explicit per-agent setting, reserving Claude Fable 5 for 16 verdict and sign-off roles at xhigh or max effort. The 89-file change maps that tier across Claude Code, Cursor, and Copilot, preserves legacy configuration names, and adds durable overrides plus provenance warnings for hand-edited model settings. The author reports more than 1,100 targeted tests and a three-adapter scratch-repository check with 12 of 12 expected emissions; the public bundle, DCO, and supply-chain gates have passed while the broader build matrix is still running.

### [A model-aware effort clamp keeps Fable requests at max](https://github.com/microsoft/amplifier-module-provider-anthropic/pull/71) — Michael J. Jabbour
Michael J. Jabbour traced an Anthropic-provider failure in which a globally valid `max` reasoning-effort request could be unsupported by the active model, leading the provider to drop the effort field and let the API silently choose a default. The repair clamps recognized requests to the highest level a model supports, while retaining `max` for Claude Fable 5 and Opus 4.8; a mocked payload run showed Sonnet 5 changing from `max` to `xhigh`, Fable 5 remaining at `max`, and unknown values still being omitted with a warning. The PR adds eight focused clamp/logging cases and reports 535 passing tests, alongside three streaming-test failures reproduced on a clean base checkout and left unchanged.

### [Nine hundred seventeen agent tasks show a narrow Fable lead](https://tessl.io/blog/claude-fable-5-vs-opus-48-the-mythos-hype-meets-reality/) — Nicolas Fortuin and Baptiste Fernandez
Tessl ran Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 over 917 shared agent-skill scenarios, with and without the relevant public skill supplied. With skills, Fable scored 92.9 against 92.0 and used fewer output tokens, but its average task cost was $1.25 against $0.74; the relevant skill lifted both models by about 17 points, versus Fable's 0.9-point model advantage. Fable also refused 26 tasks that Opus completed, so those requests sit outside the shared-score comparison rather than being hidden in its headline result.

### [LLVM cycle objects shrink from 128 to 80 bytes](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/209666) — MaskRay
LLVM contributor MaskRay credits Claude Fable 5 with aiding a compact refactor of `GenericCycle`: cycles receive preorder IDs and their exit-block caches move into `GenericCycleInfo`, eliminating the per-object cache and its repeated invalidation. The 31-line change makes the cache lazy and indexed by cycle ID while reducing `sizeof(GenericCycle)` from 128 to 80 bytes. An LLVM reviewer approved the open PR, and its nine visible completed checks are green.

### [JupyterLab gets renameable code-console tabs](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/pull/19156) — jtpio
JupyterLab contributor jtpio marked Claude Fable 5 as an AI model used for this feature and says the code was carefully reviewed and run by a human. The five-file patch adds a `console:rename` command, a tab-context-menu entry, user documentation, and a Galata browser test that renames “Console 1” to “Custom Name.” All 29 completed visible checks are green while the PR remains open.

### [A SONiC migration keeps old monitoring names while changing the collector](https://github.com/metal-stack/metal-roles/pull/670) — Sven-Ric
With the existing SONiC exporter deprecated, Sven-Ric used Claude Fable 5 to compare community and enterprise gNMI implementations, then manually confirmed the resulting deployment design. The 20-file migration runs gNMIc as a systemd-managed container, maps its telemetry into the existing Prometheus naming scheme, and adds a separate `vtysh`-based BGP collector because this deployment’s direct FRR configuration is not visible through gNMI or Redis. The open branch’s build, ansible-lint, spelling, markdown-lint, and OCI artifact checks are green.

### [IPLIKE filters move onto PostgreSQL `inet` indexes](https://github.com/OpenNMS/opennms/pull/8634) — Marshall Massengill / OpenNMS
Marshall Massengill credits Claude Fable 5 with assisting a 21-file OpenNMS rewrite of IP-address filtering, translating eligible `IPLIKE` patterns into native PostgreSQL `inet` equality or range predicates while retaining the existing stored-function fallback for cases such as IPv6 zone IDs. The change adds a 351-line translator, safe-cast expression indexes, a reference-pattern corpus, migration handling, and unit, database, JDBC, Hibernate, REST, and web-filter coverage so the fast path has to match legacy semantics. The PR points readers to benchmark results in its linked issue; separately visible evidence shows its five named GitHub checks successful and maintainer Dino Tognini approving the open branch.

### [A Bedrock fallback turns a Fable outage into a second failure](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/71189) — Lucas Ricoy
During a live Anthropic 429 burst, PostHog's gateway saw every `claude-fable-5` request fail again when it fell back to Bedrock: Prometheus isolated the rate at 100%, and Loki showed the same 400 about Bedrock's default data-retention mode. Ricoy traced that mode to an account-level opt-in rather than a request setting, so the proposed stopgap preserves the original Anthropic error and refuses the doomed Bedrock route until the team deliberately accepts the provider-sharing setting. The three-file repair adds two incident-shaped regression tests; Ricoy reports 133 gateway tests passing.

### [A Fable planner is denied every tool, including session reads](https://github.com/omnigent-ai/omnigent/pull/2637) — Charlie-McDaddy
Omnigent added an example Claude Fable 5 agent for turning a supplied brief into a prose plan or reviewing a supplied diff against a supplied contract, while mechanically withholding the usual means to inspect or change anything. Its bundle omits OS, terminal, MCP, local, built-in, skill, subagent, async, spawn, and timer capabilities, and a zero-call guard blocks even platform session-read helpers. The new structural E2E test loads the bundle through the production parser and checks the model, empty capability surface, prompt limits, and deny-all policy; the author reports five focused E2E tests passing and the visible repository matrix is green.

### [Six thousand lines of map-game code removed without disturbing sharing](https://github.com/gitjanerik/svg-insights/pull/455) — gitjanerik
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored cleanup removes CurveBall's tournament flow, HUD, layer, flippers, sound composable, and roughly 6,200 lines of game-specific map code from svg-insights. The remaining map picker keeps ordinary `shareInvite` behavior, while the retained DEM sampler is separated from game state because terrain and elevation profiles still depend on it. The author reports 1,145 tests green and a successful production build for the ten-file change.

### [An adversarial data review catches 6,813 silent policy-data changes](https://github.com/owid/etl/pull/6454) — paarriagadap / Our World in Data
Pablo Arriagada used Claude Fable 5 for an adversarial review while updating Our World in Data’s LGBTI National Policy Dataset to its producer’s June revision. The review found 6,813 changed cells among roughly 370,000 proportions, including earlier country codes that conflicted with documented gender-marker policy; it also caught a new Mexico/Venezuela incitement-to-hatred misclassification and adds a guarded local correction until the source fixes it. The open nine-file update has successful build-and-deploy and security checks, while its chart-diff job remains for review.

### [PlayerData makes its dirty-state path lock-free](https://github.com/dreamingdog0529/PlayerData/pull/1) — dreamingdog0529
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored PlayerData change replaces a versioned-list snapshot cache with copy-on-add arrays and `Volatile.Read` for the frequent dirty-state and participant-read paths. The author’s short benchmark reports no-op commits dropping from 50.1 to 30.1ns, unsuppressed document updates from 70.5 to 48.4ns, and collection upserts from 142.8 to 117.3ns, while dirty commits stayed within measurement noise. The five-file patch also parallelizes serialization and deserialization only when multiple dirty documents are involved, and it has been merged.

### [Studio Code can be redirected while it is still working](https://github.com/Automattic/studio/pull/4216) — draganescu / Automattic
Automattic’s draganescu says Claude Fable 5 authored the design, implementation, and tests for adding mid-turn steering to Studio Code: a typed message reaches a live run before its next model step, while a late message safely falls back to the next turn instead of disappearing. The change records those interventions in the session log and returns an explicit delivered-or-not result across the CLI and desktop message channel. Companion Fable-authored work adds [prompt rules](https://github.com/Automattic/studio/pull/4214) to diagnose before changing files, preserve manual edits, and resume after compaction, plus [mandatory skill-loading rules](https://github.com/Automattic/studio/pull/4215) for matching workflows; each PR says it was human-reviewed before opening and its visible CodeQL analysis checks passed.

### [Three documentation-build failure modes get regression coverage](https://github.com/NVIDIA/sphinx-llm/pull/104) — kartben / NVIDIA
NVIDIA's `sphinx-llm` maintainer marked three related fixes as Fable-assisted. The primary PR prevents a failed Sphinx build from launching a pointless sequential Markdown sub-build and terminates an already-running parallel child, with mocked-process tests for both cases. A [second PR](https://github.com/NVIDIA/sphinx-llm/pull/105) adds `llms_txt_exclude`, keeping individually generated Markdown pages while omitting matching documents from the aggregate `llms.txt` and `llms-full.txt` outputs, tested under both HTML and directory-HTML builders. The [third](https://github.com/NVIDIA/sphinx-llm/pull/106) passes an explicitly separate `conf.py` directory to the Markdown build and verifies that a relocated configuration still produces all three expected outputs.

### [A Fable pin served Opus, not a billing alias](https://github.com/Gurio/brr/pull/425) — Gurio
A `brr` maintainer revisited an earlier assumption that `claude-opus-4-8` in `modelUsage` was a harmless alternate billing label for Fable-pinned runs. Historical observations instead showed Fable IDs until the moment those requests began receiving Opus, so the merged repair removes the exception and makes a Fable pin observed as Opus fire `core_mismatch` again. The change adds explicit single- and joined-observation regression cases; its author reports 1,845 tests passing, while the upstream explanation remains open.

### [A 2.5 GB sonar bag becomes a 480 ms multi-pass viewer](https://github.com/rolker/marine_perception_tools/pull/23) — rolker
Rolker credits Claude Fable 5 with a 17-file addition to a marine survey explorer: operators can select several `mbes-bathy` passes and inspect them together as a colour-keyed 3D cloud, with explicit warnings when coordinate frames cannot safely be aligned. On a 2.5 GB Massabesic recording, the author reports that a 2.3-minute pass produced 994,908 soundings from 3,921 pings in about 480 ms; fixing a seek strategy and an accidental per-ping `reserve()` cut the same path from 50 seconds to 478 ms. The merged change adds 37 tests for reprojection, transform fallback, rendering and colour assignment, bringing the reported suite to 311 passing tests; its visible build-and-test job was still running when inspected.

### [Resync stops inventing task lanes from weak identifiers](https://github.com/Crpaxton4/devcontainer-features/pull/399) — Crpaxton4
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored resync overhaul changes how an Odoo SDK reconstructs work: it accepts documented task-ID forms, rejects short issue references, validates IDs in a batched lookup, and records unknown values as weak evidence rather than joining them to a session. It also pulls unmerged Git history, open pull requests, reviews, comments, multiple author identities, and author-wide Odoo messages, while making the day boundary configurable by timezone. The author documents fixtures for the false-positive, unknown-ID, unmerged-branch, multi-identity and timezone cases, reports 1,762 tests and 98% coverage, and the merged PR’s 17 visible checks are successful.

### [Fable plans and reviews in a portable multi-model workflow](https://github.com/arvind9981/harness-workflow/pull/8) — arvind9981
Arvind9981’s merged 46-file workflow assigns Fable and Sonnet to planning and review, Codex to inspection, Sol to the single-writing role, and Terra to bounded reconnaissance; it can route Claude, Codex and OpenCode traffic through one proxy while retaining Docker MCP and durable memory on demand. The author reports two live smoke runs: Fable plan → Codex inspection → Fable review, and an automatic OpenCode route that selected Fable as architect before critical review; both left the repository byte-identical. Its three workflow scripts reported 58 passes and no failures, alongside syntax, ShellCheck, compilation, data-format and diff checks; the repository’s visible workflow check is green.

### [FalkorDB credentials and topology probes stop disagreeing](https://github.com/rkrumins/dataviz/pull/245) — rkrumins
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored FalkorDB connectivity pass makes blank or whitespace-only credentials mean “absent” before both the lightweight preflight and the real client connection are built. That closes a disagreement where redis-py would issue `AUTH <user> ''` while the raw RESP probe sent no authentication at all; it also refuses cluster mode with no startup nodes instead of accidentally probing a standalone host, and clears stale warmup failures after an operator changes a provider configuration. The 22-file branch adds a Docker cross-cluster fixture whose announced private node addresses only work after explicit address remapping, sentinel-TLS and auth-flip cases, plus configuration tests; CodeQL and the dependency and security checks are green, while its separate “Every feature flag is really wired” guard is failing.

### [Contract-audit slices recover value-moving functions](https://github.com/Replikanti/agentis-colonies/pull/1703) — ylohnitram
A Claude Code/Fable 5 patch fixes an audit-preparation blind spot in Agentis Colonies: each Solidity contract was reduced to its first eight declared functions, so admin setters could hide every withdrawal or recovery handler from the classifier. The author reproduced that failure on Dodo’s `GatewayCrossChain`, `GatewaySend`, and `GatewayTransferNative` contracts, where the old slices caught none of `withdraw*`, `onRevert`, `onAbort`, or `onCall`. The revised ranking puts value-moving and recovery names first without deleting anything; the real-target rerun emitted those handlers, the new fixture pins the case, and the branch reports 525 local checks plus a green visible lint job.

### [Cache backend failures stop becoming process panics](https://github.com/josh-project/josh/pull/2261) — Christian Schilling
A Claude Fable 5-assisted josh patch replaces `expect()` calls that turned cache-backend errors into process panics. Transaction operations now return and propagate `anyhow::Result`; where Git revwalk callbacks cannot return an error, they conservatively treat the cache lookup as a miss so the normal fallible path can report the underlying problem. The four-file Rust change merged with the repository’s visible CI check green.

### [Desktop remote sessions persist empty thinking blocks](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77934) — jaidhyani
A detailed Fable 5-authored Claude Code report isolates a remote-session failure to the Agent SDK entrypoint rather than a particular release: transcripts from `sdk-cli` sessions on versions 2.1.197 through 2.1.204 contain an empty `thinking` string plus a signature, while interactive `cli` sessions on the same machines retain their text. The author tested the entrypoint distinction across four versions and found the state sticks to the session until it is resumed once in a terminal, after which later turns persist normally even when viewed in the desktop app. That leaves remote-launch users without recoverable thinking summaries and may produce the empty-block shape involved in an existing resume-time 400 error.

## 2026-07-16

### [A cp932 locale turns Fable punctuation into MCP crashes](https://github.com/Cjbuilds/Codex-Orchestration/pull/11) — glayht96-ctrl
A Windows 11 user found that a Fable 5 response containing an em dash could make their Codex-orchestration MCP server die in Python’s `cp932` decoder: two of four natural `review_plan` calls crashed, while a third returned mojibake instead of failing. Setting `PYTHONUTF8=1` eliminated the crash in the same four-call repro, pointing to subprocess decoding rather than the MCP protocol itself. The Fable co-authored patch sets `encoding="utf-8", errors="replace"` at every model/CLI-output subprocess site; the author reports 23 focused tests and Ruff passing, and four patched calls succeeding without the environment workaround.

### [FFT padding choices are checked against every input through 20,000](https://github.com/CWBudde/algo-fft/pull/22) — MeKo-Christian
A Fable 5 co-authored `algo-fft` change adds a helper that finds the smallest 5-smooth number at least as large as a requested length, bounded by the next power of two. It lets Bluestein transforms consider mixed-radix padding that the engine can execute exactly, while retaining the current calibrated choice where a dispatched power-of-two sub-FFT wins. The test suite brute-forces minimality through 20,000 inputs and covers allocation behavior and transform agreement; all completed visible format, fuzz, unit, lint, Linux, Windows, macOS, ARM, and WASM checks are green.

### [A robotaxi data update checks every earlier filing for revisions](https://github.com/owid/etl/pull/6449) — paarriagadap
For Our World in Data’s California robotaxi series, the author says Claude Fable 5 rebuilt the CPUC ingestion chain and re-fetched all 16 previously scraped deployment-report archives rather than assuming old values were fixed. Every overlapping month-and-carrier value matched the prior snapshot; the new filing adds Waymo’s January through March 2026 data, including 1.36 million completed trips in March. The branch also adds schema, carrier, non-negativity, uniqueness, coverage, and contiguous-month checks, each negative-tested with corrupted inputs; its build-and-deploy and security checks are successful, while the chart-diff job is currently marked failed.

### [A Jungle engine gains forty-five Elo over its frozen predecessor](https://github.com/xmiao76/claude_cc_jungle/pull/2) — xmiao76
xmiao76 used Claude Fable 5 at max effort to turn the shipped v1.3 Jungle-game engine into a separately configurable v1.4, preserving a frozen v1.3 baseline so every later change could be tested against it. Two color-swapped, 200-game self-play runs at 300 ms per move put the new engine at 56.5% overall — about +45 Elo — while a two-second GUI benchmark rose from 13.2k to 22.5k nodes per second and one extra ply. A full-diff review found that null moves could contaminate repetition detection; the repair and regression test were included before the executable was rebuilt, and the author reports 113 tests, Ruff, package launch, human-versus-AI, and a completed 57-move AI-versus-AI game.

### [Coach-only reports get an optimistic-concurrency guard](https://github.com/towynlin/kanchazo/pull/31) — Zachary Crockett
Zachary Crockett’s Fable 5 co-authored feature gives coaches a separate final-score and private-notes record for each game, stored in a new `game_reports` table and never served to parents or mixed with shared event notes. Each edit carries a version number, so a stale save gets a 409 plus the current report rather than silently overwriting another coach’s work. The 12-file, 2,137-line change includes unit and integration coverage; visible lint/typecheck, build, CodeQL, and unit/integration checks are green, while Playwright E2E was still running.

### [A recovery path stops releasing money that a rail already settled](https://github.com/bb-connor/arc/pull/1018) — connor
On a money-path durability branch, Fable 5 identified a crash-recovery bug in which a prepaid rail could report funds already settled after an authorization, but local reconciliation would release the hold and write no receipt. The repair makes the adapter’s settlement state explicitly three-way — no authorization, held, or settled — so recovery releases only a proven hold, writes a reconciliation receipt for settled funds, and opens an incident when the rail cannot answer. Other Fable-authored commits replace a saturating spend projection with a fail-closed overflow denial, validate bounded retry settings at load time, and change monetary-orphan lookup from a scan to a keyed query; the author reports 10,795 workspace tests passing, while the public matrix was still underway.

### [Seven client-work categories, including a deliberately flawed API route](https://ssntpl.com/blog-claude-fable-5-real-world-test-review/) — Sambhav
Over 72 hours, Sambhav ran Fable 5 on prior client briefs for SEO strategy, a loan-origination requirements document, market research, competitor analysis, long-form content, a code review, and data interpretation, comparing outputs with earlier Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 work. On the 180-line Stripe-webhook route, Fable found all three planted defects — missing signature verification, an unhandled rejection, and an untransactional status update — while the author says it cut the SEO exercise from three rounds to one and the long-form piece from two revisions to none. The write-up also records the limits: keyword-difficulty estimates needed SEO-tool validation, India-specific market figures needed current sourcing, and financial regulation still needed a domain-expert pass.

### [Sixteen blind real-job comparisons find technical wins and three fabricated details](https://sidsaladi.substack.com/p/claude-fable-5-101-the-complete-guide) — Sid Saladi
Sid Saladi’s team put Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 through 16 real tasks spanning debugging, code review, system design, PRDs, investor updates, data cleanup, and research synthesis, then used a judge that did not know which model wrote each response. The authors report that Fable found all three deliberately planted security problems, kept a refactor within a 25-line cap, and correctly normalized every vendor in a messy CSV; it also wrote three unsupported narrative details across an investor update, a pitch deck, and a changelog. That split led the authors to recommend explicit source-grounding checks for narrative documents even as their technical and cited work held up better in the exercise.

### [An MLX attention-kernel change passes correctness but misses promotion](https://github.com/Layr-Labs/mlxfast-challenge-dev/pull/631) — yukon-eigen
A Claude Fable 5/OpenCode multi-agent submission changed a Swift staged sliding-attention kernel to skip 240 fully masked QK tiles per 512-key block and write token-major output directly, avoiding an 8.4 MB transpose/reshape per layer. The author reports raw-bit agreement on real weights, 445 of 445 package tests passing, and a paired M5 Max measurement of 1.360–1.363 ms/token with the change versus 1.393–1.396 ms/token without it. The challenge harness independently recorded zero maximum absolute difference and a 5.95% prefill speedup, but its 1.1730 score did not beat the 1.1765 incumbent, so the submission was not promoted.

### [A Windows hook fix chooses Git Bash over fragile shell guessing](https://github.com/obra/superpowers/pull/1993) — Jesse Vincent
Jesse Vincent used Claude Fable 5 to investigate why Superpowers’ Claude Code SessionStart hook silently failed for Windows users, then reproduced both the PowerShell parser error and `cmd.exe` path-truncation failure on a real Windows 11 VM. The three-file repair declares `shell: "bash"` for the hook, adds a registration test, and updates the platform documentation; it preserved a 3,276-character bootstrap context on Linux and on Windows paths containing both spaces and parentheses. With Git Bash removed, the new route produced an actionable install/configuration error instead of silently failing, while live tests on Claude Code 2.0.77 and 2.1.80 showed those older versions ignoring the unknown field and retaining prior behavior.

### [Three evidence graphs expose a bio-context refusal](https://github.com/Episteme-Foundation/episteme/pull/156) — jacksonqueenking / Episteme
Episteme ran Claude Fable 5 as both Steward and Curator through three isolated research corpora—LHC safety, egg-health evidence, and COVID-origin claims—and committed the resulting claim graphs as static JSON. The black-hole run alone preserved 39 claims, 10 source instances, 17 arguments, and 12 assessments, including a separately qualified assessment of Hawking radiation rather than treating it as directly observed. The team also found that Fable intermittently refused assessment calls in the full multi-tool context for bio-adjacent claims: one eggs claim and two lab-leak claims were parked even though extraction and matching completed; the open branch’s Vercel and repository checks are green.

### [Nine hundred forty-two Sanskrit grammar claims get a corpus audit](https://github.com/gasyoun/SanskritGrammar/pull/256) — Dr. Mārcis Gasūns
A Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 workflow audited five Russian-language Sanskrit teaching texts against pinned DCS corpus snapshots, Whitney’s grammar, and a root-class catalogue, with the report’s numbers generated from registries and nine reproducible tools rather than typed in manually. It records 942 claim verdicts plus 214 audited parses, then measures a 4.07-million-token corpus across 41 texts: five style metrics rise monotonically from Vedic to classical Sanskrit, while one causative pattern falls from 81.3% to 20.8%. The report publishes scripts and data paths for each result and says the repository’s Python tests and Docusaurus smoke build passed.

### [A handset charging test samples watts once a second](https://github.com/Theprofitplatform/ad-cleaner/pull/23) — Theprofitplatform
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored Android utility now turns ADB battery telemetry into a one-hertz charging-port test that reports volts, amps, watts, negotiated maximum, peak, and a plain-language verdict. Its author tested the tool live on an S26 Ultra; the code avoids Samsung event-log fields shadowing live readings and falls back to `sysfs` current data when `dumpsys` omits it. The merged three-file change adds a GUI smoke test for a 25.2 W fast-charge reading, and its visible pytest and security checks passed.

### [A devcontainer build keeps version bumps and checksums in one change](https://github.com/bare-devcontainer/images/pull/182) — nozaq
When Renovate updated a Rust version, this image catalog could leave the committed `rustup-init` checksums stale and make every Rust image build fail until a second housekeeping PR landed. A Fable 5 co-authored repair makes build configuration declare its trust material, then has one script update versioned checksums through Git’s blob/tree/commit API and either amend the triggering PR or open a scheduled maintenance PR. The 11-file branch preserves verified commits, avoids contents-API ref races and large base64 payloads, and its CodeQL and initial image-matrix jobs are green while the broader matrix is running.

### [A frozen-prompt browser experiment finds two blank outputs](https://github.com/noricha-vr/oreore-bench/pull/11) — noricha-vr
Noricha-vr added Claude Fable 5, three GPT-5.6 variants, and Gemini 3.1 Pro to a ten-model browser-output comparison, running the new models at high reasoning effort on two frozen prompts and publishing each unedited result. Fable’s headless Claude Code run went beyond the requested one-shot response by autonomously verifying and committing its result; the author left that behavior intact to preserve the record. Playwright smoke tests found Fable’s generated fluid-art demo working, while GPT-5.6 Luna and Sol produced no JavaScript errors but rendered a blank page after interaction; the merged PR publishes the raw HTML outputs and the test notes.

### [A blind second pass checks 115 Sanskrit grammar verdicts](https://github.com/gasyoun/SanskritGrammar/pull/284) — gasyoun
A Claude Fable 5 workflow added a reproducible second-pass audit to a 728-claim Sanskrit-grammar verification project, drawing all 30 fact flags, all 25 untestable claims, and 60 seeded-random true claims. Sonnet 5 received six blind 15–20-item packages containing claims and deterministic evidence but no original verdicts or notes; the resulting 106/115 agreement produced Cohen’s κ of 0.877, with a 2,000-resample 95% interval of 0.796–0.946. All nine disagreements were preserved for human adjudication, and the merged PR includes the sampler, blind and gold packages, second-pass responses, calculation script, plus successful Python and Docusaurus checks.

### [A second MLX kernel submission decodes 21% faster but misses the leaderboard](https://github.com/Layr-Labs/mlxfast-challenge-dev/pull/633) — yukon-eigen
A Fable 5/OpenCode multi-agent submission to the MLX Fast challenge combined co-tiled attention payloads, packed metadata, and causal-tile skips while keeping the arithmetic and output boundaries unchanged. The challenge runner found a maximum absolute difference of zero and measured decoding at 36.33 ms per token against a 44.08 ms baseline, a 1.213× speedup; prefill was also 1.070× faster, and the author reports 486 package tests plus byte-level validation modes. Its composite score of 1.17585 fell just below the 1.17652 leader, so the closed PR was not promoted despite successful Swift and ranked-benchmark checks.

### [A repository-mirror review finds a broken APT index and signed XML injection](https://github.com/define42/ArtiGate/pull/50) — define42
A Fable 5-assisted review of ArtiGate’s roughly 45,000 non-test Go lines across about 60 files found two high-severity defects after the author says every agent finding was checked against source. Rebuilt multi-package APT indexes joined deb822 stanzas with one newline rather than a blank line, making all but one package disappear to `apt`; separately, unescaped fields from a potentially unverified upstream RPM `repomd.xml` could be inserted into the high side’s newly signed XML. The branch changes the APT join, escapes the RPM fields, and adds two-package and hostile-input regression tests; its visible test, lint, e2e, CodeQL, and vulnerability checks are successful.

### [LLVM stores compiler-cycle trees in a compact preorder array](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/209981) — MaskRay
LLVM contributor MaskRay credits Fable 5 with aiding a refactor of `GenericCycle` storage: instead of heap-allocating every cycle and retaining child `unique_ptr` vectors, the cycle forest is flattened into a value array in preorder. That reduces `sizeof(GenericCycle)` from 72 to 48 bytes, while child and top-level traversal use pointer arithmetic and a new descendant count to skip whole subtrees.
