The open 11-file branch adds 797 lines and removes 372; its formatter, documentation, and ABI-annotation checks are successful, while Linux, Windows, and AArch64 build-and-test jobs were still running when inspected.

### [A relay stops mistaking a Fable child process for a nested session](https://github.com/rattanasak-ops/hermes-agent/pull/55) — rattanasak-ops
A Claude Code user found that launching a short `claude -p` call through their relay could inherit `CLAUDECODE` and be rejected as a nested session, crashing subscription-routed Fable and Sonnet calls. The Fable 5-assisted patch removes that environment variable only for child `claude` processes, leaving other tools alone. A focused regression test verifies the split, and the author reports 98 of 98 tests passing plus a live `relay-call --tool fable` response whose model label was `claude-fable-5`; the PR has no public CI checks yet.

### [Note flashcards gain their own API and AI suggestions](https://github.com/Crossbill-Highlights/crossbill-web/pull/407) — Tumetsu
This Fable 5 co-authored Crossbill feature lets readers attach flashcards to a note, keep them when the note is deleted as book-level cards, and request suggestions generated from the note’s title, body, and linked highlights. It adds create and suggestion endpoints, returns cards with the note detail response, and carries the feature through backend, API-client, and dialog changes across 42 files. New tests cover default and explicit book links, rejected unlinked books, suggestion input composition, and note retrieval; visible backend, frontend, Python and JavaScript CodeQL checks are green.

### [SQL placeholder defaults become dialect-aware](https://github.com/jbergstroem/filtron/pull/314) — jbergstroem
A Claude Code agent running Fable 5 added a `dialect` option to Filtron’s SQL converter so PostgreSQL selects `$1` placeholders while MySQL and SQLite select `?`, without changing callers that omit the option. The implementation deliberately gives an explicitly supplied placeholder style precedence over the dialect preset. Five new tests pin the three defaults, the override rule, and backwards compatibility; the open three-file PR has 20 visible successful checks, including unit tests and Node, Bun, and Deno E2E runs.

### [A Sanskrit voice-grammar hypothesis gets a reproducible corpus check](https://github.com/gasyoun/SanskritGrammar/pull/317) — gasyoun
A Fable 5 handoff extended a Sanskrit-grammar evidence register from 74 to 96 claims by adjudicating 22 previously unregistered assertions about voice. Because the underlying corpus tags only 36,701 passive tokens and lacks active/middle labels, the new committed tool recovers unambiguous endings instead, classifying 49.1% of 494,022 finite non-passive tokens as 77.8% parasmaipada and 22.2% ātmanepada. The results mark 23 of 29 voice claims true, one overstated, and five untestable; notably, the textbook's claim that an instrumental noun is usually first in a passive sentence held in only 18.6% of the 8,332 relevant sentences. The merged branch includes the deterministic Python instrument, JSON results, claim records, and the raw limitations rather than forcing verdicts where the corpus cannot answer them.

### [A Fable-assisted SELinux investigation keeps PID 1’s bus alive](https://github.com/fedora-selinux/selinux-policy/pull/3289) — Benjamin Vouillaume
Benjamin Vouillaume used Fable 5 while investigating an SELinux failure in which `systemd-run --pipe` redirected through `/tmp` or `/root` could have PID 1 lose its API-bus connection after the kernel stripped a denied inherited file descriptor. The proposed two-file policy change grants only the missing inherited-FD permissions to `init_t`, covering read, write, append, and read/write redirections without granting new `open` access to admin-home files. On a real enforcing Fedora 44 system with dontaudit disabled, the author reports the full redirect matrix passing with delivered content, zero relevant AVCs, and no bus disconnect; removing the local equivalent module restored all three failures. The PR is still open, with three Fedora build and reverse-dependency checks in progress.

### [A Fable-directed 20-worker test finishes without a retry](https://github.com/agent-os-inc/fleet-sandbox/pull/32) — agent-chad-bongo
An Agent OS end-to-end run used Claude Fable 5 as the conductor for 20 OAuth-routed GPT-5.6 Sol workers, asking each for a typed “Hello World” result. The committed trace records every leg as complete after one attempt, with 20 matching result, expected-result, and turn-evidence SHA-256 values; the 62-event job had no timeout, retry, salvage, error, or failover event. The run lasted just over 20 seconds with API-key fallback disabled, so it is a narrow but fully logged orchestration check rather than a general capability benchmark.

### [Terminal drag-selection no longer leaves a shell stuck in scrollback](https://github.com/sinelaw/fresh/pull/2708) — sinelaw
A Fable 5 co-authored Fresh investigation traced a terminal bug in which copying a mouse selection left an idle shell in read-only scrollback, because output-driven resume remained suppressed after the copy. The author reproduced the state transitions through the web bridge and found a second failure: an uncleared selection anchor could reappear on the next scrollback entry and permanently suppress automatic resume. The nine-file implementation separates drag-initiated from deliberate scrollback, resumes after copying or clicking away from the former, and includes 294 lines of E2E coverage; visible Playwright, formatter, lint, docs, schema, and Linux, macOS, and Windows tests are green.

### [A cross-model review route preserves uncertainty about what Cursor served](https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/pull/1152) — gmledbetter-cmyk
A Fable 5 route added to Compound Engineering’s adversarial-review tooling sends a high-thinking request through Cursor while keeping the review read-only and requiring both host and Cursor egress approval. Because Cursor does not return a model receipt, the implementation records the requested route rather than claiming it independently verified the served model; it also refuses Fable as a peer on a Claude-family host. The author reports a live canary that produced a normalized review artifact with three findings repaired before the final run, alongside 2,199 Bun tests and 146 focused route-and-contract tests passing.

### [A local proxy compacts a million-token Fable session at 96,000 tokens](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/65574) — carlotestor
A production Fable 5 session routed through a local Anthropic-compatible proxy was treated as a 128,000-token model because the client read the API’s `max_tokens` output limit instead of its 1,000,000-token input limit. The cached value made the client compact at about 96,000 tokens; the author captured a 101,454-token request being compressed before it ever reached the API. The proposed repair prefers `max_input_tokens`, keeps an OpenAI-compatible fallback, and the author reports 40 focused tests, including live-shaped model-list and detail responses.

### [Airflow task logs fall back to stderr after a broken socket](https://github.com/apache/airflow/pull/69302) — guan404ming
A Claude Code/Fable 5-assisted Airflow Task SDK change addresses logs silently disappearing after its same-host logging socket closes unexpectedly. Rather than attempting a reconnect to a peer that has died, it emits one warning and sends later JSON log records to stderr, which the coordinator already captures. The added tests destroy a real test-server socket, confirm both root and child loggers reach stderr afterward, and verify that an explicit close still drops later records.

### [A 200-run prompt-steering rerun corrects a cold-cache bias](https://ai.georgeliu.com/p/claude-opus-48-vs-claude-fable-5) — George Liu
George Liu reran his fixed ten-prompt, five-wrapper Claude Code matrix across Fable 5 and Opus 4.8, instrumenting 200 sessions with his session-metrics plugin. He found that `ultrathink` increased Fable 5’s high-effort session cost by 31.0%, while the no-tools wrapper cut costs by 10–24%; Fable also met an exact 120-word constraint in all ten relevant cells, where Opus 4.8 overran it five times. The write-up corrects two flaws in Liu’s April comparison—a broken tool prompt and cold-cache bias—rather than carrying its earlier savings claim forward unchanged.

### [A hedged perpetual position unwinds when its inverse leg cannot open](https://github.com/richkuo/go-trader/pull/1376) — richkuo
Rich Kuo used Fable 5 at medium effort in a separate worktree to add per-strategy inverse hedge legs to a Hyperliquid perpetuals trader: a long ETH position can open a ratio-sized short BTC hedge and reconcile it each dispatch cycle. The code persists ownership and quantity watermarks, excludes hedge losses from the strategy’s loss streak, rejects conflicting hedge coins, and unwinds the primary leg with a sized reduce-only order if opening the hedge fails. The open branch adds 677 lines of scheduler logic and 649 lines of Go tests; its visible Go and Python checks are successful, while the author reports 2,852 Python tests passed with one skipped.

### [A fallback formatter no longer returns failure after writing its output](https://github.com/shakespark/asn1c/pull/32) — shakespark
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored fix in asn1c repairs a fallback `vasprintf()` whose inner `actual_length` variable shadowed the value returned to its caller, so successful formatting was reported as `-1` and assertion-enabled builds could abort. The author reproduced four compiler-test failures on Debian 12 with glibc 2.36 and then added runtime error handling for the failure path, correct `va_list` cleanup, and size calculations that avoid signed overflow. The single-file patch was merged after the author reported a clean `./configure && make check`: 89 passes, one pre-existing expected failure, and no failures or errors.

### [An unmount cleanup stops confetti from rendering into a destroyed Skia surface](https://github.com/AlirezaHadjar/react-native-fast-confetti/pull/38) — rbracco
After correlating production Sentry crashes on iOS and Android with an in-flight confetti burst, rbracco asked Fable 5 to investigate `react-native-fast-confetti`; the resulting one-file PR adds `cancelAnimation(progress)` in a `useLayoutEffect` cleanup. The report says the layout cleanup runs before React removes the Skia canvas, unlike a passive effect that would leave the UI-thread animation racing surface teardown; it identifies `EXC_BREAKPOINT` GPU-cache crashes on iOS and `SIGTRAP` in Skia surface flushing on Android. The contributor says the exact patch has already been deployed with `patch-package`; the upstream PR remains open without public checks.

### [A four-second idle cutoff turns Fable thinking into false answers](https://github.com/Replikanti/agentis-colonies/pull/1710) — ylohnitram
In Agentis Colonies’ corpus benchmark, the flat-cyborg runner treated four seconds without terminal output as an idle response. On the same 26 KB prompt and model, that captured Fable’s pre-answer `high · /effort` footer instead of its `ZONE|...` classification; increasing the cutoff to eight seconds or more returned the real answer. Raising it to 12 seconds across four scraper paths changed the author’s end-to-end Dodo run from one of six zones classified to six of six, without changing the model or prompt.

### [A one-line Fable fix stops coqdep exhausting file descriptors](https://github.com/rocq-prover/rocq/pull/22278) — Jason Gross
Jason Gross reports that Fable 5 found an unclosed source-file handle in Rocq’s `coqdep`, which leaked one descriptor for every warning location. On VST’s roughly 2,900 files, the resulting truncated dependency output could silently poison parallel builds; with a 64-file-descriptor limit, the reported reproduction failed after about 60 warnings. The one-line `Fun.protect` repair completed 990 warnings without error and was merged after the public Linux, macOS, and Windows checks passed.

### [A missing provider plugin sent Fable calls into a crash loop](https://github.com/Kyzcreig/hermes-agent/pull/374) — Kyzcreig
A Hermes worker requested `claude-apr/claude-fable-5` from a profile missing its provider plugin, but silently stripped the provider prefix and sent the bare model name to the default Codex provider, producing a 400 error six times. The patch distinguishes vendor namespaces from unresolved provider-qualified model names and fails at startup with a corrective error instead; the author reports clean-profile end-to-end coverage and 33 focused tests. Nine of ten visible Python-test slices are green, but one slice is failing, so the PR remains open.

### [GeoSPARQL accessors stay off by default](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2374) — jeswr
A SPARQ agent run attributed to Claude Fable implemented five GeoSPARQL 1.1 geometry accessors behind an opt-in feature: dimensions, simplicity, and geometry type. It delegates to existing pure metadata functions, leaves the default registry byte-for-byte unchanged, and returns an error rather than inventing a value for empty geometries or geometry collections. The Fable implementation agent stalled at its final gate sequence, so the orchestrator reran the tests and had Opus review the patch; the public CI includes successful test shards, coverage gates, CodeQL, and opt-in feature jobs.

### [A 91-finding review becomes a security hardening release](https://github.com/cloudnative-co/claude-code-starter-kit/pull/149) — loadbalance-sudachi-kun
A Claude Fable 5-orchestrated review of the Claude Code Starter Kit condensed 91 findings into 38 tracked items, then delegated investigation and repairs to Haiku and Sonnet agents. The merged 24-file release fixes a stored-XSS dependency, unsafe privileged npm installation, overly broad secret-file permissions, and shell scripts that could die silently when `grep` found no match; it also adds regression tests for those cases. The author says Codex independently reviewed all eight commits with no findings, while the public matrix shows shellcheck, unit, and macOS/Ubuntu scenario jobs succeeding.

### [A stale browser tab no longer freezes after a self-update](https://github.com/fgarofalo56/csa-inabox/pull/2116) — fgarofalo56
A live investigation found that a tab left open across an in-place update could request chunks from its old build graph and freeze for more than 30 seconds on the next soft navigation; fresh tabs did not reproduce it. The Fable 5 co-authored fix adds a single AppShell guard that remembers the build SHA, rechecks it every five minutes and on refocus, then performs a safe hard reload on the next route change or after a quiet interval. The two-file PR is still open, with its larger CI matrix underway.

### [A router turns disabled thinking into Fable’s lowest valid effort](https://github.com/workweave/router/pull/748) — devin-ai-integration[bot]
WorkWeave found 89 production 400 responses in 30 days, all on `claude-fable-5`, when cloud and agent workflows sent `thinking: {type: "disabled"}` to a model that only supports adaptive thinking. The merged repair maps that intent to adaptive thinking at `low` effort, leaves legacy extended-thinking models alone, and drops the block for non-thinking models. It adds regression coverage for all three routes; the public Test, routing-review, CodeQL, and language-analysis checks passed, although a separate `review` check failed.

### [A 160-million-token Fable bill was hiding under other model labels](https://github.com/ItsJazii/pane/pull/70) — ItsJazii
A Devin user reported that Fable usage was missing because the scanner charged every message to the session’s final model label; their machine had 129.8 million `claude-5-fable-medium` tokens and 30.4 million Fable-high tokens recorded as GPT-5.6. The merged three-file fix reads each message’s generation-model metadata, normalizes Fable effort suffixes, and leaves Devin’s unpriced summarizer tokens out of dollar totals. On the same 30-day data, the reported total changed from $57.39 to $50.64 without changing token counts, demonstrating that the labeling error had inflated some work and discounted other work; the PR has no public CI checks.

### [An HTTP/3 server puts a 256-request ceiling on each connection](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/pull/2377) — jeswr
A Claude Fable 5 SPARQ agent repaired an HTTP/3 path that spawned an unbounded task for every accepted request stream, allowing one connection to fan out without a per-connection limit. The patch adds a default limit of 256 and holds an owned semaphore permit for the whole request task; its new test runs two requests with a limit of one, proves the second is held outside the router for 250 ms, then releases it after the first finishes. The author reports mutation-checking two wrong variants, and the open PR’s public HTTP/3 feature job, CodeQL, test shards, and coverage gates are successful.

### [Filtered RPM repositories keep every metadata index in sync](https://github.com/define42/ArtiGate/pull/54) — define42
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored ArtiGate repair addresses filtered RPM mirrors whose primary index dropped packages while `filelists.xml` and `other.xml` retained stale counts and orphaned entries. It also removes an ambiguity in Helm archive names: pairs such as `a-1` at `1.0.0` and `a` at `1-1.0.0` previously collapsed to the same hyphen-joined key. The six-file branch rewrites every package-ID index and uses an underscore separator that the version grammar excludes; its test, lint, CodeQL, vulnerability, and completed end-to-end checks are successful, with one end-to-end run still in progress.

### [A 67-task vision evaluation puts Fable tenth](https://blog.roboflow.com/claude-fable-5-for-vision/) — Erik Kokalj / Roboflow
Roboflow ran Fable 5 through 67 real-image tasks across object, spatial and document understanding, defect detection, and counting, using the Anthropic API with adaptive thinking at maximum effort. It completed 50 tasks (74.63%), ranking tenth; it was perfect on object understanding but managed only 3 of 10 object-counting tasks. The team found it one task ahead of Opus 4.8 but nearly four times slower, and recommends using it for visual question answering or document extraction rather than counting.

### [NeoWiki documents its supported Cypher path](https://github.com/ProfessionalWiki/NeoWiki/pull/1045) — malberts / Professional Wiki
Malberts says a Fable 5 Max Claude Code session, steered through roughly 12 rounds, produced new guidance distinguishing NeoWiki's unsupported rendered HTML and raw Neo4j client from its read-only, limited PHP query service. The author had four Opus 4.8 review subagents check claims, links and anchors across two passes; an Opus subagent implemented the accompanying real-Neo4j RedHerb example and its red-green integration test. The open eight-file branch has successful visible lint, PHPUnit, PHPStan and PHPCS checks, while the author explicitly notes the example code still awaits human review.

### [A game server cuts snapshot bandwidth from 52.3 to 22.7 KB/s](https://github.com/gpitot/Archer2/pull/5) — gpitot
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored Archer2 change keeps the server simulation at 60 Hz but sends snapshots every third tick, accumulates intervening events, and has clients reconstruct projectiles from their initial fire event. Its new local WebSocket harness drives four active bots for 15 seconds and reports per-client downstream traffic falling from 52.3 to 22.7 KB/s while the client increases its interpolation delay to 150 ms. The seven-file branch is open and does not yet have public checks, so that bandwidth result remains the author's measurement.

### [A Bedrock model picker offers a Fable ID that cannot be invoked](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/58201) — PRATHAMESH75
On an on-demand Bedrock account, selecting the bare `anthropic.claude-fable-5` identifier in Hermes's `/model` picker persisted it to configuration and made every later call fail with Bedrock's inference-profile 400. PRATHAMESH75 traced the mismatch to shared model discovery returning both the bare ID and its invokable regional profile, then added a profile-preference deduplication path and regression cases for covered and uncovered models. The author reports 137 focused tests, and the open two-file repair's visible Python slices, lints, E2E, Docker builds and required-check rollup are successful.

### [OpenNMS removes its version-locked flow plugin](https://github.com/OpenNMS/opennms/pull/8638) — Marshall Massengill / OpenNMS
Marshall Massengill says Claude Fable 5 assisted a 19-file OpenNMS flow-query change that replaces a version-matched Elasticsearch Drift plugin with inline Painless scripts compatible with stock Elasticsearch and OpenSearch. The branch distributes flow bytes across overlapping graph buckets, retains an opt-in legacy-plugin strategy, and adds unit plus stock-Elasticsearch integration coverage; its release notes warn that corrected non-aligned windows can produce higher totals than the old undercounting plugin. The draft PR's public build, pre-build, label and reviewer checks are successful.

### [Nine end-user experiments put an agent runtime through crash-and-replay tests](https://github.com/ThousandBirdsInc/chidori/pull/135) — kvey
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored Chidori branch began as nine no-key end-user experiments covering build friction, policy gates, deterministic replay, modern JavaScript, error messages, throughput, and resuming after a `SIGKILL`. It verified zero side-effect re-execution by replaying a three-line on-disk ledger byte-for-byte, and verified that a paused run resumed in a new process without repeating its already-journaled tool call. The critique also found eight issues—including false positives from a raw `import(` substring scan, misleading source locations, and surprising fixed clock/RNG defaults—which the same branch says it repaired and reran against the original repros; its visible Rust, SDK, isolation, Test262, audit, and dependency-policy checks are successful.

### [A model switch sends GPT reasoning back to Fable as unsigned thinking](https://github.com/bigduu/Bamboo-agent/issues/520) — bigduu
A Bamboo-agent report describes a failure after a tool-using GPT-5.6 Sol session switches to real Claude Fable 5: the next request returns HTTP 400 because the Anthropic adapter turns stored OpenAI reasoning summaries into `thinking` blocks without the signatures Anthropic requires. The author traced the issue to an SSE parser that drops `signature_delta` entirely, so even Claude-originated thinking cannot be replayed safely; the report distinguishes this from a separate Responses-continuation bug already addressed elsewhere. Its proposed boundary repair is to persist provenance and signatures, then omit unverifiable or foreign thinking blocks rather than replaying them; a follow-up says a Fable orchestrator has dispatched an implementation agent for that path.

### [A Fable 5 terminal transport heals dead tmux channels](https://github.com/agent-of-empires/agent-of-empires/pull/2911) — njbrake
A Claude Code session running Fable 5 repaired the terminal transport in agent-of-empires after a recreated tmux session left viewers attached to a dead channel and stuck on slow capture. The author verified against live tmux 3.6 that `pipe-pane` survives `respawn-pane -k` and that `keypad_cursor_flag` tracks DECCKM, then added regression tests for dead-entry refusal, cache invalidation, reader generation changes, and cursor-mode replay. The patch also caches idle scrollback samples instead of rebuilding ANSI frames every 25 milliseconds; its visible formatter, Clippy, supply-chain, web-build, and CodeQL analysis checks were successful while the larger test matrix was still running.

### [A corpus test finds Russian translators blur Sanskrit past tenses](https://github.com/gasyoun/SamudraManthanam/pull/81) — gasyoun
A Fable 5 run examined whether Russian translations preserve the distinction between Sanskrit perfect and imperfect forms across 41,023 verse-aligned Sanskrit–Russian pairs. The committed detector used 342 imperfect, 179 aorist, and 193 perfect forms, fired on 7,574 pairs, and found Russian perfective past in 64.7% of perfect-only verses versus 67.7% of imperfect-only ones; the reported Cramér’s V of 0.084 makes that difference negligible despite a significant chi-squared result. The report is explicit about its boundary: it works at verse-group level, without token alignment or a Sanskrit morphological layer, and treats precision as more important than recall.

### [Copilot’s Fable endpoint needs adaptive thinking](https://github.com/decolua/9router/pull/2652) — ryanngit
Ryan Ngit found that Claude Fable 5 on GitHub Copilot’s native `/v1/messages` endpoint rejects the older `thinking.type=enabled` request shape and instead requires adaptive thinking with `output_config.effort`. Updating 9router’s Fable capability metadata from `claude-budget` to `claude-adaptive` changed an isolated 0.5.35 request at maximum effort from the documented provider error to HTTP 200 through `/v1/responses`. The author also reports 18 targeted capability and routing tests passing, making this a concrete integration constraint for clients that share Claude request schemas.

### [A citation census halves a Sanskrit dictionary’s estimated scan-linkable share](https://github.com/sanskrit-lexicon/MWS/pull/246) — Mārcis Gasūns
A Claude Code run using Fable 5 turned an outline into a 492-line journal-manuscript draft and a deterministic census of all 320,828 tagged citations in Monier-Williams’ Sanskrit-English Dictionary. It found that only 60,820 citations (18.96%) name a work and locator, correcting a previously imported corpus-wide figure that had suggested about 40% were linkable; the new artifacts also show a separate parser undercounting the apparatus by 28.6% because it misses an attributed XML shape and Roman-only locators. Three hostile review passes caught 14 errors in the draft and census code before merge, and the branch includes the input-classification script, CSV/JSON results, and successful Python, lint, security, and format checks.

### [A background-agent wait line stops Claude sessions flapping to idle](https://github.com/agent-of-empires/agent-of-empires/pull/2915) — njbrake
In agent-of-empires, Claude Code with Fable 5 reproduced a status-monitor regression by recording 433 tmux frames alongside hook events while background agents ran under Claude Code 2.1.211. The monitor had treated a parked main REPL as idle despite its “Waiting for 1 background agent to finish” line, so Fable added that line as an active signal and raised the stale-running grace period from 6 to 30 seconds; rescanning the captured corpus produced zero false idle flaps. The merged Rust patch includes verbatim-pane regression tests, and its public Cargo, Playwright, Vitest, Clippy, CodeQL, macOS, web-build, and supply-chain checks succeeded.

### [Three K=2 smoke tests catch a multi-catalog crash](https://github.com/ignaciomagana/darksirens/pull/243) — ignaciomagana
A Claude Code/Fable 5 co-authored `darksirens` branch unifies its multi-tracer inference path and documents mixtures of two or more galaxy catalogs. Three real K=2 smoke runs—plain field weighting, per-catalog marks, and per-catalog Q_LSS—exercise mock generation through the CLI, bundle loader, Dynesty sampling, and host-fraction analysis; they exposed an `AttributeError` that the subprocess tests had missed when an unset mark model left catalog names undefined. The open nine-file PR adds the regression coverage and reports an 805-pass, zero-failure chunked pytest run plus the three end-to-end smokes.

### [Fable’s advertised ACP side-session capability fails its fork](https://github.com/BrokkAi/mjolnir/issues/401) — jbellis
Mjolnir’s Claude ACP integration advertises side-session support, but a tested `claude-fable-5` session failed when `/side` tried to fork it: the adapter requested a resource UUID different from the active session UUID and returned “Resource not found.” jbellis recorded a three-step tmux reproduction against build `6c65fc6`, Claude Code 2.1.207, and ACP protocol 2025-11-25, then repeated the failure in an optimized release build. The contrast is useful: the same command correctly declines the unsupported path for Codex ACP, while Claude ACP advertises it and only fails after the fork attempt.

### [A 31B local model is rejected after measuring 0.5 tokens per second](https://github.com/SirsiMaster/sirsi-pantheon/pull/233) — SirsiMaster
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored repair to Sirsi Pantheon replaces a local-model selector that chose a 31B model from RAM arithmetic alone. Under real memory pressure, the author measured only 0.5 decoded tokens per second—30–50 times below the intended floor—so the resolver now probes a warm server after selection and steps down when throughput is under five tokens per second; triage also exits with the observed rate instead of hanging until a supervisor timeout. The merged three-file change has successful public test, lint, macOS build, binding-hold, and secret-scan checks.

## 2026-07-17

### [A magic-link portal keeps customer and staff identities separate](https://github.com/Juan-CO-dev/co-ops/pull/134) — Juan-CO-dev
A model-tiered Claude Code build put Fable 5 on the security-core libraries for a catering customer portal, with Opus handling migrations and routes and Sonnet the UI. Customer JWTs have a distinct issuer, require a `customer_id`, carry no role claim, and fail the staff verifier; the branch also uses atomic single-use links, constant-shape requests, per-email rate limits, and deny-all RLS on portal tables. A final Fable review caught a blocking double-foreign-key migration error and four other issues before merge; the author reports a passing build and live-development smoke covering replay, expiry, audit persistence, and principal separation, while the real production email round trip remains a stated follow-up.

### [Eight cron failure modes repaired after a Fable review](https://github.com/Growth-Escalators/Growth-Escalators-CRM/pull/52) — Jatin-ge
A Fable review of Growth Escalators CRM’s worker and startup paths became a 16-file reliability patch for duplicate delivery, cron overlap, swallowed bootstrap errors, partial failures logged as success, concurrent migrations, repeated boot backfills, and an unindexed placement scan. One finding was especially costly: a five-minute placement job could resend a stuck contact’s full WhatsApp-and-email sequence up to 288 times per day; the patch adds a delivery log check and turns cross-process cron locks on by default. The author reports 476 passing tests, including 21 new cases, while the open branch still calls out a migration-number collision and deployment-topology review before merge.

### [A thread-scoped auto-approve rule was applying everywhere](https://github.com/jeremylongshore/claude-code-slack-channel/pull/282) — parciv4l
A Fable 5 adversarial security review found that this Claude Code-to-Slack channel ignored `thread_ts` when evaluating an `auto_approve` rule, so a rule scoped to one thread would have matched every thread and channel. The repair also pins the journal’s first accepted sequence number to one, preventing head-truncated logs from verifying cleanly, and replaces prototype-chain truthiness checks in channel allowlists with own-property tests. The author reports 1,257 passing tests and four new regressions; at publication time, public CodeQL, Typecheck, JavaScript analysis, and gitleaks checks were successful, while a Kilo Code Review check was failing and Greptile Review remained in progress.

### [A 32-question RAG A/B makes Fable the hard-query default](https://github.com/Peaky8linders/regenold-eu-ai-act-rag/pull/290) — Peaky8linders
A Claude Code run compared Fable 5 with Opus 4.8 on 32 complex EU AI Act RAG questions, swapping answer position and using a Sonnet judge. Fable won conciseness 19–7 with six ties (`p=0.029`), led correctness 6–4 with 22 ties, and tied reference quality 9–9; the author explicitly invalidated an earlier 32-row run when a wrapper outage left both sides without decisive pairs. That result was the merge gate for changing the complex Stage-2 default to Fable, with a live end-to-end routing check, 60 routing/provider/resolver tests, and a one-variable rollback; production traffic still falls back to Groq until a separate Cloudflare tunnel-auth problem is repaired.

### [A hook pair restores Claude Code’s lost work state after compaction](https://github.com/CueCrux/Crux/pull/420) — CueCrux-Myles
A Fable 5-authored Claude Code contribution tackles a practical long-session failure: compaction can summarize away the open todo list, files in play, and safety notes. Its PreCompact hook extracts those details from the transcript into a session snapshot, and its SessionStart companion returns the snapshot as `additionalContext` after compaction or resume. The included proof script first demonstrates that an empty snapshot restores nothing, then asserts that a captured todo, file name, and do-not-touch note come back; it also exercises a foreign payload safely. The author reports idempotent installer tests, JSON validation, and a delivery ZIP installed and proved offline; the PR’s public lint and documentation checks were green while its main test and coverage jobs were still running.

### [Agent worktrees reclaim disk after a 444GB volume nearly fills](https://github.com/Kizunad/Bong/pull/1219) — Kizunad
After a 444GB development disk fell to 682MB free, a Claude Code/Fable 5 maintenance pass traced 207GB to `.agent-worktrees` and `.claude/worktrees`, where each Rust worktree carried a full 16–54GB build tree. The patch keeps line-level debug locations while dropping variable debug data, replaces one-off agent checkouts with persistent slots that can reuse `target` and frontend builds, and adds a report-first janitor that refuses to touch the main checkout, active processes, dirty trees, or slots. The author reports 34 state-machine tests, a real 25-worktree classification run that skipped two active end-to-end jobs, and cleanup of three stale worktrees totaling about 54GB; the only visible end-to-end check was still running at publication time.

### [Four IMO proofs archived with complete agent traces](https://github.com/SignalPilot-Labs/AutoFyn/pull/360) — tempcollab / SignalPilot Labs
AutoFyn ran Claude Fable 5 at medium effort on four 2026 International Mathematical Olympiad problems and archived the full multi-agent traces: solutions were marked complete after 26, 51, 49, and 27 minutes respectively. The merged archive includes 8,042 new lines across 103 files, with competing approaches, reviewer notes, lemmas, run state, and JSONL tool logs; its own integrity pass checked each archive against the recorded run, prompt mapping, lifecycle events, JSONL parsing, and role coverage. The author explicitly calls every “solved” status self-reported rather than independently verified, but the artifacts preserve two approved proof approaches for each of the four problems and include failed duplicate runs instead of silently excluding their existence.

### [A loop-evaluation harness finds its own headless failure](https://github.com/jmeagher/dotfiles/pull/26) — jmeagher
Jmeagher built a real-CLI harness to compare a scaffolded Claude Code loop against ordinary prompts on task-completion and reward-hack-bait fixtures. In its single-run smoke matrix, Fable 5 completed both loop tasks in an average 9.5 turns with a 5.0 judge score and no detected hacks, matching Opus 4.8; the easy fixtures did not show the loop beating baseline. More importantly, live runs exposed that headless `claude -p` does not load plugin slash commands or Stop hooks, so the supposed gate never fired; the merged repair switches the runner to inline prompts and reconstructs verification, hack-abort, and no-progress guards outside the unavailable hook path.

### [Three real PDF exports test a BOM parser rewrite](https://github.com/anishdhar9/BOMCompare.github.io/pull/5) — anishdhar9
A Claude Fable 5 co-authored repair fixes a live BOMCompare crash in which every multi-level Vault PDF upload called two undefined extractor functions. The author replaced the extraction path, added Inventor BOM imports and reference-item detection, and reports 33 synthetic assertions plus ten baseline assertions against three real Vault exports of 13, 23, and 64 pages; the source code preserves those real-fixture checks but keeps the sensitive files out of the repository. A headless browser run reportedly reproduced the original upload failure and then produced 22 actionable findings from 24 missing parts with no console errors; the merged PR has no public CI checks.

### [A walking-game MVP is rebuilt in one Fable pass](https://github.com/scrimshawlife-ctrl/Waykin/pull/15) — prabu-openclaw
The author opened a Claude Fable 5 one-pass rebuild of Waykin, an iOS walking game, alongside the existing implementation so it can be judged against the project rubric before merge. The replacement splits a Foundation-only movement and experience core from a SwiftUI/RealityKit app, includes a terminal simulator for all three experiences, and its test suite directly checks pursuit behavior, future-self catch-up, and registering a fourth experience without changing the engine. The author reports 20 passing Swift tests, a two-day command-line scenario, and an iPhone 17 Pro simulator session driven through simulated GPS; the open branch currently has no public CI checks.

### [High-effort Fable work stays polished but ungrounded](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/78325) — cives93
A Claude Code user spent a long session with Fable 5 at xhigh and max effort on a real content-and-design restructuring project, then documented three cases where it wrote confident deliverables without reading the implementation files they depended on. A merge framework covering roughly 114 entries made claims a later subagent review disproved; a mockup invented a six-section layout when the actual 15-section definition was in an unopened constants file; and the first handoff prompt omitted the implementation surface altogether. Their post-mortem is a useful distinction about effort: it made reasoning inside the chosen frame more elaborate, but did not make the model gather missing ground truth.

### [A Fable worker gets told to exit while it is still running](https://github.com/awslabs/cli-agent-orchestrator/issues/459) — Starling02
Testing Claude Fable 5 through AWS’s CLI Agent Orchestrator, Starling02 found its blocking `handoff` path declaring success after about 20 seconds and pasting `/exit` into a worker that was still showing an active spinner, leaving the requested file uncreated. The report traces the false completion to Claude Code 2.1.212 rendering `● high · /effort` at column zero, where the orchestrator’s response-marker regex mistakes it for worker output. Switching to asynchronous `assign` with an inbox callback worked across four subsequent exchanges in the same run, narrowing the failure to completion detection rather than the delegation itself.

### [A pavement plan exposes a multi-path DWG round-trip bug](https://github.com/LibreDWG/libredwg/pull/1313) — Marco Sumari Tellez
A Fable co-authored LibreDWG fix came from a real pavement plan whose two associative hatches with multiple boundary paths lost their pattern data during DXF→DWG→DXF conversion. The author reduced it to a two-path ANSI31 ring: before the fix, it came back with scale zero and no pattern lines because a per-path handle index was not reset; afterward the reduced case round-trips identically. Re-running the original plan produced zero differences across all 57 hatches, and the author reports all 254 unit tests passing.

### [Build artifacts get identity hashes and atomic publication](https://github.com/Yuu6798/ugh-prompt-engine/pull/184) — Yuu6798
Fable authored a 14-file provenance change for the Ugh Prompt Engine that gives each arrangement bundle a hash over its published output bytes, records compiler version and git commit, and publishes immutable build directories by content digest. The branch centralizes path confinement checks, preserves byte-identical derived outputs in two fresh-process runs, and adds 552 lines of build-root CLI coverage plus path-safety tests. The author reports Ruff clean and 1,385 pytest cases passing; the two public Python test jobs were still in progress when inspected.

### [Worktree commits stop disappearing after agent dispatch](https://github.com/DaveGerson/agent-baton/pull/113) — Dave Gerson
A Fable-authored Agent Baton repair targets a dangerous worktree-isolation failure: its Claude launcher inspected the configured parent checkout rather than a dispatched worker’s `cwd_override`, so a real worker commit could look absent and trigger cleanup of the worktree that contained it. The branch makes HEAD and diff probing use the effective working directory, then adds a second guard that independently verifies a reported commit and re-derives a worktree’s dirty state before any discard path can run. It includes regression tests for both the launcher and worktree manager; the public Bead-suite checks had succeeded when inspected.

### [A four-tile cosmology pipeline reaches the bash baseline](https://github.com/CosmoStat/shapepipe/issues/848) — cailmdaley
A Fable-authored Snakemake orchestration design met real survey data on nibi: its recovery run completed in 3 hours 47 minutes, produced four `final_cat` files, and passed scientific-equivalence checks against ShapePipe’s existing bash pipeline for every tile. The report says 34 tile-to-exposure edges collapsed into 19 unique exposure chains, while one 34,321-row tile exactly matched its SExtractor object count, with eight ngmix chunks covering each object once. An append dry run then scheduled 65 jobs only for four new tiles and none for the finished set, testing the promised incremental-DAG behavior rather than assuming it.

### [Fable reads handwritten arrangement marks on three church bandsheets](https://github.com/jungsunp/worship-deck-builder/issues/209) — jungsunp
An operator tested Fable 5’s vision in a normal Claude Code session on three real weekly bandsheets, where red notes encode song repeats, segues, and section order. They report that it read every arrangement mark, including the handwritten first sheet, and identified the red verse/chorus/bridge letters at their lyric positions so they could be mapped onto canonical song blocks. The proposed workflow keeps those outputs as human-reviewed suggestions and deliberately avoids putting a paid API call into the church’s production pipeline; its stated evidence is one three-sheet week, not a general accuracy claim.

### [Mid-turn Fable explanations disappear from Claude Code transcripts](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/77960) — amartere
A Fable 5 user isolated a Claude Code failure in four controlled runs: three paragraphs emitted after a tool result and before the next tool call vanished both from the terminal and the session JSONL, while the same text at turn start survived. The report covers Claude Code 2.1.210 and 2.1.211, cmux and stock Terminal.app, and includes the persisted block sequences plus a control that rules out text length and tool type. A separate WSL2 user reproduced the same positional rule, making the failure a workflow constraint for question-and-answer agent sessions rather than a one-machine rendering glitch.

### [An installer fix survives an escape-encoding bypass](https://github.com/SouthwestCCDC/magpie/pull/545) — duplico
A Claude Code implementation using Fable 5 stopped Magpie's installer from sourcing its own `.env` and added strict validation around values that flow into Caddy and Docker configuration. An adversarial review then found that the first ACME URL repair still accepted escape-encoded characters that `awk` decoded into a reachable admin endpoint; the follow-up closes that path and exercises hostile `.env` files, domains, proxies, bind addresses, paths, and log flags by asserting a marker command never runs. The author reports 978 unit tests passing, and the branch's visible lint, security, and test checks are successful while its end-to-end and review jobs continue.

### [A translation editor separates user mode from project mode](https://github.com/deferredreward/bible-editor-multilingual/pull/33) — deferredreward
A Fable 5 co-authored change gives a multilingual Bible editor a per-user Translate/Author mode without changing the fact that a non-translation project is always in Author mode. The branch also hardens the surrounding identity path: failed preference writes cannot roll a newly logged-in user's UI back to an old user's state, and DCS organization pagination fails closed rather than treating a truncated list as authoritative. The author ran 90 API and 14 web tests, applied the migration to a local D1 database, and the PR's public check is successful.

### [Shared reports run under the viewer’s own database credentials](https://github.com/bagofwords1/bagofwords/pull/683) — yochze
A Fable 5 co-authored Bag of Words branch changes shared report runs from a creator-owned snapshot into an explicit choice: authenticated viewers can run with their own credentials, while creator-mode runs are refused outside the creator's organization. Its committed feedback loop uses real Postgres row-level security to show three different results for the same dashboard—Alice's owner snapshot, Bob's viewer run, and a creator-mode run returning Alice's rows—without changing the owner snapshot. The author also committed full-stack Playwright drivers and screenshots; public LLM, Playwright, and data-source integration checks are successful, while the SQLite and Postgres end-to-end jobs are still running.

### [A 175-call agent workflow exposes recovery failures](https://github.com/ThousandBirdsInc/chidori/pull/138) — kvey
A Fable 5 co-authored consumer test built a multi-agent newsroom with live Hacker News tools, a critic, branch fan-out, a human approval gate, and a detached service, then killed processes and injected flaky upstream failures. It found that trusted tool-using runs could not resume from the CLI, restart cached a 500 instead of retrying, actor death could starve a parent for 480 seconds, and detached work could remain stuck as running; the same harness also replayed a 175-call actor-and-branch tree byte-for-byte in 79 ms on an invalid key. A follow-up commit repairs the findings, but the open PR's Rust workspace job was failing when inspected, even though its Python and TypeScript SDK, cargo-deny, and npm-audit checks had succeeded.

### [An OpenClaw memory importer scans for tampering and prompt injection](https://github.com/CueCrux/Crux/pull/421) — CueCrux-Myles
CueCrux-Myles credits Fable 5 with a local OpenClaw-memory importer that records each Markdown file with a BLAKE3 digest, source path, mtime, declared date, and provenance receipt, then makes re-imports idempotent. Its companion scan rereads the workspace, checks each live file against the stored hash, flags forged or missing provenance and suspicious instructions in data logs, and escapes untrusted text before terminal or Markdown reporting. A synthetic workspace includes a post-import edit and an injected instruction; the author reports 13 new adversarial tests and 1,010 green corecruxctl tests, while public test, lint, coverage, audit, and compatibility checks succeeded.

### [Two live failures in AutoBot turn into narrowly tested repairs](https://github.com/mrveiss/AutoBot-AI/pull/11819) — mrveiss
A Fable 5 inline implementation fixed AutoBot deployments whose backend rendered `CORS_ORIGINS` but never read it: a live TLS deployment had logged 288 rejected WebSocket handshakes in an hour, taking down its event and voice streams. Five tests cover environment precedence and the exact HTTPS-origin case; reverting the three-line read made three fail, and the merged PR's public security, static-analysis, smoke, and CodeQL checks succeeded. In a companion [root-path repair](https://github.com/mrveiss/AutoBot-AI/pull/11824), Fable changed `/` and `//` from a false “outside sandbox” error into the sandbox root while preserving traversal rejection, with eight focused tests and the same successful visible security gates.

### [A Sanskrit cross-check becomes data and a reproducible census](https://github.com/gasyoun/SanskritGrammar/pull/349) — gasyoun
A Fable 5 run cross-checked 53 Sanskrit aorist records from four registries against the Talmud 2.1.6 reference: 31 were confirmed, four contradicted the books while reinforcing earlier verdicts, and 18 had no answer. It also adds a Python census over 745 roots, including a new thematic class with 116 members, and moves 18 annotations out of generated Markdown into YAML so the next regeneration will not erase them. The PR merged with its Python unit tests green; its Docusaurus smoke build was still running when inspected.

### [A USB library bounds an enumeration hang without freeing live I/O](https://github.com/libusb/libusb/pull/1882) — sonatique
A libusb contributor gave Claude Code with Fable 5 their analysis of a Windows failure where a nonresponsive Nacon PS4 Compact Controller could leave hub-descriptor I/O hung forever while enumeration held the device-list lock. The resulting patch issues the vulnerable IOCTLs asynchronously, gives them a five-second deadline, and keeps timed-out heap-owned requests on an orphan list until the kernel actually finishes touching their buffers — avoiding a use-after-free in the repair itself. The author reviewed the code before posting, and the open PR's Windows, Android, build, and fuzzing checks were successful when inspected.

### [A trace store gets a reproducible load harness](https://github.com/otterscope/otterscope/pull/94) — DimitrovK
A Fable 5 co-authored Otterscope PR turns its storage performance claims into Go benchmarks for ingest, run listing, aggregate statistics, and search, then records the command and seeded-scale results in the repository. On the author's Ryzen 7 9700X, it reports roughly 3,000 ingest spans per second, indexed listing at about 0.17 ms from 1,000 through 10,000 runs, and 0.9 ms for aggregate statistics. The same experiment identifies full-text search as the weak path — around 41 ms when a term appears in nearly every run — and files it as follow-up work; the merged PR's Go check succeeded.

### [Three query-path optimizations come with the disappointing benchmark too](https://github.com/sagarm85/unidb/pull/142) — sagarm85
A Fable 5 architectural analysis of the Rust database unidb led to three changes in filtered `SELECT`: disabling diagnostic atomics by default, binding predicate columns to indexes before scanning, and rejecting fixed-width integer rows directly from page bytes before materializing values. The author reports 415 release tests, 46 crash-harness tests, Clippy, formatting, and a 32/32 concurrency matrix passing, alongside a 12 MiB peak-RSS reduction. The committed benchmark is unusually candid: its 5%-selectivity B-tree path reaches 2.04 million rows per second but only 0.39 times PostgreSQL, and misses the project's 0.50x target; the report explains why that cannot be compared directly with the earlier full-scan baseline.

### [A release-integrity audit repairs four fail-open controls and leaves seven blockers open](https://github.com/Raoof128/divan-open-day/pull/17) — Raoof128
A Fable 5 audit of the static DIVAN Open Day site inventories 392 backend-equivalent files, checks 2,503 available skills for relevance, and records both accepted and rejected findings against pinned tool versions. It repairs four concrete fail-open paths — cache transformations of content-addressed assets, a gitignore gap for private poetry evidence, a CI dependency-scan bypass, and a case-insensitive health endpoint — with red-first regressions and 721 passing Vitest cases reported. More importantly, the branch refuses to call the result a pass: it escalates seven unresolved concerns, including incomplete Persian source text, unsupported rights records, a vacuous raster check, and a missing asset-to-MIME drift test.

### [A transcript audit finds Fable silently inheriting into seventy-two subagents](https://github.com/doctor-school/ds-platform/pull/1092) — sidorovanthon
A Doctor School platform maintainer audited 341 subagent dispatches from five days of Claude Code work and found that 72 ran on Fable 5, all because Fable-led parent sessions implicitly passed their model choice down to judgment, implementation, review, and specification workers. The project had intended those roles to run on Opus, so the merged two-line instruction change requires `model: opus` explicitly for every judgment subagent and forbids inherited Fable subagents while retaining Sonnet for mechanical fan-out. The report is a useful operational warning about model routing rather than a claim of a Fable defect; public CI, lint, build, unit, accessibility, and static preflight checks were successful.

### [A token tracker finds a 59% cost-reporting gap](https://github.com/binhnt20/Claude-Token-Tracker/pull/5) — tuntran
Tun Tran ran a revised cost calculation over an all-time Claude Code transcript set of about 89,000 assistant responses and found the dashboard had reported roughly $7,090 where API-equivalent list prices came to about $11,100. An unrecognized `claude-fable-5` entry had silently been charged at Sonnet rates, under-billing it by 3.3×, while 82.9% of cache writes in the sample used the more expensive one-hour tier but were charged as five-minute writes. The proposed repair centralizes rates, leaves unfamiliar models unpriced instead of guessing, and adds 29 `unittest` cases; the author notes those tests are not yet wired into CI.

### [A pane runner says it started Fable but leaves the prompt unsent](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/pi-extensions/issues/18) — RenMunetsuna
Testing a freshly split Claude Code pane running Fable 5 at high effort, RenMunetsuna found that `pi-herdr` returned “Started” after its `run` action but left the reviewer prompt sitting in the composer. A 30-second watch saw no model response, while sending a separate Enter immediately began the file-reading task; the issue includes a six-step reproduction using `claude --model fable --effort high --permission-mode auto`. For an orchestrator, that difference matters: a waiting caller can label a healthy reviewer stalled, while retrying can duplicate the unsent composer text.

### [CSS selector costs join browser performance traces](https://github.com/sitespeedio/browsertime/pull/2517) — soulgalore
A Fable co-authored Browsertime change adds Chrome’s `SelectorStats` trace category only to the tool’s non-measured profile run, then aggregates which CSS selectors consume matching time and which make many attempts without ever matching. The parser retains total style cost but excludes Chrome’s own user-agent rules from the actionable lists, emitting separate rankings capped at 25 selectors by elapsed time and zero-match attempts. Unit fixtures cover aggregation across recalculations, user-agent filtering, and the no-trace case; the merged branch’s visible build, basic, video, and browser-version checks were successful when inspected, with scripting still running.

### [Tone curves stop forcing color through luminance](https://github.com/furious-banana/film_proccessing/pull/36) — furious-banana
A Fable co-authored, merged film-processing repair replaces a luminance-derived gain that made shadow lifts neon and could shift saturated colors with an Adobe DNG SDK-style RGBTone curve. The shader and Python export now evaluate the scalar curve at each pixel’s minimum and maximum channels and interpolate the middle channel, preserving hue while allowing saturation to relax near endpoints. The author rewrote the hue test for that contract, added a saturation-relaxation test, and reports 20 tests in total across the shared mobile and desktop implementation.

### [A Kafka dispatcher stops spraying tiny batches across twenty workers](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/71890) — José Sequeira / PostHog
PostHog engineer José Sequeira directed Fable 5 in Claude Code after sampling roughly 30,000 live-development sub-batches: 60-message Kafka batches were spreading across 17–18 of 20 workers, leaving an average sub-batch of 3.5 messages. The implementation adds a configurable candidate cap that keeps a 60-message batch on one or two workers while allowing a 1,000-message batch to spread, plus gzip transport with raw-versus-gzip counters and a kill switch. The author reports 185 ingestion-consumer tests, fmt, Clippy, and Shear clean, but explicitly has not yet run it against a live cluster; the next test is a dev-chart rollout against the captured distribution.

### [Record-level authorization reaches bulk deletes and collection writes](https://github.com/HarperFast/harper/pull/1842) — kriszyp / HarperFast
HarperFast's KrAIs agent, using Fable 5, implemented record-scoped authorization for conditional deletes and array puts after those operations were found to authorize only once per request. Denied rows are now filtered from bulk deletes, while a denied element aborts a collection write; asynchronous overrides are awaited and defaults retain their existing single entry check. The open branch adds 149 integration-test lines and 477 unit-test lines, documents two accepted limits around app-managed transactions and commit retries, and had a successful Socket Security check when inspected.

### [A native agent TUI gains structured sessions, then trips three CodeQL alerts](https://github.com/agent-of-empires/agent-of-empires/pull/2923) — njbrake / Agent of Empires
Agent of Empires says Fable 5 implemented this six-commit native structured-view expansion end to end under human direction: paste and mouse input, context-cost display, permission-mode switching, native single-choice answers, structured diffs, Markdown tables, and creation/switching between terminal and structured sessions. The author reports 67 new or updated unit tests, 22 local tmux-and-Node end-to-end tests, and 5,285 passing library tests, while identifying three unrelated base failures. It is not a clean finish: the open PR's current CodeQL run reports three new critical alerts in changed code, and several broader test jobs were still running when inspected.

### [A timer-emulation fix restores Virtua Fighter’s missing hair physics](https://github.com/mamedev/mame/pull/15715) — frangarcj
MAME contributor frangarcj used Claude Fable 5 in an investigation of a regression where a stopped Model 1 timer returned `0x8d00` instead of its latched zero value, making Virtua Fighter’s ponytails, braid, and scarf vanish in attract-mode scenes. A commit and hunk-level bisect, instrumentation covering more than 400,000 timer reads in 200 emulated seconds, and deterministic snapshot A/Bs narrowed the change to that one read path; the three affected frames now match a pre-regression build pixel-for-pixel. The open one-file repair also has successful Windows GCC/Clang, macOS, and Linux GCC/Clang builds.

### [A 54-paper probe moves an axion-extraction pipeline to Fable](https://github.com/FaroutYLq/AutoAxionLimits/pull/736) — FaroutYLq
AutoAxionLimits ran a 54-paper, single-run Fable 5 versus Opus 4.8 extraction probe with a pre-registered three-part rule, then merged a switch making Fable the production extractor for daily, weekly, and backfill workflows. The reported result recovered 6 of 14 catastrophic-tail papers outright, fixed 5 of 20 wrong-type cases, and left the 20-paper control median flat; the author also records one genuine regression in which Fable read a plotted `f_a` axis and shifted the mass window. The merged five-file change leaves the reviewer on Opus, uses an existing fail-fast guard if Actions lacks Fable access, and has two successful public test runs while its gate job was skipped.

### [A sixteen-session fleet stops cross-process Claude session clobbering](https://github.com/agent-of-empires/agent-of-empires/pull/2921) — simon-spektrum
A Claude Code/Fable 5 investigation of a fleet with roughly 16 sessions sharing one repository found two processes could each accept the same Claude conversation UUID from stale in-memory views, creating duplicate assignments within minutes. The repair makes ownership checks under the on-disk `flock`, defers tmux publication until persistence succeeds, and normalizes worktree paths that had reopened a stopped-peer exclusion bug. The author says Fable traced and implemented the recurrence from field logs, while targeted tests were each shown to fail when their guard was neutralized; `cargo test --lib` reports 4,008 passing tests, though the open PR’s visible jobs currently cover template and title checks rather than the full Rust suite.

### [Atomic JSONL publication survives injected partial writes](https://github.com/Goldislops/UtilityFog-Fractal-TreeOpen/pull/375) — Goldislops
A Claude Code session running Fable 5 repaired a JSONL observer whose direct append could leave its canonical log with a malformed, unterminated tail after a partial write. The replacement serializes first, stages a complete copy in the log directory, fsyncs it, and atomically replaces the original; its stated guarantee is deliberately limited to a single writer and in-process failures, not power loss. Deterministic fault injection covers partial writes, hostile serialization, `fsync`, and replacement failure, while the PR’s visible Python, CodeQL, safety, and frontend checks passed.

### [A three-crate GPUI application layer ships after five agent review cycles](https://github.com/BumpyClock/gpui-component/pull/8) — BumpyClock
The author says Fable 5 orchestrated Opus and Codex agents for this 64-file GPUI platform layer, then ran five structured review cycles that fixed 24 findings. The merged change adds storage with atomic and locked writes, an app-manifest crate with a packaging doctor, and an AppShell layer with lifecycle, window, menu, settings, and capability APIs, plus conformance examples. Windows, Linux, and macOS test and native-launch-smoke jobs all succeeded.

### [A Fable worker follows a stale Sonnet attribution instruction](https://github.com/sparq-org/sparq/issues/2504) — jeswr / SPARQ
A SPARQ maintainer found that a worker verifiably routed to `claude-fable-5` was adding `[SONNET-4.6]` markers and a Sonnet co-author trailer to its code. The cause was not model routing: an injected agent brief hard-coded “you are Sonnet,” and the Fable worker followed it despite the registry’s Fable tag. The proposed repair removes fixed model names from agent briefs and treats the harness-selected model as the source of provenance, a useful warning that generated commit trailers can be wrong even when execution metadata is right.
